KCHC . Anticoagulation Recommendation
Indication
VTE Treatment (DVT/PE)
Reasoning
Source: KCHC Anticoagulation Policy - Revised May 2025
Enoxaparin 1 mg/kg Q12h SQ (preferred) or UFH IV
Based on actual body weight (ABW). Round to nearest 5 mg. CrCl below 30: reduce to 1 mg/kg QD.
Monitoring
Anti-Xa levels if obese, pregnant, CrCl below 30, or weight extremes. CBC and SCr.
Warfarin
Vitamin K Antagonist · KCHC Protocol
IndicationINR Target
Antiphospholipid Antibody Syndrome2–3
Atrial Fibrillation (non-valvular)2–3
DVT / PE Treatment2–3
Mechanical valve — Mitral position2.5–3.5
Mechanical valve — Aortic position (standard)2–3
Mechanical valve — Aortic position (high risk*)2.5–3.5
Bioprosthetic valve — Mitral position2.5–3.5
Ordering Policy
KCHC Anticoagulation Policy · Updated: May 2025
🧮 CrCl Quick Calc (Cockcroft-Gault) ▼
CrCl = [(140-Age) × Weight / (72 × SCr)] × 0.85 if female · Source: Cockcroft-Gault
Primary Source: Justin Wilson, PharmD Presentation
  • Ordered as a one-time order, repeated daily
  • New starts: baseline INR within 24 hours must be in EPIC
  • All subsequent orders: INR within 24 hours prior to order
  • Standard initiation: 5 mg daily
  • Sensitive patients (age >75, ESRD, CHF, hepatic disease, malnutrition, malignancy): consider 1–2.5 mg daily
INR Monitoring — What the KCHC Protocol Says
New Start — Initiation Phase
  • Day 1: No INR required — give selected starting dose
  • Day 2 onward: INR must be drawn and resulted in EPIC within 24 hours before every warfarin order — this is KCHC policy, not optional
  • Warfarin is a one-time daily order — it must be re-ordered each day, and the 24h INR rule applies to every re-order
  • Continue daily INR until 2 consecutive therapeutic INR values are achieved
Maintenance Phase
  • KCHC policy: INR required within 24 hours prior to each warfarin order — applies throughout the hospital stay
  • Stable outpatient monitoring intervals (weekly → biweekly → monthly) are not specified in this presentation
  • For stable patient intervals: refer to Shareplus → Pharmacy → Staff → Clinical → Anticoagulants
Argatroban → Warfarin Transition
  • Check INR 4 hours after stopping argatroban infusion
  • INR 2–3: continue warfarin monotherapy
  • INR <2: restart argatroban · repeat INR check following day
  • See HIT / Argatroban tab for full transition protocol
◆ Full monitoring protocol: Shareplus → Pharmacy → Documents → Staff → Clinical → Anticoagulants → Attachment B
Maintenance Dose Adjustment Guide
Goal INR 2–3ActionGoal INR 2.5–3.5
<2Reload ×0–1 · Increase weekly dose 5–15%<2.5
2–3No change — therapeutic2.5–3.5
3.1–3.5Decrease weekly dose 0–15%3.6–4
3.6–4Hold 0–1 dose · Decrease 5–15%4–4.5
>4Hold until therapeutic · ±mini-dose Vit K · Decrease 10–20%>4.5
Management of Elevated INR
Above therapeutic but <4.5 — No bleeding
  • Reduce or hold · Monitor INR · Resume when therapeutic · Dose reduction may not be needed if only slightly above range
Above therapeutic but <4.5 — With bleeding
  • Hold warfarin · Consider Vitamin K 2.5 mg PO
INR 4.5–10 — No bleeding
  • Hold 1–2 doses or until INR therapeutic · Vitamin K NOT routinely recommended · If urgent surgery: Vit K 2.5 mg × 1 PO, additional 2.5 mg in 24h if needed · Monitor INR daily
INR 4.5–10 — With bleeding
  • Hold warfarin · Vitamin K 2.5 mg PO or 1 mg IV
INR >10 — No bleeding
  • Hold · Vitamin K 2.5–5 mg PO (IV formulation may be given PO mixed with OJ)
INR >10 — With bleeding
  • Hold · Vitamin K 1–2 mg IV over 30 min · Repeat q6–24h
Major bleeding — any INR
  • Hold · Vitamin K 10 mg in 50 mL NS over 30 min · Consider 4-PCC (Kcentra) — Attachment A on Shareplus
Perioperative / Surgery
  • Hold warfarin 4–5 days until INR <1.5 before procedure · Minor procedures: INR 1.5–1.8 may be acceptable
  • Restart 12–24 hours after surgery if hemostasis adequate and tolerating PO
  • High VTE risk: consider bridging with parenteral anticoagulant
Sensitive patients — start at 1–2.5 mg if: age >75 · ESRD · CHF · hepatic disease · malnutrition · malignancy. Standard start = 5 mg.

2–3No change2.5–3.5

Unfractionated Heparin (UFH)
IV Infusion Protocol · KCHC
⚠CBC with platelet count must be checked DAILY while patient is on heparin. Monitor for HIT.
VTE Prophylaxis Dosing
KCHC Anticoagulation Policy · Updated: May 2025
Primary Source: Justin Wilson, PharmD Presentation
  • Standard: 5,000 units SQ every 8h or 12h
  • Obese (BMI >40): Consider 7,500 units SQ every 8h (general clinical practice — not explicitly in KCHC Policy May 2025)
Concurrent Medications
  • ⚠ No concurrent aspirin >162 mg/day for patients on continuous heparin — unless cardiac indication (any dose of scheduled aspirin OK for cardiac patients)
  • ⚠ Discontinue COX-2 inhibitors and NSAIDs (except aspirin) due to increased bleeding risk
Monitoring
  • CBC with platelets daily while on heparin · Platelets <100,000/μL → call prescriber
  • ALOC patients on SQ heparin prophylaxis: minimum monthly CBC

aPTT Nomogram (Alternative)
aPTT RangeBolus IVPRate Change
<40 sec50 units/kg (max 5,000)+ 3 units/kg/hr
40–49 sec50 units/kg (max 5,000)+ 2 units/kg/hr
50–59 sec25 units/kg (max 2,500)+ 1 units/kg/hr
GOAL: 60–90 sec—No change
91–105 secNone- 2 units/kg/hr
106–120 secNone- 3 units/kg/hr
>120 secNoneStop 1h then - 4 units/kg/hr
UFH Reversal — Protamine Sulfate Calculator
Calculation
  • Ratio: 1 mg protamine : 100 units UFH · Max single dose: 50 mg
  • Only the dose given over the last 3 hours counts (UFH half-life ~60 min IV)
  • Neutralizes in ~5 min · Activity lasts ~2 hours · Unbound protamine → paradoxical bleeding
Enoxaparin (Lovenox)

Anti-Xa Monitoring
BID therapeutic: draw 4h after 3rd dose · Goal 0.6–1.0 units/mL · QD therapeutic: draw 4h post dose · Goal 1.0–2.0 units/mL · Trough (before next dose): <0.5 units/mL · Pediatric prophylaxis goal: 0.1–0.3 units/mL
When to Obtain Anti-Xa Level — Indications
KCHC Anticoagulation Policy · May 2025
Justin Wilson, PharmD Presentation · March 2026

Anti-Xa monitoring is recommended in the following patient populations:

  • BMI ≥40 — therapeutic or prophylactic dosing
  • Weight >150 kg — standard weight-based dosing may be unreliable
  • Pregnancy — pharmacokinetics altered; Anti-Xa required for all therapeutic doses
  • CrCl <30 mL/min — accumulation risk; monitor closely
  • Underweight — Female <45 kg or Male <57 kg
Draw Timing
  • BID regimens: draw 4 hours after the 3rd dose (steady state)
  • QD regimens: draw 4 hours after the dose
  • Trough (optional): draw before next dose — goal <0.5 units/mL
Target Ranges
  • BID therapeutic: 0.6–1.0 units/mL
  • QD therapeutic: 1.0–2.0 units/mL (KCHC Policy May 2025)
  • Prophylactic (adult): 0.1–0.3 units/mL
  • Pediatric prophylactic: 0.1–0.3 units/mL
Anti-Xa (units/mL)RegimenHold?Dose ChangeNext Level
<0.35BIDNo↑ 25%4h after next dose
0.35–0.59BIDNo↑ 10%4h after next dose
0.6–1.0 — Therapeutic BIDBIDNoNo changeNext day, then within 1 week
1.0–2.0 — Therapeutic QDQDNoNo changeNext day, then within 1 week
1.1–1.5 (BID)BIDNo↓ 20%Before next dose
1.6–2.0 (BID)BID3 hours↓ 30%Before next + 4h after next
>2.0 (either)BID/QDUntil <0.5↓ 40%Before next + Q12h until <0.5

DOACs — Factor Xa Inhibitors
Apixaban (Eliquis) · Rivaroxaban (Xarelto) · KCHC
Apixaban (Eliquis)
VTE Treatment
10 mg BID × 7d → 5 mg BID
Reduce to 2.5 mg BID after 6 months
AFib
5 mg BID
2.5 mg BID if 2 of: age≥80, wt≤60kg, SCr≥1.5
Hip replacement
2.5 mg BID × 35 days
Knee replacement
2.5 mg BID × 12 days
ESRD
No adjustment (consider 2.5 mg BID)
Severe hepatic impairment
Avoid
Rivaroxaban (Xarelto)
VTE Treatment
15 mg BID × 21d → 20 mg QD with evening meal
Reduce to 10 mg QD after 6 months
AFib
20 mg QD with evening meal
CrCl 15–50: 15 mg QD · CrCl <15: Avoid
Hip replacement
10 mg QD × 35 days
Knee replacement
10 mg QD × 12 days
ESRD
AVOID for most indications
Severe hepatic impairment
Avoid
DOAC Dose Verification
Flags obesity warnings for BMI >=40
Only applies to apixaban AFib
Perioperative Management
KCHC Anticoagulation Policy · Updated: May 2025
Primary Source: Justin Wilson, PharmD Presentation
Hold Before Procedure
  • Low bleed risk: hold 24 hours before
  • Moderate–high bleed risk: hold 48 hours before
Restart After Procedure
  • After adequate hemostasis: 24–72 hours post-surgery
Reversal — Kcentra (4-PCC)
  • Bleed reversal: 2,000 units × 1 (preferred) or 25–50 units/kg (max 5,000)
  • Rate limit: ≤8.4 mL/min or ≤210 units/min · ⚠ Contains heparin — avoid if HIT
  • Pre-emergent procedure: 2,000 units × 1
HIT — Argatroban Protocol
Heparin-Induced Thrombocytopenia · KCHC Adult Protocol
⚠Stop ALL heparin immediately — infusion, SQ heparin, enoxaparin. Discontinue aspirin doses >162 mg/day.
Prior to Argatroban — Required Steps
KCHC Anticoagulation Policy · Updated: May 2025
Primary Source: Justin Wilson, PharmD Presentation
  • Baseline aPTT, PT/INR, and LFTs
  • Order HIT antibody with reflex Serotonin Release Assay — argatroban may start before results
  • Discontinue ALL heparin: IV, SQ, enoxaparin
  • Discontinue aspirin >162 mg/day
Argatroban Infusion Calculator
Argatroban Order
Initial Rate—
Maximum Rate10 mcg/kg/min
First aPTT2h after start (normal hepatic) · 4h (critically ill/impaired)
Goal aPTT45–76 seconds (1.5–3× baseline)
aPTTActionNext Level — Normal HepaticNext Level — Critically Ill
<45 sec↑ infusion rate 20%Every 2 hoursEvery 4 hours
GOAL: 45–76 secNo changeQ2h until 2× therapeutic, then every AMQ4h until 2× therapeutic, then every AM
>76 secStop 1h → restart at 50% reduced rateEvery 2 hoursEvery 4 hours
Monitoring · Contraindications · Transitions
Ongoing Monitoring
  • LFTs every 3 days minimum · PT, PTT, CBC daily
  • Call MD: unexplained BP drop, tachycardia, hematoma, Hgb ↓ >1 g/dL, bleeding signs, gross hematuria
Contraindications
  • Severe hepatic insufficiency · No anticoag within 24h of therapeutic tPA for stroke
  • No concurrent epidural/spinal/lumbar puncture · Active bleeding
→ DOAC
  • Stop argatroban · Start DOAC within 2 hours
→ Warfarin (dose ≤2 mcg/kg/min)
  • If INR >4: stop infusion · Check INR 4h later · INR 2–3: warfarin · INR <2: restart argatroban
→ Warfarin (dose >2 mcg/kg/min)
  • Reduce to 2 mcg/kg/min × 4h · Check INR · INR ≤4: continue concomitant · INR >4: stop → check 4h · INR 2–3: warfarin · INR <2: restart
Reversal Agents
KCHC Protocol Reference · Contact clinical pharmacist for life-threatening bleeds
⚠For life-threatening bleeds, refer to Attachment A on Shareplus. Contact attending and clinical pharmacist immediately.
KCHC Anticoagulation Policy · May 2025 · KCHC Antidotes & Reversals · Protamine Package Insert
!Steps 03 and 04 require aPTT still elevated before giving protamine. If ≥2 hours have elapsed and aPTT is normal — protamine is likely not needed.
Protamine Sulfate — UFH Reversal CalculatorStrictly per KCHC Antidotes & Reversals
Current infusion rate
Select the clinical scenario that applies
Enter actual hours — app caps at 3 (only recent heparin is still active)
Anaphylaxis Risk Factors
Source: Protamine Package Insert · Check all that apply — elevates anaphylaxis risk, does NOT contraindicate use
Protamine Dose — UFH
!NOT IV Push. NOT IVPB. Give as a slow IV injection, undiluted, via peripheral line only — max 50 mg over at least 10 minutes. Central line increases histamine release and drops SVR. Vasopressors and resuscitation equipment must be immediately available. Check aPTT every 5–15 minutes after.
KCHC Anticoagulation Policy · Updated: May 2025
Enoxaparin Reversal — Protamine Sulfate
  • Within 8h: 1 mg protamine : 1 mg enoxaparin
  • >8h or 2nd dose needed: 0.5 mg protamine : 1 mg enoxaparin · Max: 50 mg
  • Neutralizes ~60% of anti-Xa · Unbound protamine → paradoxical bleed
Protamine Dose Calculator — EnoxaparinKCHC Protocol
Use actual mg dose — not mg/kg
Protamine Dose — Enoxaparin
KCHC Anticoagulation Policy · Updated: May 2025
DOAC Reversal
Kcentra (4-PCC) — Factor Xa Inhibitors (Apixaban, Rivaroxaban, Edoxaban)
  • Major bleed: 2,000 units × 1 slow IV push at 200 units/min (max ≤8.4 mL/min · ≤210 units/min)
  • Or: 50 units/kg (max 5,000 units) · Monitor for thromboembolic events
  • Pre-emergent procedure: 2,000 units × 1
  • ⚠ Contains heparin — contraindicated if HIT
  • Activated charcoal 25 g if ingestion within 3 hours
  • For refractory bleeding: consult Heme/Coag
Andexanet Alfa (for apixaban and rivaroxaban)
  • Specific Factor Xa reversal · Refer to Attachment A on Shareplus for dosing protocol
Kcentra (4-PCC) Calculator — DOAC ReversalKCHC Protocol
Used for weight-based dosing option
Kcentra Dose — DOAC Reversal
KCHC Anticoagulation Policy · Updated: May 2025
Warfarin Reversal — Vitamin K + 4F-PCC (Kcentra)
Above therapeutic but <4.5 — no bleeding
  • Reduce or hold · Monitor INR · Resume when therapeutic · Dose reduction may not be needed if only slightly above range
INR 4.5–10 — no bleeding
  • Hold 1–2 doses or until INR therapeutic · Vitamin K NOT routinely recommended · If urgent surgery: Vit K 2.5 mg PO × 1 (+ 2.5 mg in 24h if needed)
INR 4.5–10 — with bleeding
  • Hold · Vit K 2.5 mg PO or 1 mg IV
INR >10 — no bleeding
  • Hold · Vit K 2.5–5 mg PO (IV formulation mixed with OJ is acceptable)
INR >10 — with bleeding
  • Hold · Vit K 1–2 mg IV over 30 min · Repeat q6–24h
Major bleeding — any INR (STAT — target within 30 min)
  • Hold warfarin
  • Vit K 10 mg in 50 mL NS infused over 30 min
  • 4F-PCC (Kcentra) — dosed by initial INR:
      • INR 2–3.9 → 25 units/kg (max 2,500 units)
      • INR 4–6 → 35 units/kg (max 3,500 units)
      • INR >6 → 50 units/kg (max 5,000 units)
  • Give 1st 1,000 units slow IV push at 200 units/min over 5 min · Then remainder based on initial INR
  • Recheck PT/INR: 30 min after infusion, then 6h and 24h later
  • If no PCC available: FFP 15 mL/kg (250 mL = 1 unit)
  • ⚠ Contraindicated if HIT · Consult Heme/Coag for refractory bleeding
Perioperative / Surgery
  • Hold warfarin 4–5 days until INR <1.5 before procedure · Minor procedures: INR 1.5–1.8 may be acceptable
  • Restart 12–24 hours after surgery if hemostasis adequate and tolerating PO
  • High VTE risk: consider bridging with parenteral anticoagulant
Kcentra (4F-PCC) Calculator — Warfarin ReversalKCHC Protocol · INR-Based Dosing
Kcentra Dose — Warfarin Reversal
Verification Checklist
I-Vent Attestation · KCHC Anticoagulation Order Review
◆Complete before verifying any anticoagulant. i-vent attestation required in EPIC — use ANTICOAGULATIONVE SmartText.
0 of 12 items completed
  • Patient diagnosis and indication for anticoagulation confirmed
  • Patient height, weight, age, and gender reviewed for dose calculation
  • Allergies checked — pork, heparin, sulfa, etc.
  • Pregnancy status obtained (if applicable)
  • CBC with platelets reviewed — baseline and current
  • SCr / CrCl assessed for renal dose adjustment
  • INR, PT, aPTT, and Anti-Factor Xa reviewed as applicable
  • LFTs reviewed — especially for DOACs, argatroban, warfarin
  • Dose verified for indication, weight, and renal/hepatic function
  • Timing verified — date and time of last anticoagulant confirmed in MAR
  • Contraindications evaluated — active bleeding, HIT history, recent tPA, surgical timing
  • i-vent ANTICOAGULATIONVE attestation completed in EPIC with pharmacist initials and last admin date/time
Trauma VTE Prophylaxis Protocol
Initiate within 24h · VTE risk rises if delayed beyond 72h · SCDs mandatory for all
Patient ProfileChemoprophylaxisMechanical
18–65 yo, No TBI/SCI, CrCl >60LMWH 40 mg q12h (BMI>40: 0.5 mg/kg)YES (SCDs)
>65 yo, TBI, SCI, Pregnancy, CrCl 30–60LMWH 30 mg q12hYES (SCDs)
Renal Failure — CrCl <30 / ESRDUFH 5,000 units q8hYES (SCDs)
Weight <50 kg (consider pharmacist consult)UFH 5,000 units q12hYES (SCDs)
Chemoprophylaxis contraindicatedNoneYES (SCDs)
Epidural catheter presentLMWH 40 mg QDYES (SCDs)
I-Vent — EPIC SmartText
KCHC Anticoagulation Policy · Updated: May 2025
  • Type "anticoag" in i-vent documentation field to trigger SmartText
  • Select ANTICOAGULATIONVE — Anticoagulation Order Verification Attestation
  • Enter date and time of last anticoagulant administration
  • Enter your pharmacist initials
Anticoagulation Competency Assessment
NYC Health + Hospitals / Kings County Hospital Center · Pharmacy Services

Competency Assessment — To be completed. 80% = PASS

Purpose

It is the purpose of NYC H+H/Kings County Pharmacy Services to:

  • Improve adherence to evidence-based guidelines for anticoagulation therapy
  • Reduce the risk of harm to patients treated with anticoagulant medications
  • Ensure all pharmacy staff are competent in anticoagulation management protocols
  • Support a culture of safety and continuous professional development

This assessment covers Warfarin, UFH, Enoxaparin, DOACs, HIT/Argatroban, and Reversal Agents. Questions are randomized on each attempt. A score of ≥80% is required to pass and receive a certificate of completion.

Question 1 of 15
Certificate of Completion
Enter your information to generate your certificate
OrderMind v2 · KCHC Anticoagulation Clinical Decision Support
Dr. Esdras Obas, PharmD · Kings County Hospital Center

  • ◆ CHEST Guidelines: Antithrombotic Therapy and Prevention of Thrombosis (10th Ed.)
  • ◆ American Society of Hematology (ASH) 2023 VTE & Anticoagulation Guidelines
  • ◆ Kcentra (4F-PCC) Prescribing Information · CSL Behring
  • ◆ Micromedex & Lexicomp Drug Interaction & Dosing Databases
App Last Updated: May 2025 · For clinical decision support only · Always verify against current KCHC protocols
PK — Trough Only
One-Compartment · Cockcroft-Gault · Matzke Ke · New Dose Simulator
KCHC Antimicrobial Stewardship · P&T Approved: Oct 2020
🧮 CrCl Quick Calc — IBW / AdjBW (Cockcroft-Gault) ▼
Age
ABW (kg)
Height (cm)
SCr (mg/dL)
Sex
CrCl = [(140-Age) × DosingWt / (72 × SCr)] × 0.85 if female · AdjBW threshold: >130% IBW (KCHC Vancomycin Guideline)
⚠
Population-based PK. For patient-specific PK use Peak+Trough tab. ABW used for vancomycin per KCHC. Use AdjBW if ABW >130% IBW.
Patient + Regimen
KCHC protocol: trough drawn 15–30 min before the 4th dose

PK — Peak + Trough
Two-Level Method · Patient-Specific Ke · Individualized Dosing
KCHC Antimicrobial Stewardship · P&T Approved: Oct 2020
⚠
Levels must be at steady state (at or after 4th dose). Use when trough timing is imperfect or patient-specific PK is required.

Vancomycin Loading Dose
IV · ABW-Based · Max 2,000 mg · KCHC Protocol
KCHC Antimicrobial Stewardship · P&T Approved: Oct 2020
⚠
All doses based on ABW. Maximum single dose 2,000 mg. Round to nearest 250 mg.
Patient Parameters
Loading Dose
ABW
—
kg
Calculated
—
mg
Order As
—
mg IV STAT
TypeLoading DoseMax
IHD25 mg/kg ABW2,000 mg
Non-HD Severe25 mg/kg ABW2,000 mg
Non-HD Mild20 mg/kg ABW2,000 mg
IHD Maintenance Dosing
Intermittent Hemodialysis · 3×/Week · Pre-Dialysis Level Adjustment
KCHC Antimicrobial Stewardship · P&T Approved: Oct 2020
⚠
Give maintenance dose AFTER each HD session. Target pre-dialysis level 15–20 mcg/mL. ABW-based, rounded to nearest 250 mg, max 2,000 mg.
Patient Parameters
Drawn before this session
IHD Maintenance
Pre-HD Level
—
mcg/mL
Base Range
—
mg (7.5–10 mg/kg)
Adjusted Dose
—
mg — give after HD
Pre-Dialysis LevelAction
<15 mcg/mLIncrease dose by 250–500 mg
15–25 mcg/mLNo change
26–29 mcg/mLDecrease dose by 250–500 mg
≥30 mcg/mLHOLD — recheck next session
Non-HD Dosing
CrCl-Based Interval · Maintenance Dose · AKI Protocol
KCHC Antimicrobial Stewardship · P&T Approved: Oct 2020
⚠
Maintenance 15–20 mg/kg ABW. Interval by CrCl. AKI or CrCl <20: one dose then random level in 12–24 hrs. Re-dose only when <20 mcg/mL.
Patient Parameters
IBW auto-calculated

CrCl
—
mL/min
Dose
—
mg
Interval
—
hours
CrClIntervalNotes
>80 mL/minQ8–12hStandard
55–79 mL/minQ12hStandard
20–54 mL/minQ12–24hMonitor closely
<20 or AKIOne dose onlyRandom level 12–24h. Re-dose when <20 mcg/mL
CRRT Dosing
Continuous Renal Replacement Therapy · Vancomycin + ICU Antibiotics
KCHC Antimicrobial Stewardship · P&T Approved: Oct 2020
⚠
Assumes standard CRRT flow rates 20–25 mL/kg/hr. Reassess if CRRT mode changes.

ICU Antibiotic CRRT Reference
Antibiotic Lookup
AntimicrobialNormal DoseIHD (Post-HD)CRRT
Vancomycin15–20 mg/kg q8–12h7.5–10 mg/kg by level7.5–10 mg/kg q12h
Cefepime2g q8h1g q24h or 2g post-HD2g q8–12h
Meropenem1–2g q8h500mg q24h1g q8h
Pip/Tazo3.375g q6h2.25g q12h2.25–3.375g q6h
Ceftriaxone1–2g q24hNo adjustmentNo adjustment
Ceftazidime2g q8h1g q24h2g q8–12h
Cefazolin2g q8h1g q24h2g load, then 1g q8h
Daptomycin4–6 mg/kg q24hUsual dose post-HD4–6 mg/kg q24h
Gentamicin5–7 mg/kg q24h2–3 mg/kg, then 1.5–2 mg/kg post-HD2–3 mg/kg load, then 1.5–2.5 mg/kg q24–48h
Amikacin15 mg/kg q24h5–7.5 mg/kg post-HD10 mg/kg load, then 7.5 mg/kg q24–48h
Levofloxacin750mg q24h750mg ×1, then 500mg q48h750mg ×1, then 500mg q24h
Fluconazole6 mg/kg q24hFull dose after HD only400–1200mg q24h
Acyclovir (CNS)10 mg/kg q8h5 mg/kg q24h10 mg/kg q12–24h
TMP/SMX5 mg/kg q8h5 mg/kg q24hNo adjustment
Antibiotic Lock Therapy
Dialysis Catheter CRBSI · Vancomycin + Cefazolin · KCHC Protocol
KCHC Antimicrobial Stewardship · P&T Approved: Aug 2020
⚠
ALT used WITH systemic antibiotics. Instill at end of dialysis. Dwell up to 72 hrs. WITHDRAW completely and flush before next use. Duration 7–14 days; 4 weeks if S. aureus catheter retained.
Lock Solution Parameters
ALT Preparation
Concentration
—
mg/mL
Vol per Port
—
mL
Total Drug
—
mg
Step-by-Step Preparation
AgentWith HeparinWithout HeparinDwell
Vancomycin2.5 mg/mL + Hep 2,500 u/mL5 mg/mLUp to 72 hrs
Cefazolin5 mg/mL + Heparin5 mg/mLUp to 72 hrs
MUST withdraw lock solution and flush line before each use
C. difficile Treatment
Oral Vancomycin Protocol · Treatment + Recurrence · KCHC
KCHC Antimicrobial Stewardship · P&T Approved: Aug 2020
⚠
Oral vancomycin is NOT systemically absorbed. GI-only action. No PK monitoring, no trough targets, no renal adjustment. Entirely separate from IV vancomycin management.
EpisodeRegimenDuration
Initial — Non-severe or SevereVancomycin 125 mg PO q6h10 days
Initial — Fulminant CDIVancomycin 500 mg PO q6h + Metronidazole 500 mg IV q8h
If ileus: add rectal vancomycin 500mg in 500 mL NS		10–14 days
1st Recurrence (metro used initially)Vancomycin 125 mg PO q6h10 days
1st Recurrence (vanco used initially)Tapered + Pulsed Regimen ↓~10–12 weeks
2nd+ RecurrenceID Consultation RequiredPer ID
Tapered + Pulsed Regimen
PhaseDoseFrequencyDuration
1125 mg POq6h (4×/day)10–14 days
2125 mg POBID1 week
3125 mg PODaily1 week
4125 mg POEvery 2–3 days (pulse)2–8 weeks
Critical Reminder — Oral vs IV Vancomycin
Oral vancomycin = GI-only. Not absorbed. No serum levels. No IV monitoring applies.
IV vancomycin = systemic. PK monitoring, trough targets, renal adjustments all apply.
C. diff Prophylaxis
Oral Vancomycin · ICU High-Risk Patients · KCHC Protocol
KCHC Antimicrobial Stewardship · Approval Date Pending
⚠
Clinically supported per IDSA/SHEA 2017. P&T approval date pending. Use per clinical judgment and ID/ASP guidance. Authors: Rampersad, Li, Qu Zhong, Luka (PharmD) · Quale, MD.
Primary Prophylaxis — ALL 3 Criteria Required · ICU Only (MICU, NSICU, SICU)
①On systemic beta-lactam, clindamycin, or fluoroquinolone
②Age ≥ 60 years
③Prior hospitalization within 30 days + antibiotics during that stay
No prior hospitalization? Current stay ≥ 7 days qualifies
Secondary ProphylaxisCase-by-Case (ID/ASP)
Prior C. diff within past 3 months
Immunosuppression		<60 yrs or immunocompromised in ICU on antibiotics >7 days — ID/ASP review required
Exceptions — Do NOT Give Prophylaxis
Suspected active C. diff infectionPeri/postop antibiotics ≤ 24 hours
Currently on metronidazole for active infectionAllergy/contraindication to oral vancomycin
NPO — start as soon as oral access obtained. Limited data for IV metro or rectal vanco while NPO.
MedicationDoseDuration
Vancomycin oral125 mg once daily PO
125 mg BID if severely immunocompromised		Until 5 days post-completion of systemic antibiotics
Adult Sepsis — Empiric
Initial Selection · Normal Renal Function (CrCl >50 mL/min)
KCHC Antimicrobial Stewardship · P&T Approved: Oct 2020
⚠
Empiric therapy only. De-escalate based on culture results. Adjust for renal function using the Renal Dosing section.
Source / SettingPrimary Agent(s)Add MRSA Coverage IfAdd AG If
Unknown Source — CommunityCeftriaxone 1–2g IV q24h+ Vancomycin 20–25 mg/kg IV+ Gentamicin 7 mg/kg IV
Unknown Source — Hospital/HAIPip/Tazo 4.5g IV q6h OR Cefepime 2g IV q8h+ Vancomycin 20–25 mg/kg IV OR Linezolid 600mg IV+ Amikacin 15 mg/kg IV
Intra-Abdominal — CommunityCeftriaxone 1g IV + Metronidazole 500mg IV OR Cefoxitin 2g IV q6hAnaerobic coverage included. Add MRSA only if skin source.
Urosepsis — CommunityCeftriaxone 1g IV +/- Gentamicin 5 mg/kgCulture-directed de-escalation ASAP.
CAP — CommunityCeftriaxone 1g IV + Azithromycin 500mg IV+ Vancomycin if MRSA riskPip/Tazo + Levofloxacin if Pseudomonas risk
Meningitis — CommunityCeftriaxone 2g IV + Vancomycin 20–25 mg/kg IV +/- Ampicillin 2g IVDexamethasone 10mg IV before or with first dose if pneumococcal meningitis suspected.
Necrotizing FasciitisPip/Tazo 4.5g IV + Vancomycin 20–25 mg/kg IV + Clindamycin 900mg IVEmergent surgical debridement required.
Pip/Tazo + Vancomycin Incompatibility
Piperacillin/tazobactam and Vancomycin are incompatible via Y-site. Administer through separate IV lines or flush line completely between infusions. Same incompatibility applies to Cefepime + Vancomycin.
🧮 Vancomycin TDM InterpreterEnter patient data + levels → therapeutic assessment + dose suggestion
Community-Acquired Pneumonia
Adult · Empiric · Normal Renal Function
KCHC Antimicrobial Stewardship · P&T Approved: Aug 2020
SettingRegimenDuration
Outpatient — HealthyAzithromycin PO OR Doxycycline PO5 days
Outpatient — ComorbiditiesLevofloxacin PO OR Amox + Azithromycin PO5–7 days
Inpatient — Non-ICUCeftriaxone 1g IV q24h + Azithromycin IV/PO OR Levofloxacin IV/PO5–7 days
ICU / Severe CAPCeftriaxone 1–2g IV + Azithromycin IV + Vancomycin if MRSA risk7 days
ICU — Pseudomonas RiskPip/Tazo IV + Levofloxacin IV OR Cefepime IV + Levofloxacin IV7 days
IV to PO Conversion Criteria
Switch when: afebrile <100°F for 24h · HR <100 · RR <24 · SBP >90 · SpO2 >90% on RA · tolerating oral intake · normal mental status. Exclude: Endocarditis · Meningitis · Brain Abscess · Febrile Neutropenia · Osteomyelitis · Bacteremia not cleared.
Adult UTI & Pyelonephritis
Empiric · Outpatient + Inpatient
KCHC Antimicrobial Stewardship · P&T Approved: Aug 2020
⚠
Culture before antibiotics. Modify empiric therapy based on culture results once available.
ConditionAgentDuration
Uncomplicated CystitisNitrofurantoin 100mg BID (CrCl >60) OR Cefpodoxime 100mg BID OR Cephalexin 500mg BID OR TMP/SMX 160/800mg BID5 / 5 / 5 / 3 days
Complicated / PregnancySame agents as uncomplicated7–14 days
Pyelonephritis — OutpatientLevofloxacin 750mg QD OR Ceftriaxone 1g IV ×1 → oral step-down7 days
Pyelonephritis — Inpatient (Community)Ceftriaxone 1g IV q24h OR Gentamicin 5 mg/kg IV q24h7–14 days
Pyelonephritis — Inpatient (Hospital-acquired)Cefepime 1g IV q12h OR Pip/Tazo 3.375g IV q6h10–14 days
UrosepsisSame as inpatient + consider Gentamicin or AmikacinPer clinical response
Skin & Soft Tissue Infections
Adult · Empiric · Non-Purulent + Purulent + Diabetic Foot
KCHC Antimicrobial Stewardship · P&T Approved: Aug 2020
TypeSeverityAgent
Non-purulent (Cellulitis)Mild (Outpatient)Cephalexin PO · PCN allergy: Clindamycin PO
Non-purulentModerate (Inpatient)Cefazolin IV or Oxacillin IV · PCN allergy: Vancomycin IV
Non-purulentSeverePip/Tazo IV + Vancomycin IV OR Linezolid IV/PO
Necrotizing FasciitisSevere — emergent surgeryClindamycin IV + Pip/Tazo IV + Vancomycin IV
Purulent (MRSA)Moderate (Outpatient)TMP/SMX PO or Doxycycline PO
Purulent (MRSA)Severe (Inpatient)Vancomycin IV
Diabetic Foot — MildOutpatientAmox/Clav PO or Cephalexin PO
Diabetic Foot — ModerateInpatientAmp/Sulbactam IV OR Ceftriaxone IV + Metronidazole IV
Diabetic Foot — SevereInpatientPip/Tazo IV + Vancomycin IV OR Ceftriaxone IV + Metro IV + Vancomycin IV
MRSA Site-Specific Treatment
Adult · Treatment (Not Empiric) · Confirmed MRSA
KCHC Antimicrobial Stewardship · P&T Approved: Oct 2020
Site / DiagnosisAgentDurationNotes
Bacteremia — UncomplicatedVancomycin IV OR Daptomycin 6–10 mg/kg IVMin 2 weeksEcho recommended for all MRSA bacteremia
Bacteremia — Complicated / EndocarditisVancomycin IV OR Daptomycin 8–10 mg/kg IV4–6 weeksProsthetic valve: + Rifampin + Gentamicin
OsteomyelitisVancomycin IV OR Daptomycin IV OR Linezolid PO/IV · PO: Bactrim + RifampinMin 8 weeksSurgical debridement often required
Pneumonia (MRSA)Vancomycin IV OR Linezolid 600mg IV/PO7–21 daysLinezolid preferred for lung penetration
Meningitis / CNSVancomycin IV · Alt: Linezolid, Bactrim IV2–6 weeksDuration depends on site
Clostridioides difficile (CDI)
Complete Oral Vancomycin Protocol · Treatment + Recurrence + Prophylaxis
KCHC Antimicrobial Stewardship · P&T Approved: Aug 2020
⚠
Oral vancomycin is NOT systemically absorbed. Acts locally in GI tract only. No PK monitoring required. Entirely separate from IV vancomycin management.
EpisodeRegimenDurationNotes
Initial — Non-severe or SevereVancomycin 125 mg PO q6h10 daysFirst-line.
Initial — Fulminant CDIVancomycin 500 mg PO q6h + Metronidazole 500 mg IV q8h10–14 daysIf ileus: add rectal vancomycin 500mg in 500 mL NS.
First Recurrence (metro used initially)Vancomycin 125 mg PO q6h10 daysStandard course.
First Recurrence (vanco used initially)Tapered + Pulsed Regimen (see below)~10–12 weeksDo NOT repeat standard course.
Second or Subsequent RecurrenceID Consultation RequiredPer IDConsider fidaxomicin, FMT.
* High Risk Definition (KCHC Anticoagulation Policy, May 2025)
High Risk = presence of: Atrial fibrillation, prior embolus, LA or LV clot, severe LV dysfunction (EF below 30%), hypercoagulable state, or coronary artery disease.
Tapered + Pulsed Regimen
PhaseDoseFrequencyDuration
Phase 1125 mg POq6h (4×/day)10–14 days
Phase 2125 mg POBID1 week
Phase 3125 mg PODaily1 week
Phase 4125 mg POEvery 2–3 days (pulse)2–8 weeks
Critical Reminder — Oral vs IV Vancomycin
Oral vancomycin = GI-only action. Not absorbed. No serum levels. No IV monitoring applies.
IV vancomycin = systemic action. PK monitoring, trough targets, renal adjustments all apply.
C. diff Prophylaxis — ICU High-Risk Patients
KCHC Antimicrobial Stewardship · Approval Date Pending
⚠
This protocol is guideline-based and clinically supported but P&T approval date is pending. Use per clinical judgment and ID/ASP guidance. Authors: Rampersad, Li, Qu Zhong, Luka (PharmD) · Quale, MD.
Primary Prophylaxis — ALL Criteria Must Be Met
ICU Only (MICU, NSICU, SICU)
①
Currently on systemic beta-lactam, clindamycin, or fluoroquinolone antibiotics
②
Age ≥ 60 years
③
Prior hospitalization within 30 days AND antibiotics received during that stay
If no prior hospitalization: current hospital stay ≥ 7 days qualifies instead
Secondary Prophylaxis
✓
Prior episode of C. difficile within past 3 months
✓
Immunosuppression
Case-by-Case (ID/ASP Review)
Patients <60 years or immunocompromised in ICU on prolonged antibiotics (>7 days) — reviewed and recommended by ID/ASP individually.
Exceptions — Do NOT Give Prophylaxis If:
✗
Suspected active C. difficile infection
✗
Peri/postoperative prophylactic antibiotics for ≤ 24 hours
✗
Currently receiving metronidazole for treatment of ongoing infection
✗
Documented allergy or contraindication to oral vancomycin
✗
Strict NPO precautions — start oral vancomycin as soon as oral access obtained. Limited data supports IV metronidazole or rectal vancomycin for prophylaxis while NPO.
MedicationDosingDuration
Vancomycin oral125 mg once daily PO
Consider 125 mg BID in severely immunocompromised		Until 5 days post-completion of systemic antibiotics
Pediatric CAP
Community-Acquired Pneumonia · Ages 3 Months – 18 Years
KCHC Pediatric Antimicrobial Dosing Guideline · June 2023 · P&T Approved April 2023
⚠
Pediatric dosing requires weight-based calculation. Verify max doses at each step. Exclusions: Sickle Cell, Immunodeficiency.
SettingAgentDoseMax Dose
Outpatient — No AllergyAmoxicillin PO90 mg/kg/day ÷ BID4,000 mg/day
Outpatient — Not Fully ImmunizedAmox PO or Cefdinir POAmox 90 mg/kg BID · Cefdinir 14 mg/kg BID4,000 / 600 mg/day
Outpatient — PCN AllergyClindamycin PO40 mg/kg/day ÷ TID1,800 mg/day
Outpatient — AtypicalAzithromycin PO10 mg/kg Day 1, then 5 mg/kg Days 2–5500 / 250 mg
Inpatient — No AllergyAmpicillin IV50 mg/kg/dose q6h (200 mg/kg/day)8,000 mg/day
Inpatient — Not Fully ImmunizedAmpicillin IV or Ceftriaxone IVAmp 50 mg/kg/dose q6h (200 mg/kg/day) · Ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg daily8,000 / 2,000 mg
Inpatient — PCN AllergyClindamycin IV40 mg/kg/day ÷ q8h2,700 mg/day
Pediatric SSTI
Skin & Soft Tissue · >60 Days Old
KCHC Pediatric Antimicrobial Dosing Guideline · June 2023 · P&T Approved April 2023
⚠
Exclusions: Immunocompromised · Necrotizing fasciitis · Periorbital/orbital · Perianal/perineal/groin · Bone/joint · Animal bite.
TypeRouteAgentDose
Non-purulent — Mild (Outpatient)POCephalexin25 mg/kg q12h · 5 days
Non-purulent — Moderate (Inpatient)IVCefazolin33 mg/kg q8h
Non-purulent — Not improving on CefazolinIVVancomycin15 mg/kg q6h · Trough 10–15 mcg/mL
Purulent (MRSA) — MildPOTMP/SMX6 mg/kg q12h · 5 days min
Purulent (MRSA) — SevereIVVancomycin15 mg/kg q6h · Trough 10–15 mcg/mL
PCN/Ceph Allergy — GeneralPO/IVClindamycin10 mg/kg/dose q8h (30 mg/kg/day, max 600 mg/dose)
PCN/Ceph Allergy — Severe (osteomyelitis, MRSA cellulitis)PO/IVClindamycinPO: 13.3 mg/kg/dose q8h (40 mg/kg/day, max 600 mg/dose) · IV: 13.3 mg/kg/dose q8h (max 900 mg/dose)
Pediatric UTI
>60 Days Old · Cystitis + Pyelonephritis
KCHC Pediatric Antimicrobial Dosing Guideline · June 2023 · P&T Approved April 2023
⚠
Exclusions: Immunocompromised · Toxic appearing · Multiple infections. Culture before antibiotics. Adjust to narrowest spectrum once susceptibilities available.
ConditionRouteAgentDoseDuration
Cystitis — No AllergyPOCephalexin50–100 mg/kg/day ÷ BID or TID (max 500mg/dose)7 days
Cystitis — PCN Allergy (anaphylaxis)POCiprofloxacin10–20 mg/kg/dose BID (max 750mg)7 days
Cystitis — Needs IV (No Allergy)IVCefazolin33.3 mg/kg/dose q8h (100 mg/kg/day, max 2,000 mg/dose)Per culture
Pyelonephritis (<2 yrs)PO or IVCephalexin or CefazolinSame as cystitis dosing10 days
Pyelonephritis (>2 yrs)PO or IVCephalexin or CefazolinSame as cystitis dosing7 days
HIV PEP — Adult & Pediatric
Post-Exposure Prophylaxis · Non-Occupational · SART Policy
KCHC Pharmacy Policy · Revised: Jun 2025
⚠
Start within 72 hours of exposure. Duration 28 days. Does NOT require ID approval. Orders through EPIC. Available from Omnicell ED or Inpatient Pharmacy.
PopulationDolutegravirPartner Agent
≥12 yrs, >35 kg, CrCl >50Tivicay® 50mg QDTruvada® (FTC 200mg/TDF 300mg) 1 tab daily
≥12 yrs, >35 kg, CrCl <50 to 15Tivicay® 50mg QDVemlidy® (TAF) 25mg QD + Lamivudine 150mg QD
≥12 yrs, >35 kg, On HDTivicay® 50mg QDVemlidy® 25mg QD (after HD on HD days) + Lamivudine 150mg QD
17–22 kg (Peds SART)Tivicay PD® 25mg QDTruvada® FTC 100mg/TDF 150mg daily
22–28 kg (Peds SART)Tivicay PD® 30mg QDTruvada® FTC 133mg/TDF 200mg daily
28–35 kg (Peds SART)Tivicay PD® 30mg QDTruvada® FTC 167mg/TDF 250mg daily
3–6 kg (Peds, unable to swallow)Tivicay PD® 5mg QDSee age-appropriate regimen below
4 wks–<2 yrs (unable to swallow)Tivicay PD® per weight bandZidovudine syrup + Lamivudine oral solution 4 mg/kg/dose BID
Expedited Partner Therapy
EPT · STI Prophylaxis
KCHC Pharmacy Policy · Revised: Jun 2025
IndicationAgentDoseRoute
Chlamydia EPTAzithromycin1g oral sachet — single dosePO
Gonorrhea (add if co-treatment)Ceftriaxone500mg IM × 1 (or 1g if >150 kg)IM
Pediatric Antimicrobial Dosing
Weight-Based · Ages 2 Months – 18 Years · Normal Renal Function
KCHC Pediatric Antimicrobial Dosing Guideline · June 2023 · P&T Approved April 2023 · Authors: Dumont PharmD, Luka PharmD, Hammerschlag MD
⚠
All doses assume normal renal function. Adjust for renal impairment. Verify max doses at each calculation. Ages 2 months–18 years unless otherwise specified.
DrugIndicationRouteDoseMax Dose
AmoxicillinGeneralPO22.5 mg/kg/dose q12h (45 mg/kg/day)875 mg/dose
High-dose (CAP, AOM, penicillin-resistant pneumococcal)PO45 mg/kg/dose q12h (90 mg/kg/day)2,000 mg/dose
Streptococcal pharyngitisPO50 mg/kg once daily1,000 mg/dose
Amoxicillin/Clavulanate
*Dosed by amoxicillin component		GeneralPO22.5 mg/kg/dose q12h (45 mg/kg/day)875 mg/dose
High-dose (CAP, AOM, penicillin-resistant pneumococcal) — use 600 mg/5 mL suspensionPO45 mg/kg q12h (90 mg/kg/day)2,000 mg/dose
AmpicillinGeneral (including CAP)IV50 mg/kg/dose q6h (200 mg/kg/day)2,000 mg/dose
High-dose (meningitis, endocarditis, penicillin-resistant pneumococcal)IV75 mg/kg/dose q6h (300 mg/kg/day)3,000 mg/dose
Urinary tract infectionIV37.5 mg/kg/dose q6h (150 mg/kg/day)2,000 mg/dose
Azithromycin
Pertussis		Infants 1–5 months: 10 mg/kg once daily × 5 days · Infants ≥6 months, children, adolescents: 10 mg/kg Day 1 then 5 mg/kg Days 2–5PO/IVPer age band (see note)500 mg/dose Day 1 · 250 mg/dose Days 2–5
CefazolinGeneralIV33.3 mg/kg/dose q8h (100 mg/kg/day)2,000 mg/dose
CeftarolineInfants <2 monthsIV6 mg/kg/dose q8h—
Infants ≥2 months – <2 yearsIV8 mg/kg/dose q8h—
Children ≥2 years (≤33 kg) / (>33 kg)IV≤33 kg: 12 mg/kg/dose q8h · >33 kg: 400 mg q8h · ≥18 yrs: 600 mg q12h400–600 mg/dose
Ceftriaxone
⚠ Incompatible with Ca²⁺ — never co-infuse with Ca-containing solutions (LR, PN) in any patient		Intra-abdominal (+ metronidazole)IV50 mg/kg/dose q24h2,000 mg/dose
Community-acquired pneumoniaIV75 mg/kg/dose q24h2,000 mg/dose
Meningitis (do NOT use in patients <28 days)IV100 mg/kg/day q24h4,000 mg/day
Otitis mediaIV50 mg/kg/dose q24h × 1–3 days1,000 mg/dose
ClindamycinGeneralPO/IV10 mg/kg/dose q8h (30 mg/kg/day)600 mg/dose
Severe (osteomyelitis, MRSA cellulitis)PO/IVPO: 13.3 mg/kg/dose q8h (40 mg/kg/day, max 600 mg/dose) · IV: 13.3 mg/kg/dose q8h (40 mg/kg/day, max 900 mg/dose)PO 600 · IV 900 mg/dose
DoxycyclineGeneralPO/IV2 mg/kg/dose q12h (4 mg/kg/day)100 mg/dose
Chlamydial infections (children ≥8 years)PO100 mg twice daily × 7 days100 mg/dose
FluconazoleCandidiasis (oropharyngeal, esophageal)PO/IV6 mg/kg/dose once daily400 mg/dose
Systemic candidiasis (candidemia, invasive)PO/IV12 mg/kg/dose once daily800 mg/dose
Uncomplicated vulvovaginal candidiasis (adolescents)PO150 mg single dose150 mg
GentamicinGeneralIV2.5 mg/kg/dose q8h (7.5 mg/kg/day)—
Synergy (Gram-positive)IV2 mg/kg/dose q8h (6 mg/kg/day)—
MetronidazoleGeneral / AppendicitisPO/IV10 mg/kg/dose q8h (30 mg/kg/day)General 1,333 mg · Appendicitis 500 mg/dose
C. difficilePO/IV10 mg/kg/dose q8h500 mg/dose
Trichomoniasis (<45 kg)PO15 mg/kg/dose q8h (45 mg/kg/day)667 mg/dose
Trichomoniasis (≥45 kg / adolescents)PO500 mg q12h × 7 days500 mg/dose
Oseltamivir
Influenza treatment		Infants ≤8 monthsPO3 mg/kg/dose q12h—
Infants ≥9 monthsPO3.5 mg/kg/dose q12h—
≤15 kgPO30 mg q12h30 mg/dose
>15–23 kgPO45 mg q12h45 mg/dose
>23–40 kg / >40 kgPO>23–40 kg: 60 mg q12h · >40 kg: 75 mg q12h75 mg/dose
Piperacillin/Tazobactam
*Dosed by piperacillin component		General (including intra-abdominal), ≤40 kgIV100 mg/kg/dose q8h (300 mg/kg/day)3,000 mg/dose
General, >40 kgIV3,000 mg/dose q6h3,000 mg/dose
Nosocomial pneumonia (infants >9 months, children, adolescents)IV100 mg/kg/dose q6h (400 mg/kg/day)4,000 mg/dose
TMP/SMX
*Dosed by trimethoprim component		Severe infection (osteomyelitis, meningitis, PCP pneumonia)PO/IV5 mg/kg/dose q6–8h (15–20 mg/kg/day)—
SSTIPO/IV6 mg/kg/dose q12h (12 mg/kg/day)320 mg/dose
UTIPO4–6 mg/kg/dose q12h320 mg/dose
Gentamicin Monitoring — Pediatric
For patients with normal renal function receiving gentamicin >48 hours: draw trough just prior to the 4th dose. Target trough: <2 mcg/mL (general), <1 mcg/mL (synergy).
Ceftriaxone — Calcium Compatibility Warning
Ceftriaxone is incompatible with calcium. Do NOT reconstitute, admix, or infuse simultaneously with any calcium-containing solution (LR, PN) via Y-site in ANY patient. Sequential administration in non-neonatal patients is acceptable if the line is flushed thoroughly with a compatible solution between infusions.
Pediatric Status Epilepticus Pathway
Convulsive Status Epilepticus · All Pediatric Areas Except NICU/Nursery
Kings County Pediatric Convulsive Status Epilepticus Pathway · June 2022
⚠
Inclusion: Continuous seizure >5 min OR intermittent seizure without return to baseline. Exclusion: Infants <2 months · Strong suspicion of non-epileptic events. This pathway is a guide — does not replace clinical judgment.
0–5 min — Stabilization + First Line
Immediate Actions
  • Place patient side-lying, bed at lowest position, side rails up · Suction · 2L nasal cannula
  • Ensure BVM at bedside · Start continuous cardiorespiratory monitoring · Start time keeping
  • Check POC glucose: treat if <70 → No IV: Glucose Gel 10–20g · IV: D10W 5 mL/kg (max 150 mL)
  • If concern for ingestion: call Poison Control 1-800-222-1222
First-Line Benzodiazepines — Choose ONE (5–10 min)
RouteDrugDoseMax
IN (No IV)Midazolam (5 mg/mL)0.2 mg/kg10 mg
IM (No IV)Midazolam0.2 mg/kg10 mg
IVLorazepam (preferred if IV)0.1 mg/kg — infuse over 1–2 min4 mg
IVDiazepam0.2 mg/kg10 mg
With IV access: draw labs (CBC, BMP, Mg, Phos, LFTs, anti-seizure medication level) · Order second-line therapy
15–30 min — Second-Line (5 min after 2nd Benzo dose)
!For anti-seizure naïve patients: start with Levetiracetam. For patients on home anti-seizure medication: give that medication first if listed below. Call Rapid Response #3144 Code 66 · Page Pediatric Neurology 917-219-1267
Second-Line — Choose ONE
DrugDoseRateCautions
Levetiracetam IV ⭐ First choice60 mg/kgOver 10 minMax 4,500 mg · Available in Pyxis
Valproate IV40 mg/kgOver 10 minMax 3,000 mg · Caution <2 yrs · Avoid in POLG1 mutation, liver dysfunction, metabolic conditions, pancreatitis, thrombocytopenia
Fosphenytoin IV20 mgPE/kgOver 10 minMax 1,500 mg
Phenobarbital IV20 mg/kgOver 15 minMax 1,000 mg
Lacosamide IV200–400 mgOver 15 minApproved ≥4 years only
Levetiracetam — Pyxis Weight-Band Dosing Table (60 mg/kg)
Weight RangeDose (from Pyxis)Weight RangeDose (from Pyxis)
4.5 kg to <8.5 kg250 mg25 kg to <35 kg1,500 mg
8.5 kg to <12.5 kg500 mg35 kg to <50 kg2,000 mg
12.5 kg to <18 kg750 mg50 kg to <75 kg3,000 mg
18 kg to <25 kg1,000 mg>75 kg4,500 mg
30–45 min — Third Line / Refractory
!Prepare to intubate: #3144 Code 88 · Administer additional second-line agent (from list above) after 15 minutes · Alert pharmacy · Review labs
Special Considerations
INH (Isoniazid) Ingestion — Add Pyridoxine
  • Manage per pathway PLUS STAT Pyridoxine IV
  • Known ingestion quantity: 1g Pyridoxine per 1g INH ingested, up to 70 mg/kg (max 5,000 mg)
  • Unknown quantity: 70 mg/kg Pyridoxine (max 5,000 mg)
  • Administer over 10 min · If seizure stops, run remaining Pyridoxine over 4 hours
Indications for Emergent CT Head (Non-Contrast)
  • Suspected neurosurgical emergency
  • Known intracranial lesion OR new onset focal neurological deficit prior to seizure
  • Recent trauma preceding status epilepticus
  • Concern for serious infection (brain abscess) or need for diagnostic LP
  • Further imaging decisions in discussion with pediatric neurology
Levalbuterol Continuous Inhalation Protocol
Acute Hyperactive Airway Disease · Continuous Nebulization
⚠ DRAFT PROTOCOL — NOT YET P&T OR MEDICAL BOARD APPROVED · Use with caution · Verify with supervising pharmacist before dispensing · Pending finalization
KCHC Pharmacy · DRAFT · No Effective Date · No Approval Date
Approved Locations
• PICU • NICU • ICUs • Emergency Department
Indication
Patients requiring inhaled beta-agonist at a frequency of q2h or more. Used due to albuterol 0.5% shortage — levalbuterol 0.25% is non-formulary but not restricted in approved locations. Levalbuterol dose = 50% of desired albuterol dose.
Ordering & Preparation Tables
⚠
Pharmacist prepares syringe using levalbuterol 0.25% + NS 0.9% to 50 mL total volume. One dose at a time — request refill with ≥30 min notice. Label "For Inhalation Use Only" · 12-hour expiration. Physician reassesses every 4 or 8 hours.
Levalbuterol DoseDurationRateTotal VolLevalbuterol VolumeNS Volume
2.5 mg/hr4 hours12.5 mL/hr50 mL10 mg (4 mL)46 mL
8 hours6.25 mL/hr50 mL20 mg (8 mL)42 mL
5 mg/hr4 hours12.5 mL/hr50 mL20 mg (8 mL)42 mL
8 hours6.25 mL/hr50 mL40 mg (16 mL)34 mL
7.5 mg/hr4 hours12.5 mL/hr50 mL30 mg (12 mL)38 mL
8 hours6.25 mL/hr50 mL60 mg (24 mL)26 mL
10 mg/hr4 hours12.5 mL/hr50 mL40 mg (16 mL)34 mL
8 hours6.25 mL/hr50 mL80 mg (32 mL)18 mL
Adverse Effects
  • Tachycardia (~3%)
  • Nervousness, tremors, dizziness, nausea
  • Decrease in serum potassium — monitor electrolytes
Master Renal Dosing Table
Adult · IV Doses · ABW Unless Specified · IHD + CRRT
KCHC Antimicrobial Stewardship · P&T Approved: Apr 2021
🔬 Renal Dose Lookup
Drug + Renal Status → Direct Dose AnswerKCHC Renal Dosing Guideline · P&T Apr 2021
Enter CrCl to get the specific dose tier for this patient
🧮 CrCl Quick Calc — IBW / AdjBW (Cockcroft-Gault) ▼
Age
ABW (kg)
Height (cm)
SCr (mg/dL)
Sex
CrCl = [(140-Age) × DosingWt / (72 × SCr)] × 0.85 if female · AdjBW threshold: >130% IBW (KCHC Renal Dosing Guideline)
⚠
Use Cockcroft-Gault with IBW (or AdjBW if ABW >130% IBW). All IV doses unless noted. PD generally follows IHD unless specified separately. Source: KCHC Antimicrobial Renal Dosing Guideline · P&T Approved 4/20/2021
Renal Status:
Antimicrobial Normal (CrCl ≥50) Renal Adjustment (CrCl <50) IHD (Post-HD) CRRT
Vancomycin
Use IBW; AdjBW if ABW >130% IBW		 15–20 mg/kg q8–12h
(Refer to Vanco Dosing Guideline)		 CrCl 55–79: 15–20 mg/kg q12h
CrCl 20–54: 15–20 mg/kg q12–24h
CrCl <20 or AKI: 15 mg/kg ×1, redose when level <20 mg/L		 20–25 mg/kg loading dose, then 7.5–10 mg/kg after HD (pre-HD level goal 15–20 mg/L) 7.5–10 mg/kg q12h (adjust per trough levels)
Acyclovir
Use IBW; AdjBW if BMI >40		 10 mg/kg q8h (CNS/Zoster)
5 mg/kg q8h (HSV non-CNS)		 CNS/Zoster: CrCl 25–50: q12h · CrCl 10–24: q24h · CrCl <10: 5 mg/kg q24h
HSV: CrCl 25–50: q12h · CrCl 10–24: q24h · CrCl <10: 2.5 mg/kg q24h		 5 mg/kg q24h (CNS)
2.5 mg/kg q24h (HSV)		 10 mg/kg q12–24h (CNS)
5 mg/kg q12–24h (HSV)
Amikacin
IBW; AdjBW if ABW >120% IBW		 15 mg/kg q24h
(Hartford Nomogram or trough)		 CrCl <20 or unstable renal: 7.5 mg/kg; dose by level 5–7.5 mg/kg after HD; redose when pre-HD <10 mg/L 10 mg/kg loading dose, then 7.5 mg/kg q24–48h; redose when <10 mg/L
Ampicillin/Sulbactam 3g q6h
(consider q4h for Acinetobacter)		 CrCl 15–29: 3g q12h
CrCl <15: 3g q24h		 3g q12–24h
PD: 1.5g q12h or 3g q24h		 3g q8–12h
Aztreonam 2g q8h
(1g for non-severe)		 CrCl 10–30: 1g q8h
CrCl <10: 500mg q8h		 500mg q8–12h CVVH: 2g q12h
CVVHD/CVVHDF: 2g load, then 1g q8h or 2g q12h
Cefazolin 2g q8h
(1g for non-severe)		 CrCl 30–49: 1–2g q8–12h
CrCl 11–29: 1g q12h (2g for severe)
CrCl <11: 500mg–1g q24h		 1g q24h or 2g post-HD
PD: 500mg q12h or 1g q24h		 2g load, then 1g q8h or 2g q12h
Cefepime 2g q8h (severe/Pseudo/bacteremia)
2g q12h (UTI/non-severe)		 Severe: CrCl 30–60: q12h · CrCl 11–29: 2g q24h or 1g q12h · CrCl <11: 1g q24h
Non-severe: CrCl 30–60: 1g q12h · CrCl 11–29: 1g q24h · CrCl <11: 500mg q24h		 1g q24h or 2g post-HD 2g q8–12h
Ceftaroline 600mg q12h
(q8h for severe/refractory MRSA)		 CrCl 31–50: 400mg q12h
CrCl 15–30: 300mg q12h
CrCl <15: 200mg q12h		 200mg q12h 400–600mg q12h
Ceftazidime 2g q8h CrCl 31–50: 2g q12h
CrCl 16–30: 2g q24h
CrCl <15: 1g q24h		 1g q24h or 1g/1g/2g post-HD 2g q8–12h
Ceftazidime/Avibactam 2.5g q8h CrCl 31–50: 1.25g q8h
CrCl 16–30: 0.94g q12h
CrCl 6–15: 0.94g q24h
CrCl ≤5: 0.94g q48h		 0.94g q24h 1.25g q8h
Ceftolozane/Tazobactam 1.5g q8h (standard)
3g q8h (MDR Pseudo pneumonia)		 Standard: CrCl 30–50: 750mg q8h · CrCl ≤29: 375mg q8h
MDR Pseudo: CrCl 30–50: 1.5g q8h · CrCl ≤29: 750mg q8h		 Standard: 750mg ×1, then 150mg q8h
MDR Pseudo: 2.25g ×1, then 450mg q8h		 1.5g q8h
Daptomycin
Consider AdjBW if obese		 4–6 mg/kg q24h (SSTI)
8–10 mg/kg q24h (MRSA bacteremia/endo/osteo)		 CrCl <30: same dose q48h (SSTI)
8 mg/kg q48h (MRSA bacteremia)		 Same dose q48h (SSTI)
Alt: usual dose post-HD; +50% on 72h interdialytic day		 4–6 mg/kg q24h (SSTI)
8 mg/kg q48h (bacteremia)
Ertapenem 1g q24h CrCl ≤30: 500mg q24h 500mg q24h or 1g post-HD 1g q24h
Fluconazole 12 mg/kg ×1, then 6 mg/kg q24h (Candidemia)
200mg q24h (OPC/cystitis)		 CrCl ≤50: 50% of maintenance q24h (Candidemia)
CrCl ≤50: 100mg q24h (OPC/cystitis)		 Administer maintenance dose on HD days only after dialysis
200mg→400mg · 400mg→800mg · 800mg→1200mg q24h		 Usual dose q24h
Gentamicin
IBW; AdjBW if ABW >120% IBW		 5–7 mg/kg q24h systemic
1 mg/kg q8h (gram+ synergy)		 Systemic: CrCl <20 or unstable: 1.5–2 mg/kg; dose by level
Synergy: CrCl 40–59: q12h · CrCl 20–39: q24h · CrCl <20: by level		 Systemic: 2–3 mg/kg load, then 1.5–2 mg/kg after HD; redose when pre-HD <3–5 mg/L
Synergy: 2–3 mg/kg load, then 1 mg/kg after HD; pre-HD <1 mg/L		 Systemic: 2–3 mg/kg load, then 1.5–2.5 mg/kg q24–48h; <2 mg/L
Synergy: 2–3 mg/kg load, then 1 mg/kg q24–48h; <1 mg/L
Levofloxacin 750mg q24h CrCl 20–49: 750mg q48h
CrCl <20: 750mg ×1, then 500mg q48h		 750mg ×1, then 500mg q48h 750mg ×1, then 500mg q24h OR 750mg q48h
Meropenem 1g q8h (standard)
2g q8h (CNS/MDR organisms)		 Standard: CrCl 26–50: 1g q12h · CrCl 10–25: 500mg q12h · CrCl <10: 500mg q24h
CNS: CrCl 26–50: 2g q12h · CrCl 10–25: 1g q12h · CrCl <10: 1g q24h		 500mg q24h (standard)
1g q24h (CNS/MDR)		 1g q8h
Pip/Tazobactam 3.375g q6h (standard)
4.5g q6h (Pseudomonas)		 Standard: CrCl 20–39: 2.25g q6h · CrCl <20: 2.25g q8h
Pseudo: CrCl 20–39: 3.375g q6h · CrCl <20: 2.25g q6h		 2.25g q8h 4.5g load, then 2.25–3.375g q6h
TMP/SMX
Use AdjBW if obese		 5 mg/kg q8h IV (PCP/Steno/Burkholderia)
1–2 DS PO q12h (UTI/SSTI)		 IV: CrCl 15–30: 5 mg/kg q12h · CrCl <15: 5 mg/kg q24h
PO: CrCl 15–30: 50% dose · CrCl <15: 25% dose (caution)		 IV: 5 mg/kg q24h
PO: 1 DS ×1, then 1 SS q24h		 IV: No adjustment needed
PO: N/A
No Renal Adjustment Needed
These agents do not require dose adjustment for renal failure
KCHC Antimicrobial Stewardship · P&T Approved: Apr 2021
AgentStandard DoseNote
Ceftriaxone1–2g q24hNo adjustment regardless of CrCl, HD, or CRRT. Note: 2g q12h for meningitis/endocarditis.
Linezolid600mg q12h IV/PONo dose adjustment. Monitor for thrombocytopenia with prolonged use.
Oxacillin2g q4–6h IVHepatically metabolized. No renal adjustment.
Clindamycin600–900mg q8h IVHepatically metabolized. No renal adjustment.
Metronidazole500mg q8h IVNo adjustment for renal failure.
Doxycycline100mg q12h PO/IVNo renal adjustment required.
Azithromycin500mg q24h IV/PONo renal adjustment.
Tigecycline100mg ×1, then 50mg q12hNo renal adjustment.
Polymyxin B2.5 mg/kg ×1, then 1.25–1.5 mg/kg q12hNo renal adjustment. Use AdjBW if obese.
Peritoneal Dialysis (PD)
Adult · Generally Follows IHD Unless Specified
KCHC Antimicrobial Stewardship · P&T Approved: Apr 2021
AgentPD DoseNotes
VancomycinLD 25 mg/kg, then 7.5–10 mg/kg by levelSame as IHD protocol. Level-guided.
Cefazolin500mg q12h OR 1g q24hPD-specific dosing
Ampicillin/Sulbactam1.5g q12h or 3g q24hPD-specific dosing
CeftriaxoneNo adjustmentStandard dose regardless of modality
All othersFollow IHD dosingUnless specifically noted above
CrCl + Body Weight Calculator
Cockcroft-Gault · IBW · AdjBW · ABW · Auto-calculates on input
KCHC Clinical Protocol · Cockcroft-Gault 1976
!
Vancomycin: use ABW · Aminoglycosides: use IBW (AdjBW if >120% IBW) · CrCl: use IBW (AdjBW if >130% IBW) · Per KCHC guidelines.
🧮 Patient Parameters
Auto-calculates — no button needed

ft in

Cockcroft-Gault
—
Weight Selection Guide — KCHC
  • Vancomycin dose: Use ABW always · Per KCHC Vancomycin Dosing Guideline
  • CrCl calculation: Use IBW. If ABW < IBW → use ABW. If ABW >130% IBW → use AdjBW · Per KCHC Vanco + Renal Dosing Guidelines
  • Aminoglycosides (gentamicin, amikacin): Use IBW. If ABW < IBW → use ABW. If ABW >120% IBW → use AdjBW · Per KCHC Renal Dosing + Sepsis Guidelines
  • IVIG: Use IBW. If ABW < IBW → use ABW. If ABW >120% IBW → use AdjBW · Per KCHC IVIG Protocol
  • Acyclovir: Use IBW if ABW > IBW. If ABW < IBW → use ABW. If BMI >40 → consider AdjBW · Per KCHC Renal Dosing Guideline
  • ⚠ Gray zone (120–130% IBW): CrCl → IBW · Aminoglycosides/IVIG → AdjBW · Verify per drug
  • AdjBW formula: IBW + 0.4 × (ABW − IBW)
  • Devine IBW: Male = 50 + 2.3×(in over 5ft) · Female = 45.5 + 2.3×(in over 5ft)
  • Robinson IBW: Male = 52 + 1.9×(in over 5ft) · Female = 49 + 1.7×(in over 5ft)
Extended Infusion Beta-Lactams
ICU Only · Maximizing %T>MIC · KCHC Protocol
KCHC Antimicrobial Stewardship · P&T Approved: Apr 2021
⚠
Extended infusions require dedicated IV line. Loading dose given over standard 30 min before starting extended maintenance. Do NOT interrupt extended infusion.
AgentLoading DoseMaintenance (Extended)Infusion Time
Piperacillin/Tazobactam4.5g over 30 min3.375g q8h (most) · 4.5g q8h (Pseudomonas/sepsis)4 hours
Cefepime2g over 30 min2g q8h (CrCl ≥60) · 2g q12h (CrCl 30–60)3 hours
Meropenem1–2g over 30 min1g q8h (most) · 2g q8h (CNS/resistant)3 hours
Clinical Rationale
Beta-lactams are time-dependent — efficacy depends on %T>MIC. Extended infusion prolongs time above MIC without increasing total daily dose. Most beneficial for organisms with MIC ≥4 mcg/mL or resistant GNR in ICU.
Surgical Prophylaxis
Pre-Operative · Timing + Dosing · By Procedure Type
KCHC Antimicrobial Stewardship · P&T Approved: Aug 2020
⚠
Single dose prior to incision. No further prophylaxis after closure for clean/clean-contaminated procedures.
Surgery TypeRecommended AgentPCN Allergy AlternativeTiming
Most ProceduresCefazolin 2g IV (3g if ≥120 kg)Vancomycin + Levofloxacin or GentamicinWithin 60 min of incision
ColorectalCefoxitin 2g IV OR Amp/Sulbactam 3g OR Cefazolin + MetronidazoleClindamycin + Levofloxacin or GentamicinWithin 60 min
Vancomycin (MRSA risk)Vancomycin 15 mg/kg IVUsed for MRSA colonization or high-risk patientsWithin 60–120 min of incision
Cardiac/ThoracicCefazolin IV or Amp/Sulbactam IVVancomycin or ClindamycinWithin 60 min
OB/GYN — C-sectionCefazolin 2g IVClindamycin + GentamicinWithin 60 min
Urologic — Lower tractCiprofloxacin 400mg IV OR TMP/SMX OR CefazolinGentamicinWithin 60–120 min
AgentAdult DoseIntra-op Redosing
Cefazolin2g (3g if ≥120 kg)Every 4 hours
Cefoxitin2g IVEvery 2 hours
Amp/Sulbactam3g IVEvery 2 hours
Vancomycin15 mg/kg IVNA
Clindamycin900mg IVEvery 6 hours
Metronidazole500mg IVNA
Gentamicin5 mg/kg IV (IBW/AdjBW)NA
IV to PO Conversion
Criteria for Safe Oral Step-Down
KCHC Antimicrobial Stewardship · P&T Approved: Nov 2016
⚠
Switch after at least 48–72 hours of IV therapy. All criteria must be met.
Inclusion Criteria — ALL Must Be Met
✓
Temperature <100°F in the previous 24 hours
✓
HR <100, RR <24, SBP >90 mmHg — hemodynamically stable
✓
Tolerating oral intake — no NPO, no significant nausea/vomiting
✗
Exclude if: Endocarditis · Meningitis · Brain Abscess · Febrile Neutropenia · Osteomyelitis · Bacteremia not cleared · Septic shock · Immunosuppression · NPO · GI obstruction · Ileus
IV AgentOral Equivalent
Levofloxacin 750mg IVLevofloxacin 750mg PO (1:1)
Metronidazole 500mg IVMetronidazole 500mg PO (1:1)
Linezolid 600mg IVLinezolid 600mg PO (1:1)
Fluconazole 400mg IVFluconazole 400mg PO (1:1)
Azithromycin 500mg IVAzithromycin 500mg PO (1:1)
Cefazolin 1g IV q12hCephalexin 500mg PO q6h
Ceftriaxone 1g IV q24hLevofloxacin 750mg PO OR Cefpodoxime PO (per culture)
Vancomycin IV (SSTI)Bactrim DS 2 tabs PO BID OR Doxycycline 100mg PO BID
Doxycycline 100mg IVDoxycycline 100mg PO (1:1)
Y-Site Compatibility Alerts
Critical IV Incompatibilities in ICU
KCHC Antimicrobial Stewardship · P&T Approved: Apr 2021
Drug 1Drug 2CompatibilityAction Required
VancomycinPiperacillin/Tazobactam❌ INCOMPATIBLESeparate lines or flush completely between
VancomycinCefepime❌ INCOMPATIBLESeparate lines or flush completely between
VancomycinMeropenem❌ INCOMPATIBLESeparate lines or flush completely between
CeftriaxoneCalcium-containing solutions⚠️ AVOID in neonatesAdults: separate — neonates: contraindicated
DaptomycinDextrose-containing solutions❌ INCOMPATIBLEDilute in NS only
Pip/TazoAcyclovir · Amiodarone · Ciprofloxacin · Levofloxacin · Tobramycin❌ INCOMPATIBLESeparate lines required
CefepimeAmphotericin B · Ciprofloxacin · Vancomycin · Propofol❌ INCOMPATIBLESeparate lines required
MeropenemAcyclovir · Amiodarone · Ciprofloxacin · Propofol❌ INCOMPATIBLESeparate lines required
Remdesivir (Veklury®) — Pharmacist Guide
COVID-19 · Verification Criteria · KCHC Antimicrobial Stewardship
KCHC Antimicrobial Stewardship · Last Revised: Jan 2022
⚠
Verify LFTs, oxygen requirement, and comorbidities before approving. NOT used in mechanically ventilated patients. Flush with at least 30 mL NS after each infusion.
Step 1 — Contraindications (Hold Remdesivir If):
AST >350 U/L (10× ULN) — CONTRAINDICATED
ALT >310 U/L (10× ULN) — CONTRAINDICATED
Patient StatusRemdesivir
Asymptomatic❌ No remdesivir
Symptomatic — No oxygen required, has comorbidities✅ 200mg ×1, then 100mg × 2 doses (3 days total)
Symptomatic — Requiring nasal cannula, high flow, BiPAP, or non-invasive ventilation✅ 200mg ×1, then 100mg × 4 doses (5 days total)
Mechanically ventilated❌ No remdesivir
Qualifying Comorbidities (any one qualifies)
High-Risk Comorbidities
Age ≥65 years
Obesity (BMI >30)
Pregnancy
Chronic kidney disease
Cardiovascular disease / HTN
Diabetes mellitus
Dementia
Liver disease
Current/former smoker
Substance use history
Immunosuppressive disease/treatment
Stroke / cerebrovascular disease
Chronic lung disease
Sickle cell disease
Neurodevelopmental disorders
Medical technology dependence
Administration Note
Infuse over 30 minutes. Flush with at least 30 mL NS after completion. Verify order in EPIC — check frequency (Once vs Daily), number of doses, and linked orders before verifying.
Vancomycin TDM
Trough Interpretation · KCHC Protocol
KCHC Antimicrobial Stewardship · P&T Approved: Oct 2020
⚠
Draw trough 15–30 min before 4th dose (steady state). Level-guided for IHD pre-dialysis. If >25 mcg/mL: hold and recheck when <20 mcg/mL.
Interpret Trough Level
TDM Interpretation
Level
—
mcg/mL
Target
—
mcg/mL
Action
—
 
IndicationTarget TroughAdjustment
Severe (Sepsis, Bacteremia, Endocarditis, Pneumonia, Meningitis)15–20 mcg/mL—
Mild (Cellulitis, Non-severe SSTI)10–15 mcg/mL—
IHD15–20 mcg/mL (pre-dialysis)—
<10 mcg/mLConsider more frequent dosing interval—
10–14 mcg/mLIncrease by 250–500 mg—
21–25 mcg/mLDecrease by 250–500 mg—
>25 mcg/mLHOLD — recheck when <20 mcg/mL. Max dose 2,000 mg.
Aminoglycoside TDM
Gentamicin · Tobramycin · Amikacin · Level Targets
KCHC Antimicrobial Stewardship · P&T Approved: Oct 2020
AgentTarget PeakTarget TroughSynergy (Endocarditis)
Gentamicin5–10 mcg/mL (serious) · 3–5 mcg/mL (UTI)<2 mcg/mL1 mg/kg q8–12h · peak 3–4 · trough <1
Tobramycin5–10 mcg/mL<2 mcg/mLSame as gentamicin
Amikacin20–35 mcg/mL<8 mcg/mLNot typically used for synergy
AKI ProtocolGentamicin 2 mg/kg initial, Amikacin 7.5 mg/kg initial. Subsequent doses strictly by level. Redose when <2 mcg/mL (Gent) or <8 mcg/mL (Amikacin).
Critical Calculations Reference
Cockcroft-Gault · Body Weight · Vancomycin Rules
KCHC Antimicrobial Stewardship · P&T Approved: Oct 2020
Cockcroft-Gault CrCl Formula
CrCl = [(140 − age) × weight] ÷ (72 × SCr) × gender factor

Gender factor: Male = 1.0 · Female = 0.85
Weight: Use IBW. If ABW < IBW, use ABW. If ABW > 130% IBW, use AdjBW.
AdjBW = IBW + 0.4 × (ABW − IBW)

Note: If elderly + low ABW + SCr <0.8, consider rounding SCr to 0.8.
IBW Formula
Male IBW = 50 kg + 2.3 kg per inch over 5 feet
Female IBW = 45.5 kg + 2.3 kg per inch over 5 feet
Vancomycin Hold Rule
If trough >25 mcg/mL → HOLD dose
Recheck level every 6–12 hours
Re-dose only when level <20 mcg/mL
Max single dose: 2,000 mg (ABW-based)
IHD Dosing Rule
LD: 25 mg/kg ABW IV STAT · Maintenance: 7.5–10 mg/kg ABW after each HD session
Target pre-HD: 15–20 mcg/mL
Hold: ≥30 · Decrease: 26–29 · No change: 15–25 · Increase: <15
Aminoglycoside PK
Gentamicin · Tobramycin · Amikacin · Patient-Specific Dosing
KCHC Antimicrobial Stewardship · P&T Approved: Oct 2020
🧮 CrCl Quick Calc — IBW / AdjBW (Cockcroft-Gault) ▼
Age
ABW (kg)
Height (cm)
SCr (mg/dL)
Sex
CrCl = [(140-Age) × DosingWt / (72 × SCr)] × 0.85 if female · AdjBW threshold: >120% IBW (KCHC Aminoglycoside Guideline)
⚠
Steady-state levels required. Gent/Tobra: peak 5–10, trough <2. Amikacin: peak 20–35, trough <8. AKI: Gent 2 mg/kg initial, Amikacin 7.5 mg/kg initial.
Measured Levels + Regimen
Target Peak: 5–10 mcg/mL · Target Trough: <2 mcg/mL

All non-formulary antibiotics require ID approval. Vancomycin IV and Piperacillin/Tazobactam: first 7 days no ID approval needed — ID approval required after 7 days. ICU grace periods noted below apply concurrently.

Antifungal Prophylaxis
Fluconazole SICU · KCHC Sourced Content
Fluconazole Prophylaxis — SICU (Invasive Candidiasis Prevention)
Fluconazole Prophylaxis for Invasive Candidiasis in the SICU · Effective: 05/2022 · Reissued: 05/2024 · P&T Approved: 05/2022 · Reviewed: 08/2024

Indication: Prevention of candidemia in patients receiving TPN for >6 days AND with significant abdominal pathology:

  • Open abdomen (without skin graft, skin closure, or mesh closure)
  • Ongoing known abdominal infectious source not amenable to surgical/IR drainage requiring long-term antibacterials
  • Enterocutaneous or entero-atmospheric fistula or suspected developing fistula with clinical signs of sepsis on TPN
  • Other significant abdominal pathology at clinical team discretion
Renal StatusFluconazole Dose
Normal function200 mg PO daily
CrCl ≤50 mL/min100 mg PO daily
IHD (thrice weekly)200 mg PO on HD days only, after dialysis
Peritoneal dialysis100 mg PO daily
CRRT400 mg PO daily
!
Duration: Until TPN is discontinued. Can be given IV if oral is contraindicated — refer to KCHC IV to PO Guideline.
Candida Treatment Selection
Intra-Abdominal Infections · KCHC Sourced Content
Candida Treatment Selection — Intra-Abdominal Infections
KCHC Trauma/Perioperative Surgery Antibiotic Guideline · September 2023

Indication: Antifungal therapy approved when Candida is grown from intra-abdominal cultures (healthcare-associated or severe community-acquired infection).

Species / ScenarioRecommended Agent
C. albicans (no prior fluconazole exposure)Fluconazole
Severely ill patient or fluconazole-resistant species (e.g., C. glabrata)Echinocandin (e.g., caspofungin)
Prolonged TPN + significant abdominal pathology (open abdomen)Fluconazole prophylaxis — see Prophylaxis tab
Antifungal Renal Dosing
Quick Reference · KCHC Sourced Content
Antifungal Renal Dosing — Quick Reference
KCHC Antimicrobial Renal Dosing Guideline · P&T Approved: 4/20/2021
Agent Normal Function Renal Adjustment IHD CRRT
Fluconazole (candidemia) 12 mg/kg ×1, then 6 mg/kg q24h CrCl ≤50: 50% of maintenance q24h Full maintenance dose on HD days after dialysis only Double usual maintenance dose q24h
Fluconazole (oral/cystitis) 200 mg IV/PO q24h CrCl ≤50: 100 mg q24h See above See above
Caspofungin 70 mg IV day 1, then 50 mg q24h No adjustment (use caution in renal impairment) No adjustment No adjustment
Amphotericin B Lipid Complex 5 mg/kg q24h (round to nearest 50 mg) No adjustment (use caution) No adjustment No adjustment
Amphotericin B Liposomal 3–5 mg/kg q24h (round to nearest 50 mg) No adjustment (use caution) No adjustment No adjustment
!
Fluconazole renal dosing also in Renal Dosing tab. Amphotericin B doses indicated for cryptococcal meningitis in combination with flucytosine.

Expedited Partner Therapy (EPT)
Chlamydia · NYS Law · KCHC Pharmacy Department
KCHC Policy — Expedited Partner Therapy · Date First Issued: January 2018 · Date Effective: December 2020
Purpose & Legal Basis

NYS law allows therapy for chlamydia infection to be provided to patients with chlamydia AND for their sexual partners when it is believed the partner would not be likely to present for clinical evaluation.

It is the policy of KCHC to provide best care possible for patients with documented chlamydia — KCHC will make available expedited partner treatment in the STI practice.

EPT Drug: Azithromycin 1 gram sachet — Single Dose PO

Stocked in Pyxis — par level of 15 doses in STI Practice

Order in CPOE: "Azithromycin 1g w/partner(s)" — indicate number of partners in comment section

Eligibility — ALL Must Be Met
  • Patient has confirmed chlamydia diagnosis
  • Patient is NOT co-infected with gonorrhea or syphilis
  • Patient safety not in question (no suspected intimate partner violence or sexual abuse)
EPT Dispensing Workflow
Step-by-Step Process · KCHC STI Practice
Step-by-Step Dispensing Process
  1. Clinician diagnoses chlamydia and identifies partner(s) meeting EPT criteria
  2. Order placed in CPOE: "Azithromycin 1g w/partner(s)" — indicate number of partners in comment section
  3. In comment section of order, clinician indicates the number of partner(s)
  4. Clinician writes a handwritten prescription with "EPT" written on the face for each partner
  5. Nurse retrieves 1 dose of azithromycin 1g sachet from Pyxis to be administered to the patient
  6. Subsequent doses for partner(s): override under original patient profile — select "EPT" as reason for override
  7. A label is placed on each dose — partner information filled out if available (not mandatory)
  8. EPT handwritten prescriptions placed into Pyxis to be collected by pharmacy
  9. Prescriber provides NYC Health Department handout to patient upon dispensing
!
STI practice personnel must review the medications and instructions for use with the patient or family to assure understanding of proper use. NYC Health Department resource: https://www.health.ny.gov/publications/3849/
EPT Exclusions
Contraindications & When NOT to Dispense
Do NOT Dispense EPT When:
  • Patient is co-infected with gonorrhea OR syphilis — EPT is not appropriate in this situation
  • Patient safety is in question — suspected intimate partner violence or sexual abuse
  • Partner has suspected macrolide or ketolide allergy — partner must seek clinical evaluation
  • Suspected drug interaction between azithromycin and partner's medications — partner must seek clinical evaluation
⚠
If any exclusion applies: do NOT dispense EPT. Direct patient/partner to seek formal clinical evaluation. This strategy is also not appropriate where patient safety may be in question (i.e. suspected intimate partner violence or sexual abuse).
IVIG Dosing
KCHC IVIG Dosing Protocol · Effective: October 2022 · Tiffany Chen, PharmD, BCPS · Aleksander Feoktistov, MD, MBA · P&T Approved: September 2022
!Dose by IBW. Use AdjBW if ABW >20% over IBW. Use ABW if ABW < IBW. Round to nearest vial size in adults. Preferred product: Privigen. Per KCHC IVIG Dosing Protocol October 2022.
🧮 IVIG Weight & Dose Calculator
Patient Parameters
Enter per-indication dose (e.g. 0.4–0.5 g/kg ITP · 1–2 g/kg ITP refractory · 2 g/kg IVIG for Kawasaki)
IVIG Protocol & Products
KCHC IVIG Dosing Protocol · Effective: October 2022 · Tiffany Chen, PharmD, BCPS
Dosing Weight Rules
Weight Selection — Per KCHC Protocol
ConditionUse
Standard patientIdeal Body Weight (IBW)
ABW >20% over IBW (obese)Adjusted Body Weight (AdjBW)
ABW < IBW (underweight)Actual Body Weight (ABW)
IBW (male) = 50 kg + 2.3 × (inches over 60)
IBW (female) = 45.5 kg + 2.3 × (inches over 60)
AdjBW = IBW + 0.4 × (ABW – IBW)
Products Available at KCHC
ProductConcentrationIgA ContentStabilizerVial Sizes
Privigen
⭐ Preferred		10%<25 mcg/mLL-proline5g · 10g · 20g · 40g
Gamunex-C10%46 mcg/mLGlycine1g · 2.5g · 5g · 10g · 20g · 40g
Gammagard S/D10%<1 mcg/mLGlucose 5%5g · 10g
Pre-Medications
CategoryAgentDose
Analgesic / AntipyreticAcetaminophen650 mg PO or rectal
AntihistamineDiphenhydramine25–50 mg PO or IV
Cetirizine10 mg PO
Loratadine10 mg PO
CorticosteroidMethylprednisolone20–125 mg IV
Adverse Reactions
Infusion-Related Reactions

Common: Flushing · Tachycardia · Hypertension · Hypotension · Headaches · Dizziness · Nausea

Severe (STOP infusion — contact prescriber): Bronchospasm · Angioedema · Chills · Fever

Pharmacy Workflow
Pharmacist Steps — Per KCHC Protocol
  1. After order placed — check height, weight, and IBW in EPIC (or calculate manually)
  2. Determine correct dosing weight using rules above
  3. Notify provider if dose adjustment is needed
  4. Round calculated dose to nearest vial size in adult patients
  5. Dispense based on availability — preferred product is Privigen

Opioid Tolerant Definition — KCHC
KCHC Opioid Dosing Guideline · Jan 28, 2021

Patients receiving, for 1 week or longer, at least one of the following:

AgentMinimum Daily Dose
Oral Morphine60 mg/day
Transdermal FentaNYL25 mcg/hour
Oral OxyCODONE30 mg/day
Oral HYDROmorphone8 mg/day
Oral OxyMORphone25 mg/day
Oral HYDROcodone60 mg/day
Opioid Dosing
Opioid Naive & Tolerant · KCHC Opioid Dosing Guideline · Jan 28, 2021
KCHC Opioid Dosing Guideline · Effective: Jan 28, 2021
Opioid Naive Dose CalculatorInitial dose only
Opioid Naive — Starting Dose
Drug / Route—
Initial PRN Dose—
Onset / Duration—
Scheduled Dosing—
Renal Notes—
Pain Treatment Algorithm
KCHC Opioid Dosing Guideline · Jan 28, 2021
Pain ScoreOpioid NaiveOpioid Tolerant
≤3 MildNon-opioids preferred. If contraindicated — low opioid dose (see table above)Continue home regimen. Short-acting dose = 10–20% of long-acting dose Q2–4H PRN
≥4 ModerateOpioid naive dosing. Do NOT use fentanyl for opioid naive patientsIncrease scheduled dose by 30–50%. Short-acting = 10–20% of prior 24h dose. Consider pain consult.
SevereRapidly titrate short-acting opioids. If significant anxiety — administer opioids BEFORE sedating anxiolytics. Consider pain consult.
Common Opioid Errors — ISMP
KCHC Opioid Dosing Guideline · Jan 28, 2021
  • Mix-ups between HYDROmorphone and morphine — hydromorphone is NOT a generic for morphine
  • Mix-ups between IR and ER formulations (e.g., Morphine IR vs. MS Contin)
  • Prescribing initial doses too high for opioid naive patients — especially HYDROmorphone
  • Unfamiliarity with proper oral-to-IV dose conversions
  • Failure to remove old fentanyl patch before applying new patch
Equianalgesic Converter
Opioid Conversion Calculator · KCHC Opioid Dosing Guideline · Jan 28, 2021
KCHC Opioid Dosing Guideline · Effective: Jan 28, 2021
!When converting between opioids, round DOWN 20–50% to account for incomplete cross-tolerance. This is not a substitution calculator — clinical judgment required. Pain management consult recommended for complex conversions.
Conversion Calculator
KCHC: round down 20–50% for incomplete cross-tolerance
