📊 Pain Assessment Scales
Pain Assessment Scales
KCHC Pain Management Policy · Date Effective: July 2021
KCHC Pain Management Policy · Date Effective: July 2021
Pain ScalePatient PopulationMildModerateSevere
Numeric Rating Scale (NRS)Self-report — adults and children ≥10 yrs1–34–67–10
Wong-Baker FACESChildren 5 yrs to adult · learning difficulties · poor language1–34–67–10
NVPS (Nonverbal)Non-communicative adults · ventilated patients1–34–67–10
Riley Infant Pain ScaleInfants 3 months to 5 years123
COVERS ScaleNeonates birth to 3 months1–34–78–12
PAINADAdvanced dementia · non-communicative adults0–10 (probability of pain — NOT equivalent to verbal 0–10)
Reassessment Intervals
Route / SituationReassess At
IV, IM, Subcutaneous30 minutes after administration
Oral analgesic1 hour after administration
Patients with history of painEvery 4 hours and as needed
No pain on admission / no pain in last 24hDaily
Key Safety Points
KCHC Pain Management Policy · July 2021
  • Sedation precedes respiratory depression — reduced consciousness is the best early indicator of opioid toxicity, NOT slow respiratory rate
  • Notify MD if: respiratory rate <10 · sedation level >2 · pulse ox <92%
  • Patient can be hypoxic with normal RR and pulse ox — watch for mental status changes indicating early hypoxia
  • All patients on opioids should have orders for a bowel regimen
Transdermal Fentanyl Patch
KCHC Transdermal FentaNYL Patch Protocol · Effective: Jul 1, 2022
KCHC Transdermal FentaNYL Patch Protocol · P&T Approved: June 2022
!Non-formulary — requires Pain Management, Palliative Care, or Opioid Stewardship consult before ordering. Fatal overdose risk with first dose from overestimation of conversion. Peak levels occur 24–72 hours after application.
Fentanyl Patch Dose CalculatorDonner method · KCHC Protocol
Must be stable for ≥72 hours before conversion
Fentanyl Patch Recommendation
Daily Oral Morphine Equivalent—
Recommended Patch Dose—
Breakthrough Opioid (10–15% TDD)—
Bridge Instruction—
Titration Schedule—
Oral Morphine 24h (mg/day)Fentanyl Patch Dose (mcg/hr)
30–9025 mcg/hr
91–15050 mcg/hr
151–21075 mcg/hr
211–270100 mcg/hr
Every additional 60 mg/day+25 mcg/hr
Inclusion Criteria — Who Can Receive Fentanyl Patch
  • Opioid tolerant (see definition) for ≥1 week
  • Requires continuous, around-the-clock opioid for extended period
  • Pain cannot be managed by non-opioids, combination products, or IR opioids
  • History of persistent moderate-to-severe chronic pain (>6 months)
Exclusion Criteria — Contraindications
  • NOT opioid tolerant
  • Acute pain requiring short-term opioids (<30 days expected duration)
  • Post-operative pain management (including outpatient/day surgery)
  • Mild pain management only
  • Opioid-free for 1 week or longer
  • Significant respiratory depression / acute severe bronchial asthma
  • Patients under 2 years of age
Factors Affecting Absorption
  • Elevated body temperature (fever, heating pads, hot baths, electric blankets) — increases absorption, faster onset, shorter duration, possible overdose
  • Elderly, cachectic, low body weight — altered PK due to poor fat stores and muscle wasting. May not respond well.
  • CYP3A4 inhibitors — increase plasma concentrations
  • Half-life 17 hours — upon removal, ≥17 hours required for 50% decrease in serum concentration
⚠
Drug Interaction — Fentanyl + Linezolid: Risk of serotonin syndrome. Fentanyl has pro-serotonergic activity (phenylpiperidine structure). Linezolid is a weak, reversible MAOI. Combination can cause serotonin toxicity — tremor, myoclonus, hyperthermia, hyperreflexia, agitation, altered mental status. FDA warns against co-administration with serotonergic drugs. If both are clinically necessary (e.g. critically ill patient), notify MD and monitor closely. Symptoms typically resolve within 24h of discontinuing one agent. Source: Mayo Clinic / Journal of Clinical Pharmacy & Therapeutics 2020 · FDA Drug Safety Communication
Methadone — OUD / Withdrawal
KCHC Methadone for Inpatient/ED/CPEP Use · Effective: January 2025
KCHC Methadone Policy · Date Effective: January 2025 · Final Approval: March 17, 2025
!Methadone for PAIN is non-formulary — requires Pain Management or Palliative Care approval. This section covers methadone for OUD/withdrawal only. Place CATCH consult for all OUD patients.
Withdrawal Dose CalculatorNot enrolled in OTP
Age >65 · CNS depression · Respiratory risk · Recent benzo/alcohol use
Methadone OUD Order
Scenario—
Initial Dose—
PRN / Titration—
Maximum Daily Dose—
Formulation to Order—
Hold Criteria—
Missed Dose Protocol — OTP Patients
KCHC Methadone Policy · January 2025
Days MissedSafe Restart DosePRN Option
Up to 3 days (presents day 4)Continue usual dose—
4 days missed (presents day 5)Higher of: 50% previous dose OR 30 mg+10 mg q1–2h up to 50 mg/24h for ongoing withdrawal
5+ days missed (presents day 6+)Restart at 30 mg+10 mg q1–2h up to 50 mg/24h for ongoing withdrawal
Tablets vs. Diskettes — Which to Order
KCHC Methadone Policy · January 2025
FormulationUse ForStrengthsAdministration
Methadone TabletsOUD Withdrawal · Acute withdrawal not in OTP5 mg · 10 mgSwallow whole
Methadone Diskettes (Methadose)OTP Maintenance — dose verified40 mg scored (¼ = 10 mg)Do NOT chew/swallow. Dissolve in liquid. Rinse cup if residual remains.
Rikers / Correctional Health — Weekend Protocol
KCHC Methadone Policy · January 2025
CHS Pharmacy Contact Numbers
  • CHS Central Pharmacy: 347-774-7928 (M–F 8:30am–4pm · Sat–Sun 7am–3pm)
  • EMTC: 347-774-7852 · GRVC: 347-774-7771 · RNDC: 347-774-7722
  • NIC: 347-774-7999 · OBCC: 347-774-8725
Saturday Release from Rikers
  • Give instructions to return to ED on Sunday for daily methadone dose
  • If Sunday: discharge with instructions to attend OTP Monday morning
  • Place Ambulatory Referral to Psychiatry / Chemical Dependency Clinic
  • Order take-home Narcan kit for all OUD patients
ED Buprenorphine Macro-Induction — COWS-Based
KCHC ED Buprenorphine Protocol · Updated Feb 2023
COWS <10 (or ≥10 without objective symptoms)
  • Consider adjunctive medications, micro-induction, or observation
  • Adjunctive: Clonidine 0.1 mg PO · Ondansetron · Loperamide 4 mg · Diphenhydramine · NSAIDs
COWS ≥10 WITH objective symptoms (diarrhea, vomiting, piloerection, sweating, yawning)
  • Give 16 mg SL buprenorphine · Reassess in 30 minutes
  • If same or worse → give another 16 mg buprenorphine · Reassess 30 min
  • If still same or worse → give 16 mg buprenorphine (consider admission)
  • Discharge: prescribe 14 films 8 mg Suboxone OR return to ED x3 consecutive days
  • Give naloxone kit · Place Ambulatory Ref to Psychiatry/Chemical Dependency Clinic
ICU Pain, Sedation & Delirium
KCHC ICU Clinical Forum · Referenced 2018 Guidelines
KCHC ICU Clinical Forum · Referenced: Crit Care Med 2018;46(9):e825-e873
ICU Infusion Rate CalculatorWeight-based starting rates
ICU Infusion Starting Rate
Agent—
Concentration—
Starting Rate—
Max Rate—
Titration—
Goal / Monitoring—
Analgesia Reference
DrugConcentrationDose RangeMaxTitrationNotes
FentaNYL1000 mcg/100 mL NS (10 mcg/mL)25–100 mcg/hr200 mcg/hr25 mcg/hr q15minTitrate to NVPS <3. May accumulate in hepatic failure and obesity.
Ketamine500 mg/250 mL NS (2 mg/mL)0.1–0.4 mg/kg/hr1 mg/kg/hrProvider titrationAdjunct for refractory pain or opioid use history. AE: emergence reactions, hypertension.
Sedation Reference — RASS Goal: 0 to -2
DrugConcentrationDose RangeMaxTitrationKey Notes
Propofol (Diprivan)1000 mg/100 mL5–50 mcg/kg/min80 mcg/kg/min5 mcg/kg/min q5–10minRapid onset/offset. AE: hypotension, hypertriglyceridemia, PRIS.
Dexmedetomidine (Precedex)400 mcg/100 mL NS0.2–1 mcg/kg/hr1.5 mcg/kg/hr0.1 mcg/kg/hr q10minCan use in non-intubated patients. No significant respiratory drive effect. May have analgesic properties.
Midazolam (Versed)
2nd line only		50 mg/100 mL NS0.025 mg/kg/hr0.2 mg/kg/hrUp: 0.03 mg/kg/hr q10min · Down: 0.03 q60min2nd line — associated with prolonged MV, increased delirium. Accumulates in elderly/renal/hepatic/obese.
Delirium — Antipsychotics (Short-term only)
!Monitor QTc before and after. Ensure Mg2+ and K+ are repleted. Use only for significant distress or harm risk after non-pharmacologic interventions.
DrugRouteDoseOnsetHalf-lifeNotes
HaloperidolIV/IM0.5–10 mg · re-dose after 15 min · Maint: 25% of calming dose q6h PRNIV: 3–20 min · IM: 28 min14–26 hrsHighest EPS risk. QTc prolongation (IV > PO). Hepatic metabolism.
OlanzapineIM/PO/ODTIM: 2.5–10 mg (repeat q2–4h) · Max 20 mg/day · PO: 2.5–10 mg QD–BID~15 min30 hrsSedative + anticholinergic. QTc prolongation. Hepatic metabolism.
QuetiapinePO50 mg q12h (50 mg HS for insomnia) · Titrate up to 400 mg/day~1.5 hr6 hrsSedative. Hepatic adjustment. QTc prolongation.
RisperidonePO/ODT0.25–0.5 mg q12h~1 hr20 hrsLess sedative/anticholinergic. Renal + hepatic adjustment. QTc prolongation.
Policy & Opioid Stewardship
KCHC Hospital-Wide · Inpatient · ED · Outpatient
💊 IV Acetaminophen — Ofirmev® Calculator
FDA Prescribing Information · Ofirmev (Acetaminophen Injection) · KCHC Opioid Stewardship · Lana Mashiyakhova, PharmD & Esdras Obas, PharmD · 2024
Patient InputEnter weight to calculate dose
!
Duplicate therapy: Percocet, Norco, Vicodin, Tylenol PM, NyQuil all contain acetaminophen. Max daily dose applies to ALL sources combined. · Administration: Infuse over 15 minutes · For doses <1g, withdraw from vial into separate container · Do not mix with other medications · Diazepam and chlorpromazine are physically incompatible. · Ofirmev brand discontinued in US — generic acetaminophen injection now available.
ER/LA Opioid Rules — KCHC
KCHC ER/LA Opioid Clinical Forum · Sept 2021
ER/LA Opioids Are NOT for Acute Pain
  • Not superior in pain relief vs. immediate-release opioids
  • Difficult short-term dose adjustments — cannot rapidly titrate
  • Most important risk factor for subsequent prescribed opioid dependence
  • Increased risk of death — especially from postoperative use of multiple opioid preparations
  • Per ISMP: inappropriate prescribing to opioid naive patients was top medication error of 2020
Where ER/LA Opioids Are Prohibited
  • Emergency Department — do not initiate ER/LA opioids
  • Perioperative use (general surgery, orthopedic surgery) — Exception: patient already on ER/LA prior to procedure
  • Opioid naive patients
  • Do NOT store in ADC/unit stock in ED, OR, PACU
If Initiating ER/LA Opioids
  • Give 66% of total required MME per day
  • Breakthrough agent: 5–15% of total long-acting dose, PRN Q3–6H
  • Add bowel regimen + naloxone orders
  • Pain consult recommended
ED Discharge Opioid Guidelines
KCHC Opioid Treatment Guidelines · Date Effective: March 2021
GuidelineRecommendation
Duration for acute painMost patients need no more than 3 days
Starting doseLowest possible effective dose
ER/LA at dischargeAvoid initiating
Concurrent benzos + opioidsAvoid when possible — respiratory depression risk
Lost/stolen prescriptionConfirm with treating physician. Replace with 1–2 day supply only.
PMP requirementMust check registry if prescribing >5 days
Patient educationProvide info on overdose risk, storage, disposal
Inpatient Opioid Order Rules
KCHC Opioid Treatment Guidelines · Date Effective: March 2021
  • All inpatient opioid orders valid for 72 hours — re-evaluate at that time
  • Pharmacist reviews all inpatient orders before dispensing — except ED and OR (nurses may override in emergencies)
  • All orders sent to pharmacy electronically — paper only if system fails
  • Naloxone standing orders + continuous pulse ox for high-risk patients
  • Opioids stored in PYXIS or double-locked cabinets
Methadone for Pain — Special Rules
KCHC Methadone Policy · January 2025
  • Non-formulary — can only be approved by Pain Management or Palliative Care
  • High interpatient variability — half-life 6–150 hours
  • Dose titration: no more frequently than every 5–7 days
  • QTc monitoring: baseline ECG, then every 2–4 weeks
  • Analgesic effect: 8–12 hours. Start TID, then BID at steady state.
  • Methadone does NOT convert linearly — ratio increases as morphine dose increases

When to Consider LAI Over Oral
KCHC BH Guidelines · June 2017
  • History of poor or inconsistent adherence with oral therapy
  • Patient preference — does not wish to take daily pills
  • Multiple hospitalizations due to non-adherence
  • Difficulty verifying whether patient is taking oral medication
  • Patients with limited insight into their illness

Oral Aripiprazole DoseAristada DoseFrequency
10 mg/day441 mgMonthly
15 mg/day662 mg · 882 mg · 1064 mgMonthly · Q6 weeks · Q2 months
20 mg/day or higher882 mgMonthly
Aristada — Missed Dose Reference
Aristada Package Insert · 2022
Last Aristada DoseNo SupplementationSupplement with InitioReinitiate with Initio + 30 mg oral
1064 mg (Q8 wk)≤10 weeks>10 and ≤12 weeks>12 weeks
882 mg (Q6–8 wk)≤8 weeks>8 and ≤12 weeks>12 weeks
662 mg (Q6 wk)≤8 weeks>8 and ≤12 weeks>12 weeks

Abilify Maintena — Missed Dose Reference
Abilify Maintena Package Insert
Weeks Since Last InjectionAction
<5 weeksAdminister as soon as possible. Resume monthly schedule.
5–6 weeksAdminister as soon as possible. Resume monthly. No oral supplementation needed.
>6 weeksRestart oral aripiprazole × 14 days with next injection.
Invega Sustenna — Missed Dose Reference
Invega Sustenna Package Insert
ScenarioAction
Missed Day 8 initiation doseAdminister ASAP. Resume monthly thereafter.
<6 weeks since last doseAdminister next dose ASAP. Resume monthly.
6 weeks – 6 monthsAdminister missed dose in deltoid ASAP, repeat deltoid in 1 month, then resume monthly deltoid or gluteal.
>6 monthsReinitiate: 234 mg deltoid Day 1 + 156 mg deltoid Day 8.
Risperdal Consta — Missed Dose Reference
Risperdal Consta Package Insert
Weeks Since Last InjectionAction
2–3 weeks (within window)Administer next scheduled injection. No supplementation needed.
3–4 weeksAdminister ASAP. No supplementation if previously stabilized.
>4 weeksResume oral risperidone. Restart Risperdal Consta with oral overlap × 3 weeks.
Vivitrol — Clinical Warnings
Vivitrol Package Insert · March 2021
  • Opioid-free requirement: Minimum 7–10 days opioid-free before first injection. Urine screen recommended.
  • Withdrawal precipitation: Severe withdrawal if opioids on board. May require medical management.
  • Injection site reactions: Some cases required surgical intervention. Must be administered by healthcare provider.
  • Hepatotoxicity: Discontinue if signs of acute hepatitis. Monitor LFTs.
  • Post-treatment overdose risk: Reduced tolerance after stopping Vivitrol — risk of fatal overdose with previously tolerated doses. Counsel every patient.
💊 Abilify Maintena — Dose Selector (400 mg vs 300 mg vs Avoid)
Source: Abilify Maintena FDA PI · Otsuka · Answer all questions to get recommendation
1. Has the patient had adverse reactions to Abilify Maintena 400 mg?
Per PI Section 2.1: reduce to 300 mg only if adverse reactions occur with the 400 mg Maintena dose — not prior oral aripiprazole reactions
2. Is the patient a known CYP2D6 poor metabolizer?
~8% Caucasians, 3-8% Black/African American cannot metabolize CYP2D6 substrates
3. On a strong CYP2D6 inhibitor for >14 days?
Fluoxetine, paroxetine, bupropion, quinidine
4. On a strong CYP3A4 inhibitor for >14 days?
Ketoconazole, itraconazole, clarithromycin, ritonavir
5. On a CYP3A4 inducer for >14 days?
Carbamazepine, rifampin, phenytoin, St. John's Wort
Source: Abilify Maintena FDA Prescribing Information · Otsuka America Pharmaceutical
🔄 Universal Oral ↔ LAI Converter
FDA Prescribing Information · Respective Package Inserts · Anna Daglyan, PharmD, BCPP 2022 · KCHC BH Pharmacy
!
Equivalences are approximate. Clinical judgment required for all conversions. Individual patient response, tolerability, and renal function must be considered before switching. Verify with prescriber before any LAI initiation or conversion.
Conversion Direction
Oral → LAISelect oral drug + dose
LAI → OralSelect LAI agent + dose
LAI Dispensing Workflow
When R Satellite is Closed · KCHC Pharmacy Services
Amanda S. Ata, PharmD, MPH, BCPP, BCACP · Asst. Director of Pharmacy, BH · March 26, 2025
!Always verify last date of LAI administration and frequency before verifying any LAI order. Errors may have lasting effects due to long half-lives. Leave duplicate label and note at R Pharmacy for all stock retrieved.
Step 1 — Which agent?
Haloperidol & Fluphenazine Decanoate — Workflow
KCHC LAI Dispensing Memo · March 26, 2025
  • ✅ Do NOT require R-Pharmacy staff review in any area (CPEP, EOU, BHPCC, inpatient)
  • ✅ Both stocked in the Omnicell
  • Pharmacist must verify patient is due for scheduled dose and dose is clinically appropriate
  • ⛔ Do NOT check the Patient Supplied box during order verification
⏱ EPS Early Dose Check
KCHC LAI Dispensing Memo · March 26, 2025
When was the last injection?Per Mar 2025 dispensing memo
Step 2 — Where is the patient?
CPEP / ED — Patient NOT Being Admitted
Workflow Steps
  1. Confirm patient is NOT being admitted — if yes, switch to "TO BE admitted" workflow
  2. Verify last date and dose of LAI administration (provider note, CAM, or inpatient order in EPIC)
  3. Confirm dose is clinically appropriate and patient is due
  4. Have prescriber send an AMB prescription to E-Pharmacy
  5. Verify the IP order in EPIC
  6. Retrieve medication from R Pharmacy — use 340B clinic stock (preferred)
  7. When verifying: check off Patient Own Supply box in EPIC
  8. Leave note at R Pharmacy (see checklist below)
Stock Source

R-Pharmacy 340B clinic stock — preferred. You do not need to wait for E-Pharmacy coverage confirmation; call E-Pharmacy or review AMB order for paid claim status.

⛔ CPEP / ED — Patient TO BE Admitted
Action
  1. Ask provider to HOLD the order
  2. Inpatient primary team will decide whether to continue treatment after admission
  3. Do NOT dispense until patient is admitted and inpatient team has reviewed
EPIC Action

No action in EPIC until patient is admitted and inpatient team places order.

Inpatient — Admitted Patient
Workflow Steps
  1. Provider must indicate medication is required for discharge
  2. Verify last date and dose of LAI administration
  3. Confirm order is clinically appropriate
  4. Retrieve from R Pharmacy — use Inpatient Free Trial Program stock (look for colored sticker dot on box)
  5. When verifying: check off Patient Own Supply box in EPIC
  6. Leave note + duplicate label at R Pharmacy (see checklist below)
Important Notes
  • For Risperdal Consta and non-standard Sustenna strengths (117 mg, 78 mg): do NOT check Patient Own Supply box
  • For Invega Sustenna: check renal function and follow package insert dosing
  • Free Trial Program stock includes: Sustenna 234/156 mg, Aristada 441/662/882/1064 mg + Initio 675 mg, Maintena 300/400 mg, Uzedy 100/125/200/250 mg · Amanda, BH Asst. Director · KCHC Staff Meeting 3/31/2026
⚠ Invega Sustenna 234 mg or 156 mg only:
Include the serial number on the duplicate label so R Pharmacy can obtain a replacement.
Remove the serial number and lot number from the box and affix to the duplicate label.
Also include prescriber / doctor name on the affixed label.
Serial number requirement: KCHC LAI Dispensing Memo · March 26, 2025 · Prescriber name: KCHC operational requirement
PCC — Outpatient Clinics
Workflow Steps
  1. Verify the CAM (Clinic Administered Medication) order is in EPIC
  2. Confirm dose is due and is the same dose as last injection
  3. Check for duplicate orders (Q28 days, Q2 weeks) — clear before dispensing
  4. If RN already has the medication: verify appropriateness, then RN removes from Omnicell after verification
  5. If medication needs to come from R-Pharmacy: review AMB order for E-Pharmacy fill; check Patient Own Supply box if filled by E-Pharmacy
  6. Leave note at R Pharmacy if retrieving stock (see checklist below)
📋 R Pharmacy Note Checklist
KCHC LAI Dispensing Memo · March 26, 2025

When retrieving any LAI from R Pharmacy, leave a note + duplicate label including:

R Building Filter — EPIC

KC ADULT BEHAV HLTH · KC Primary Care · KC Primary Care in BH · KC PS 114 HLTH BH · KC PARTIAL HOSP ADULT · KC CHILD AND ADOL BH

QTc Risk Assessment
QTc Prolongation & Psychotropic Medications · February 2021
Amanda Eloma, PharmD, BCPP · KCHC · February 2021 · Adapted from APA Schizophrenia Guidelines 2020

Weight (kg)
SCr (mg/dL)
Sex
CrCl = [(140-Age) × Weight / (72 × SCr)] × 0.85 if female · Cockcroft-Gault
QTc Risk Scorer
Azithromycin, ciprofloxacin, levofloxacin, fluconazole, metoclopramide, etc.
QTc Risk Assessment
QTc Value—
QTc Status—
Drug Risk Category—
Overall Risk Level—
Recommended Action—
Normal QTc Values
SexNormalBorderlineProlongedHigh Risk (TdP)
Male<470 ms470–500 ms500–519 ms>500 ms — risk markedly increased
Female<480 ms480–500 ms500–519 ms>500 ms — risk markedly increased
Psychotropic QTc Risk Table
CategoryAntipsychoticsAntidepressantsOther
Known Risk of TdPHaloperidol · Pimozide · Thioridazine · Chlorpromazine · Droperidol · Perphenazine · PromethazineCitalopram · Escitalopram · Venlafaxine · Mirtazapine · Desipramine · Imipramine · Nortriptyline · TrimipramineMethadone · Donepezil
Possible Risk of TdPAripiprazole · Asenapine · Clozapine · Iloperidone · Lurasidone · Paliperidone · Olanzapine · Quetiapine · Risperidone · ZiprasidoneTrazodone · Fluoxetine · Paroxetine · Sertraline · Amitriptyline · Clomipramine · Doxepin · FluvoxamineLithium · Pimavanserin
Conditional Risk of TdP——Atomoxetine · Buprenorphine · Metoclopramide · Diphenhydramine · Hydroxyzine · Amantadine · Loperamide
Antipsychotic QTc Prolongation (ms)
AntipsychoticApproximate QTc Prolongation (ms)Risk Level
Aripiprazole-1 to -4 (may shorten QTc)+
LurasidoneMinimal+
Clozapine~10++
Olanzapine2–6.5++
Paliperidone2–4++
Risperidone3.5–10++
Quetiapine6–15++
Haloperidol7–15++ (IV > PO)
Ziprasidone16–21+++
Pimozide19+++
Thioridazine33–41+++
ChlorpromazineSignificant+++
IloperidoneSignificant+++
TDM & Drug Level Monitoring
Lithium · Valproic Acid · Carbamazepine · KCHC BH Guidelines · June 2017
KCHC BH Departmental Manual · Date Effective: June 2017 · ⚠ Update pending
🧮 CrCl Quick Calc (Cockcroft-Gault) ▼
Age
Weight (kg)
SCr (mg/dL)
Sex
CrCl = [(140-Age) × Weight / (72 × SCr)] × 0.85 if female · Cockcroft-Gault
Lithium Level Interpreter
Draw 8–12 hours post dose (true trough)
Lithium Level Interpretation
Level—
Target Range—
Status—
Clinical Action—
Next Level—
Valproate Level Interpreter
Valproate Level Interpretation
Level—
Target Range—
Status—
Clinical Action—
Lithium Reference
Level (mEq/L)IndicationClinical SignificanceAction
0.6–1.2Acute ManiaTherapeuticContinue. Level q4–7 days initially.
0.8–1.0Bipolar MaintenanceTherapeuticContinue. Level q3 months year 1, then q6 months.
0.5–0.8ElderlyTherapeutic (lower end)Continue at lower end of range.
>1.2AnyAbove therapeuticRepeat level. Bring below 1.2. Check every 4–6h if persists.
>1.3AnyCritical valueReport to nursing and psychiatrist per critical lab policy.
≥1.5AnyToxic — GI/tremorContact Medical Director.
2–2.25AnyToxic — confusion/somnolenceHold lithium. IV fluids. Assess for hemodialysis.
>2.5AnySevere toxicity — seizures/deathTransfer for IV fluids. Consider hemodialysis.
≥4.0AnyLife-threateningHemodialysis required.
Valproic Acid Reference Ranges
IndicationTherapeutic RangeToxicity Threshold
Bipolar Mania85–125 mcg/mL (trough)>125 mcg/mL — toxicity risk increases
Epilepsy50–100 mcg/mL>100–150 mcg/mL — toxicity may occur
Thrombocytopenia thresholdFemales: >110 mcg/mL · Males: >135 mcg/mL
Carbamazepine Reference Ranges
ParameterValue
Therapeutic level4–12 mcg/mL
Toxic concentration>15 mcg/mL
With other anticonvulsants4–8 mcg/mL (narrower range)
Starting dose (bipolar)400 mg/day in 2 divided doses — titrate by 200 mg/day increments · Max: 1600 mg/day
Level monitoringAfter week 1 → weekly during adjustment → monthly x3 months → q6 months when stable
Psychotropic Guidelines
KCHC BH Departmental Manual · June 2017
KCHC BH Departmental Manual · Date Effective: June 2017 · ⚠ Update pending — use current version when available
🧮 CrCl Quick Calc (Cockcroft-Gault) ▼
Age
Weight (kg)
SCr (mg/dL)
Sex
CrCl = [(140-Age) × Weight / (72 × SCr)] × 0.85 if female · Cockcroft-Gault
Metabolic Monitoring TrackerAtypical antipsychotics — KCHC BH Jun 2017
Metabolic Monitoring Schedule
Antipsychotic Baseline Workup — Required
KCHC BH Departmental Manual · June 2017
  • AIMS (Abnormal Involuntary Movement Scale) — within first month
  • Renal and hepatic function tests
  • CBC
  • Physical exam — within first month
  • Beta-hCG — all women of childbearing age
  • Fasting lipid profile and fasting blood glucose / HgbA1c
  • BMI, personal/family history of obesity, waist circumference
  • Eye exam — cataracts check q6 months while on medication
  • EKG — for QTc-prolonging agents and patients with cardiac history
Clozapine — Special Monitoring Requirements
KCHC BH Departmental Manual · June 2017
  • Indicated for treatment-resistant schizophrenia and schizoaffective disorder only
  • Mandatory ANC monitoring — per manufacturer guidelines (agranulocytosis risk)
  • All patients must be registered at clozapinerems.com
  • Monitor hemodynamic status and persistent tachycardia (benign vs myocarditis)
  • RN must contact provider for HR >110 bpm per patient order
  • Monitoring: Mental status · ECG · WBC/ANC · Vital signs · Fasting lipids · Fasting glucose · HgbA1c · LFTs · BMI · Weight (baseline, 4wk, 8wk, 12wk, then quarterly)
  • Monitor bowel movements routinely — constipation risk
Dual Antipsychotic Criteria — KCHC HBIPS
KCHC BH Departmental Manual · June 2017

Dual antipsychotic use requires pharmacy review. Any ONE of the following must be met:

  • Failure of 3 antipsychotics in the past at adequate dose and duration (must be specifically documented)
  • Patient is undergoing slow taper of second antipsychotic to prevent decompensation
  • Augmentation to clozapine
EPS Prevention
KCHC BH Departmental Manual · June 2017
  • Do NOT start anticholinergics prophylactically unless EPS has already developed
  • High-risk patients (age <30, African-American males): short-term anticholinergic use may be acceptable during antipsychotic induction
Benzodiazepine / Hypnotic Extended Use
KCHC BH Departmental Manual · June 2017
  • Extended treatment (>30 days) discouraged except under specifically justifiable circumstances
  • Prescribing for >3 consecutive months requires a progress note documenting why it must be continued vs. alternative approaches (e.g., SSRIs for anxiety)
Hot Weather Warning — All Psychotropic Patients
KCHC BH Departmental Manual · June 2017
  • Patients on antipsychotics, antidepressants, antiparkinsonian agents (except amantadine), or antihistamine-sedatives are at greatly increased risk for heat exhaustion and heat stroke
  • Risk is especially high in elderly or medically compromised patients
  • Lithium patients: warn about toxicity risk from inadequate fluid intake, dietary changes, or abrupt salt restriction in hot weather
  • All patients: avoid prolonged exposure · maintain hydration
💊 Lorazepam Max Dose — KCHC Clinical Reference
Lana Mashiyakhova, PharmD & Esdras Obas, PharmD · Comprehensive Hospital Pharmacy Practice · 2024 · AGS Beers Criteria 2019
!
Beers Criteria (AGS 2019): Lorazepam should be avoided in older adults — risk of cognitive impairment, delirium, falls, and fractures is significantly increased. Use only when clinically unavoidable. If used, apply these dose caps and document fall risk assessment.
Patient TypeMax Single DoseClinical Note
👴 Elderly / ≥65 years1 mg maxBeers: AVOID — if unavoidable, use lowest effective dose · Document fall risk · Monitor closely for paradoxical agitation
👤 Standard Adult2 mg maxStandard per KCHC practice · Include hold parameters for respiratory depression
💪 Heavy / Large patient3 mgClinical judgment required · Weight-based consideration · Monitor respiratory rate and sedation level
⚠ All lorazepam orders must include: indication · hold parameters (RR <12, O₂ sat <90%, SBP <90) · fall risk documentation · Olanzapine + Lorazepam injection: space 2 hours apart per KCHC BH policy
LAI Safety & EPIC Workflow
Medication Errors — BH Long Acting Injectables · July 29, 2020
Amanda Eloma, PharmD, BCPP · KCHC Clinical Forum · July 29, 2020
!LAI errors are not typically urgent but may have lasting effects due to long half-lives. Always confirm last date of administration before verifying any LAI order.
How to Find Last LAI Injection in EPIC
Step 1 — Search for Specific LAI

Chart Review → Meds Tab → Double Click "Date" to filter most recent → Click "Load All Records Now" → Uncheck "Current Meds Only"

Step 2 — Confirm Date of Last Injection

Filter by specific medication → Click to expand field → Check each box to select medication → Select specific medication → Check off "Preview" → Review Administration Times in Preview box on right

If further clarification needed: review "Complete Scheduler History" — dates "Given" can be adjusted and documented.

Step 3 — Confirm via EPIC Search

Use magnifying glass to search specific words. Note: an order that says "Discontinue" may still have been administered (Given).

EPIC Action Item Definitions
Action ItemMeaningWas Medication Given?
DueMedication order is activeNo — not yet administered
GivenMedication was administeredYes
Not GivenMedication was NOT administeredNo
Canceled EntryAdministration was canceledNo
HandoffDocumentation was handed off — review notes for MARVerify in notes
Common LAI Errors to Avoid
  • Not checking the last date of administration before verifying order
  • Assuming a "Discontinued" order means the medication was not given — always check administration history
  • Missing orders written as ongoing with multiple administration times (e.g., Q14days, Q28days) — expand Complete Scheduler History
  • Administering too early — risks EPS accumulation with first-generation antipsychotics
  • Dispensing from wrong stock (clinic vs. inpatient free trial program)
  • Failing to leave duplicate label/note at R Pharmacy when retrieving stock
  • Not checking Patient Own Supply box in EPIC when required
OUD Injectables — Sublocade (XR-BUP)
Extended Release Buprenorphine · KCHC BH Policy June 2023 · KCHC Dispensing Memo Nov 2022
Source: KCHC Extended Release Buprenorphine (Sublocade®): Process and Procedure for Use · BHS Policy · Effective: June 2023 · Authors: Jennifer Mark MD, Amanda Eloma PharmD BCPP, Annahit Daglyan PharmD BCPP · Approved: Zoraya Pod RPh MPA, Director Pharmacy Services
⚠SUBLOCADE REMS: Never dispense directly to patient. Must be dispensed to the administering medical provider (MD, NP, DO, PA) only. Pharmacy/healthcare setting must be enrolled in Sublocade REMS program.
Sublocade (XR-BUP) Clinical Overview
ParameterKCHC Protocol
IndicationModerate to severe Opioid Use Disorder (OUD)
Drug classExtended-release buprenorphine injection (Schedule III controlled substance)
Pre-treatment requirementPatient must be dose-stabilized on 8–24 mg/day transmucosal buprenorphine (TM-BUP) for a minimum of 7 days before initiating XR-BUP, unless clinically appropriate to initiate sooner
Dosing300 mg SQ × first 2 months, then 100 mg SQ monthly thereafter (dosing may vary with certain clinical scenarios)
FrequencyEvery 28 days (range: 26–30 days)
RouteSubcutaneous (SQ) — abdominal area, rotating quadrants clockwise each month
Administration restrictionNEVER administer IV, IM, or intradermally — serious harm or death could result if administered intravenously
Room temperatureAllow at least 15 minutes outside refrigerator before injection. Do not open foil pouch until patient has arrived. May be stored at room temperature up to 7 days — discard if >7 days at room temp
Administered byMedical provider (MD/DO/PA/NP) trained via educational video or by experienced provider. Prescribing and administration overseen by the Addiction Medicine Team
Supplemental bupeSublingual buprenorphine films or tablets may be used to supplement the Sublocade injection if withdrawal symptoms occur
CounselingPatients receiving XR-BUP receive the same substance use counseling and support opportunities as other OUD patients
Procurement & Dispensing Workflow
StepAction
Option A — Specialty DistributorPharmacy procures directly from specialty distributor after REMS registration. Delivered to KCHC Pharmacy — stored in locked refrigerator via ADC. Provider sends script to KCHC Pharmacy. Pharmacy fills per NYS controlled substance guidelines and dispenses directly to HCP. Chain of custody maintained via Sublocade Dispensation Log. Provider signs for XR-BUP via EMR (POS)
Option B — Specialty PharmacyXR-BUP may be prescribed directly to a specialty pharmacy outside KCHC. Pharmacy fills per NYS guidelines and provides XR-BUP directly to HCP
CAM OrderPrescriber enters a Clinic Administered Medication (CAM) order for administration. Administration documented in eMAR by provider
EPIC verification note“Sublocade must NOT be dispensed directly to the patient, per REMS requirements. Sublocade must be dispensed directly to medical provider (MD, NP, DO, PA) administering.” May also be given to BH Pharmacy staff to hand off to provider
Billing — IP/CAM ordersWhen verifying Sublocade orders for inpatient (IP) or clinic administered (CAM) administration, ensure the “patient’s own medication supply” box is checked to prevent double billing. If error occurs, go back and re-verify
Continuity of careA script (AMB) is sent to E building Pharmacy prior to discharge to fulfill PA and insurance requirements. E building Pharmacy medication is picked up by administering provider and given directly to patient
Disposal of Inventory — No-Show / Treatment Discontinuation
  • If a patient is a no-show or no longer wishes to participate in treatment, the medication must be taken out of inventory and destroyed
  • Specialty pharmacy source: destroy with a reverse distributor within 14 days
  • Hospital specialty distributor source: return to pharmacy stock
KCHC Extended Release Buprenorphine (Sublocade®): Process and Procedure for Use · BHS Policy · June 2023 · KCHC Dispensing & Verifying Memo · November 2022
Dual Long-Acting Antipsychotic Utilization Request Form
KCHC BH Pharmacy Review Criteria · KCHC BHS Departmental Manual & Staff Meeting 3/31/2026 · Amanda S. Ata PharmD MPH BCPP BCACP
Source: KCHC Kings County Hospital Center Policy and Procedure Manual — Behavioral Health Services · Guidelines for the Use of Specific Medications (Dual Antipsychotic Utilization) · KCHC Pharmacy Staff Meeting 3/31/2026
ℹ All dual antipsychotic cases are reviewed by the Pharmacy Department to ensure appropriate use. KCHC BH follows HBIPS criteria. Note: The HBIPS-5 Joint Commission dual antipsychotic measure was discontinued in 2024 — KCHC continues to apply HBIPS-based internal policy and criteria for justifying dual antipsychotic therapy.
✓ Copied to clipboard
⚡ Interactive Criteria Checker
Answer all questions to get an approval recommendation. Static reference tables remain below.
Is this an initial or continuing dual LAI request?
A. HBIPS Criteria -- at least ONE must be met
B. Initial Dual LAI -- ALL must be met
C. Continuing Dual LAI -- ALL must be met
A. Dual Antipsychotic Utilization — HBIPS Criteria (at least ONE must be met)
#Criterion
1Patient has failed at least 3 antipsychotics in the past at maximally tolerated doses and adequate duration — each trial's dose, duration, and adherence must be specifically documented
2The second antipsychotic is being tapered to prevent decompensation (transition/crossover taper)
3The second antipsychotic is being used as augmentation to clozapine
B. Dual Long-Acting Antipsychotic Injection (LAI) — Initial Utilization (ALL must be met)
#Criterion — Required / Must Be Met
1Patient has failed at least 3 antipsychotic trials at maximally tolerated doses and adequate duration — dose, duration, and adherence clearly documented
2Patient has been trialed on at least 2 LAIs as monotherapy at maximally tolerated doses with documented clinical efficacy
3Patient has failed clozapine at a maximally tolerated dose and adequate duration, OR is a poor candidate for clozapine due to high risk of non-adherence to monitoring or required blood work
4Patient is at high risk for re-hospitalization — OR has a recent history (<12 months) of multiple readmissions (>4) primarily due to non-adherence to the current regimen
5Patient has previously received the same two antipsychotics concurrently with documented clinical efficacy and tolerability
6Patient has been stabilized during current admission on the oral combination of the two planned LAIs
6aBefore conversion to LAI formulations: patient has a documented EKG, AIMS assessment, and pertinent CMP labs (e.g., for paliperidone or risperidone formulations) while receiving both antipsychotics during the current admission
C. Continuing Dual LAI Therapy (ALL must be met)
#Criterion — Required / Must Be Met
1Patient was previously stabilized on a similar dual LAI regimen in a past admission, with current readmission primarily due to non-adherence to medication
2Patient has been stabilized during current admission on the oral combination of the two planned LAIs
2aBefore conversion to LAI formulations: patient has a documented EKG, AIMS assessment, and pertinent CMP labs (e.g., for paliperidone or risperidone formulations) while receiving both antipsychotics during the current admission
Required Documentation (Form Fields)
  • Patient Name & MRN
  • Date of Birth & Unit
  • Prescribing Provider
  • Requested Dual Antipsychotic Regimen — Antipsychotic 1 and Antipsychotic 2
  • Applicable criteria checked (Section A, B, or C above)
  • Additional Clinical Justification / Notes
  • Prescriber Signature & Date
  • Supervising Physician Signature
  • Pharmacy Approval — Signature
KCHC BHS Departmental Manual — Guidelines for the Use of Specific Medications · Dual Antipsychotic Utilization · KCHC Pharmacy Staff Meeting 3/31/2026 · Amanda S. Ata PharmD MPH BCPP BCACP, Assistant Director of Pharmacy, Behavioral Health
IV Fluid Conservation
National Shortage · NYC H+H Enterprise · Effective October 17, 2024
IV Fluid Conservation Memo · NYC Health + Hospitals · Effective: October 17, 2024
!NYC H+H is implementing conservation strategies for IV fluids (Sodium Chloride, Lactated Ringers, Dextrose, Sterile Water) due to the national shortage caused by Hurricane Helene. Remain alert for updates.
Active Conservation Strategies
IV Fluid Management
  • Standardize IV fluid hang time up to 96 hours — exception applies to pharmacy-prepared products (follow BUD)
  • Reserve IV fluids for patients with significant volume depletion unable to tolerate oral (PO) hydration
  • Reserve rapid large volume resuscitation for patients with life-threatening conditions only
  • Carefully consider and reassess need for invasive hemodynamic monitoring
  • Limit IV fluid orders to a 24-hour duration and require order renewal
  • Frequently reassess continuous IV fluid orders
Medication Conservation
  • BPA will prompt for all new IV fluid orders to recommend switching to oral (PO) hydration
  • Enforce PO medication formulations when equivalent — refer to Enterprise IV to PO Conversion Guideline
  • Enforce IV Push over IVPB medications — refer to Enterprise IVPB to IV Push Conversion Guideline
Operational Strategies
  • Preserve usable IV bags during patient transport
  • Avoid discontinuing and reordering IV fluids during transitions of care unless clinically necessary
  • Avoid discarding unopened IV fluids in working areas — return to stock
  • Frequently reassess "keep vein open" (KVO) orders
  • Match the ordered bag volume to the intended infusion volume
  • Avoid opening and "pre-spiking" fluid bags
Decision Hierarchy — IV Fluid Use
PriorityActionReference
1stConvert to PO medication/hydration if eligibleEnterprise IV → PO Guideline
2ndConvert IVPB to IV Push if eligibleEnterprise IVPB → IVP Guideline
3rdContinue IV only if clinically required and above not feasibleReassess every 24h
IV Push Reference
IVPB → IVP Conversion · IVP Guardrails · IVP Policy · NYC H+H Enterprise

NYC H+H Enterprise · Effective: October 2024 · Last Review: October 2024
!This list is not exhaustive. Clinicians should always verify appropriateness for IV push based on individual patient needs and facility-specific policies. If a medication is not listed, it may still be safely given via IV push.

Dexamethasone10 mg0.5–10 mg1 minuteUndilutedFlushing, tingling, perineal burning, itching
Diphenhydramine50 mg25–50 mg2 min · Max 25 mg/minUndilutedEuphoria
Famotidine20 mg20 mg2 min · Max 10 mg/minUndilutedN/A
Folic Acid1 mg1 mg1 minuteUndilutedN/A
Furosemide100 mg20–100 mg1–3 min · Max 40 mg/minUndilutedOtotoxicity
Hydrocortisone Na Succinate200 mg25–100 mg1 minuteReconstitute as directed; no additional dilutionN/A
Hydromorphone2 mg0.2–2 mg2–3 minutesReconstitute as directed; no additional dilutionRespiratory depression
Levetiracetam4,500 mg (1,500 mg × 3)Up to 1,500 mg per dose over 3 min · Repeat q5min × 3 doses max3 min · Max 500 mg/minUndilutedInjection site reactions, HR and BP changes
Methylprednisolone Na Succinate125 mg20–125 mg3–5 minutesReconstitute as directed; no additional dilutionDoses >250 mg: cardiac arrhythmia, circulatory collapse, flushing, tingling
Morphine5 mg2–5 mg4–5 minutesUndilutedSkeletal muscle rigidity, impaired ventilation, respiratory distress/arrest
Pantoprazole80 mg (40 mg × 2)40–80 mg · GI Bleed: 40 mg IVP × 22–4 min · Max 40 mg over 2 minReconstitute each 40 mg with 10 mL NSN/A
Phenobarbital260 mg30–260 mg3–5 min · Max 60 mg/minUndilutedRespiratory depression, apnea, vasodilation with fall in BP
Thiamine200 mg100–200 mg5 minutesUndilutedN/A

Thiamine100 mg100 mg5 minUndilutedVitamin B1 supplementRoutine monitoring
IVP Policy — General Care Units
Intravenous Push Injections in the General Care Units · September 2021
NYC H+H / Kings County · Patient Care Services · Date Effective: February 2021 · Revised: September 2021
Policy Statement

Registered Professional Nurses who demonstrate competency in IV therapy managing peripheral and/or central accesses will administer IV push injections known to have minimal adverse effects.

Orders for IV push injections must be entered in CPOE and must specify: drug name, concentration, route, maximum dosage, rate of push (slow vs rapid), and monitoring precautions.

Scope

Registered nurses in Medical/Surgical, Emergency, Critical Care, Peri-Operative, and Maternal/Child nursing care units.

RN Procedure — Step by Step
  • Verify order for IV push medications — ensure familiarization
  • Verify IV placement and patency
  • Perform necessary focused assessment including vital signs
  • Refer to IV push injection list to ensure ordered injection is approved
  • Consult Nurse Manager and Pharmacy for drugs not on the list
  • Prepare and administer IV push injection as ordered
  • Observe patient's reaction and intervene accordingly
  • Document administration, related intervention, and patient response via BCMA
Labeling Requirements
  • IV push medications must be properly labeled with: patient name, date, time of preparation, medication name and concentration
  • Exception: labeling not required if prepared at bedside for immediate administration
  • If RN cannot administer an IV push medication — escalate to nurse manager and provider
2021 Approved IVP Drug List — Reference
Policy Effective: September 2021 — See 2025 Guardrails (Exhibit A) for updated list
DrugMax DoseRateConcentrationMonitoring
Dexamethasone10 mg1 min4 mg/mL or 10 mg/mL (undiluted)Routine
Diphenhydramine25 mg2–3 min50 mg/mL (undiluted)Over-sedation, respiratory depression with opioids
Famotidine40 mg2–3 min10 mg/mL (undiluted)Routine vital signs
Furosemide40 mg1–2 min10 mg/mL (undiluted)BP
Dextrose 50%25 g2–3 min0.5 g/mL (undiluted)Fingerstick glucose 15 min post injection
Heparin5,000 units≤30 sec—Bleeding potential, aPTT, platelets
Hydrocortisone——50 mg/mL (undiluted)—
Insulin Regular2–10 units≤30 sec100 units/mL (undiluted)Blood glucose, mental status
Ketorolac30 mg1–2 min30 mg/mL (undiluted)Routine vital signs
Methylprednisolone125 mg2–3 minMultiple concentrations (reconstituted)Blood pressure
Metoclopramide10 mg1–2 min5 mg/mL (undiluted)Acute dystonic reaction, BP, temperature, muscle rigidity, tremor
Morphine1–4 mg2–3 minMultiple concentrations (undiluted)BP, respiration
Ondansetron8 mg4–5 min2 mg/mL (undiluted)Routine vital signs
Pantoprazole40 mg2–3 minDilute with 10 mL NS (4 mg/mL)Routine vital signs

Enteral Nutrition (EN)
Administration and Hydration Guidelines · December 2022
NYC H+H / Kings County · Food and Nutrition Services · Date Effective: December 2020 · Last Reviewed: December 2022
Free Water Requirements CalculatorStandard: 30 mL/kg/day
Jevity ≈ 82% · Most standard formulas ≈ 80–85%
EN Free Water Calculation
Total Daily Water Requirement—
EN Formula Volume (24h)—
Water from Formula—
Additional Free Water Needed—
Suggested Flush Order—
Restriction Note—
EN Administration Policy
Food and Nutrition Services · December 2022
  • Provider places order for continuous EN including free water for 24-hour period based on patient fluid requirements
  • Standard fluid requirement: 30 mL/kg/day — notable exceptions: CHF, ascites, renal failure, fluid restriction
  • Dietitian calculates free water requirements (total water requirement minus water content of formula)
  • Dietitian documents recommendations and discusses with care team
  • Provider writes order for supplemental hydration
  • Nurse implements formula and water order — documents in medical record
IV Admixture / Sterile Compounding
Stock Solution Calculator · Max Concentration Checker · Compounding Recipe Generator
KCHC Pharmacy · Sterile Compounding Reference
!For use in clean room compounding by qualified pharmacy personnel only. Always verify final product calculations independently. Peripheral route has strict concentration limits — check before compounding.
IV Admixture CalculatorEnter target specs → get exact stock volumes
Peripheral max: 12.5% · Central: up to 35%
Additives (leave blank if not needed)
Compounding Recipe
Max Final Concentration — Peripheral vs Central
!Exceeding peripheral concentration limits causes chemical phlebitis and tissue necrosis. Always verify route before dispensing.
ComponentPeripheral MaxCentral MaxNotes
Dextrose≤12.5%Up to 35%Higher concentration causes osmotic phlebitis peripherally
KCl≤10 mEq/100 mL≤20 mEq/100 mLNever give undiluted · Cardiac toxicity risk
KPhosNot recommended≤15 mmol/100 mLHigh osmolality — central line preferred
Calcium Gluconate≤100 mg/mL (10%)Up to 200 mg/mLExtravasation causes tissue necrosis
NaCl≤0.9% (154 mEq/L)Up to 3% (513 mEq/L)Hypertonic saline via central only
MgSO4≤20 mg/mL (2%)Up to 50 mg/mLDilute well peripherally
Amino Acids (TPN)≤3.5%Up to 10%+PPN typically ≤900 mOsm/L total
Total Osmolarity (TPN/PPN)≤900 mOsm/LNo strict limitPPN must stay ≤900 mOsm/L for peripheral administration
Example — D12.5% NS with Heparin 250 mL
KCHC Pharmacy · Reference Admixture Recipe
IngredientConcentrationVolume (mL)
Dextrose 50% (D50W)500 mg/mL40.05 mL
Dextrose 5% (D5W)50 mg/mL200.20 mL
Sodium Chloride 4 mEq/mL4 mEq/mL9.63 mL
Heparin 1,000 units/mL1,000 units/mL0.125 mL
Final Volume250 mL
Final Solution Specifications
  • Dextrose: 12.5% · NaCl: 38.5 mEq total · Heparin: 125 units
  • Route: Peripheral acceptable (dextrose ≤12.5%)
  • Mix thoroughly · Verify final volume = 250 mL · Adjust with D5W if needed
IV Bag Compounder
Alligation · Electrolyte Additives · Drug Additives · Precipitation Check · Final Recipe
KCHC Pharmacy · D12.5% Admixture Recipe · Institutional Document · FDA Safety Alert: Ca/Phos Precipitation · April 1994 · USP 797 · Effective November 2023
!
For bench compounding only — NOT for CAPS/TPN orders. All preparations require independent pharmacist verification before dispensing. Verify Ca/Phos compatibility with Trissel's before preparation. NaCl 3% / 23.4% restricted to ICU and ED per KCHC High Alert policy.
📐 Module 1 — Base Solution AlligationMix two solutions to hit target concentration
⚗️ Module 2 — Electrolyte AdditivesVolume calculated from stock concentration
💊 Module 3 — Drug AdditivesHeparin · Regular Insulin · Compatible drugs
—
—

Clinical verification tool for PN orders sent to CAPS. Does not replace pharmacist judgment. Two independent pharmacist calculations required before submission. All orders must be received by pharmacy by 11:00 AM per KCHC policy. Consult dietitian before initiating PN.

Furosemide Continuous Infusion
Pediatric / Neonatal · Weight-Based · See Peds Tab for Full Calculator
Source: KCHC Pharmacy Staff Meeting · March 31, 2026
⚠ Medication Error Alert: Provider ordered furosemide 1 mg/kg/hr × 24 hrs continuous infusion instead of intended 1 mg/kg IV push one-time. Maximum CI dose is 0.4 mg/kg/hr — do not confuse IV push and continuous infusion orders in EPIC.
Quick Reference — Furosemide Continuous Infusion (Peds/Neonatal)
ParameterValue
Starting dose0.1 to 0.2 mg/kg/hour
Titration+0.1 mg/kg/hr every 12–24h based on urine output
Maximum0.4 mg/kg/hour
Product (EPIC)Furosemide Infusion 1 mg/mL (Neonate/Pediatric)

has successfully completed the NYC H+H / Kings County Hospital Center Pharmacy Services Anticoagulation Competency Assessment, demonstrating knowledge of evidence-based guidelines for anticoagulation therapy including Warfarin, UFH, Enoxaparin, DOACs, HIT/Argatroban, and Reversal Agents.

Antidotes & Toxicology
Common Antidotes and Reversals · Kings County Hospital · July 2022
Rebecca Chu, PharmD, BCPS · KCHC Internal Medicine · Updated July 2022
!For poisoning emergencies call Poison Control: 1-800-222-1222 (24/7). Dosing in adults unless otherwise specified. Always verify with clinical pharmacist for pediatric cases.
⚡ Antidote Dose Calculator
Select a toxin to generate weight-based doses. Reference tables remain below.
Master Antidote Quick Reference
KCHC Antidotes & Reversals Cheat Sheet · July 2022
Drug / ToxinAntidoteAdult DoseKey Notes
Acetaminophen
Tylenol OD		N-Acetylcysteine (NAC)
IV or PO		IV: 150 mg/kg over 1h → 50 mg/kg over 4h → 100 mg/kg over 16h
PO: 140 mg/kg load → 70 mg/kg Q4h × 17 doses		Best within 8h of ingestion. Check level at 4h, plot on Rumack-Matthew nomogram.
Anticholinergics
Antihistamines, antipsychotics, TCAs		Physostigmine0.5–2 mg IV Q10–30 min until responseDo not infuse faster than 1 mg/min. Monitor for cholinergic rebound.
Aspirin / SalicylatesSodium Bicarbonate
(urine alkalinization)		1–2 mEq/kg IV bolus → 100–150 mEq in 1L D5W infusionGoal urine pH >7.5. Continue until salicylate level <30 mg/dL.
Benzodiazepines
Alprazolam, lorazepam, midazolam		Flumazenil0.2–1 mg IV Q1 min until awakeOnly for benzo-naïve single-agent OD. Risk of seizures in chronic users or polypharmacy.
Beta-Blockers
Metoprolol, propranolol, carvedilol		Glucagon
High-dose Insulin + Dextrose		Glucagon: 3–5 mg IV over 1–2 min; infusion 2–5 mg/hr
Insulin: 0.5–1 unit/kg IV + D25 25g; infusion 0.5–1 unit/kg/hr		Check glucose Q30 min until stable, then Q1h. Monitor K⁺ and Mg²⁺.
Calcium Channel Blockers
Diltiazem, verapamil, amlodipine		Glucagon
Insulin + Dextrose
Parenteral Calcium		Glucagon: 3–5 mg IV → 2–5 mg/hr
Insulin + D25 (same as BB)
CaCl₂ 10%: 20 mg/kg over 5–10 min (max 1–2 g)		CaCl₂ has 3× elemental Ca vs gluconate. Extravasation risk with CaCl₂.
CyanideHydroxocobalamin (Cyanokit)
OR Sodium nitrate + Sodium thiosulfate		Hydroxocobalamin: 5g IV × 1 (may repeat × 1)
Sodium nitrate 300 mg IV + Sodium thiosulfate 12.5g IV		Do NOT use sodium nitrate/thiosulfate if concurrent CO poisoning. Hydroxocobalamin preferred — better safety profile.
DigoxinDigoxin Immune Fab
(DigiFAB)		Acute: 10 vials IV empirically, repeat × 10 if needed
Chronic: 1–2 vials, repeat PRN		Each vial binds ~0.5 mg digoxin. Digoxin levels unreliable for ~7 days post-FAB. Monitor K⁺.
Ethylene Glycol / Methanol
Antifreeze, windshield fluid		Fomepizole (Antizol)
+ Thiamine (EG) / Folinic acid (MeOH)		Fomepizole: 15 mg/kg IV → 10 mg/kg Q12h × 4 → 15 mg/kg Q12h until level <20
Thiamine / B6 / Folinic acid: 100 mg IV Q6h		Easily removed by HD — redose fomepizole every 4h during dialysis.
Heparin (UFH)Protamine Sulfate1 mg per 100 units heparin (time-adjusted) · Max 50 mgIf 30–60 min elapsed: 0.5–0.75 mg per 100 units. If >2 hrs: 0.25–0.375 mg per 100 units. Check aPTT 15 min after.
LMWH (Enoxaparin)Protamine Sulfate<8h: 1 mg per 1 mg enoxaparin · 8–12h: 0.5 mg per 1 mg · >12h: may not be required · Max 50 mgDoes NOT fully neutralize anti-Xa activity. If patient not bleeding, consider withholding.
LithiumHydration + HemodialysisHydration: 0.9% NS preferred
HD: reduces half-life 12–27h → 3.6–5.7h		Toxic: >1.5 mEq/L with symptoms. HD for levels ≥4 mEq/L or severe symptoms.
Opioids
Fentanyl, oxycodone, morphine		Naloxone (Narcan)0.04–2 mg IV, repeat Q2–3 min · Infusion: 0.25–6.25 mg/hrDuration only 30–90 min — must outlast the opioid. Start low in opioid-dependent patients to avoid precipitated withdrawal.
Serotonin SyndromeCyproheptadine (Periactin)12 mg PO load → 2 mg Q2h · Maintenance: 4–8 mg Q6h · Max 32 mg/dayRemove offending agents first. Benzos for agitation. Cyproheptadine for mild-moderate cases.
Sulfonylureas
Glyburide, glipizide, glimepiride		Dextrose + OctreotideD50: 50 mL IV adults · Octreotide: 50–100 mcg SQ Q6hOctreotide suppresses insulin secretion — prevents rebound hypoglycemia. Continue monitoring glucose Q1–2h.
Valproic AcidL-Carnitine100 mg/kg IV bolus (max 3g) → 50 mg/kg Q8h OR 15 mg/kg Q4–6hTreat until clinical improvement. Especially indicated with hyperammonemia or hepatotoxicity.
WarfarinVitamin K + 4-Factor PCC (Kcentra)Major bleed: Vitamin K 10 mg IV slow + Kcentra weight-based
Elevated INR, no bleed: Vitamin K 2.5–5 mg PO		Kcentra works immediately. Vitamin K takes 6–12h. Give both for major bleeding.
Decontamination Agents
KCHC Antidotes & Reversals Cheat Sheet · July 2022
AgentIndicationDoseContraindications
Activated CharcoalMost oral ingestions within 1–2 hours10:1 ratio (charcoal:poison). Dilute 1g charcoal in 8 mL water.Altered airway, active vomiting, GI obstruction, or risk of perforation
Whole Bowel Irrigation
(Polyethylene glycol)		Sustained-release ingestions (verapamil, diltiazem XL, glyburide XL, theophylline)Adults/adolescents: 1.5–2 L/hr over 4–6 hrs
Children: 0.5 L/hr or 25 mL/kg/hr		Ileus, bowel obstruction, toxic megacolon, hemodynamic instability
🎮 Toxidrome SimulatorSelect symptoms → identify toxidrome → pick antidote
Select presenting symptoms:
📋 Quick Reference Card
Quick Reference Card
One-page rapid lookup · Poster version · KCHC July 2022
Rebecca Chu, PharmD, BCPS · Kings County Hospital Center · July 2022
!Poison Control 24/7: 1-800-222-1222 · Also review NYC H+H/Kings County Policy and Procedure for Guidelines for Antidote Use on SharePoint.
ToxinAntidoteDose (adult)
AcetaminophenNAC IV (Acetadote)150/50/100 mg/kg over 1/4/16h
AnticholinergicsPhysostigmine0.5–2 mg IV slow Q10–30 min
AspirinSodium bicarb1–2 mEq/kg bolus → 150 mEq/L infusion · Goal urine pH >7.5
BenzosFlumazenil ⚠0.2–1 mg IV Q1 min (naïve pts only)
BB / CCBInsulin + D25 · Glucagon · Ca²⁺Insulin 0.5–1 U/kg + D25 25g · Glucagon 3–5 mg IV
CyanideHydroxocobalamin5g IV × 1
DigoxinDigiFABAcute: 10 vials · Chronic: 1–2 vials
Ethylene glycol / MeOHFomepizole15 mg/kg IV → 10 mg/kg Q12h × 4
Heparin (UFH)Protamine sulfate1 mg per 100 units · Max 50 mg
LMWH (enoxaparin)Protamine sulfate1 mg per 1 mg enoxaparin (<8h) · Max 50 mg
LithiumHD + 0.9% NSHD for level ≥4 or severe sx
OpioidsNaloxone0.04–2 mg IV Q2–3 min · Infusion 0.25–6.25 mg/hr
Serotonin syndromeCyproheptadine12 mg PO load → 2 mg Q2h · Max 32 mg/day
SulfonylureasD50 + OctreotideD50 50 mL + Octreotide 50–100 mcg SQ Q6h
Valproic acidL-Carnitine100 mg/kg IV (max 3g) → 50 mg/kg Q8h
WarfarinKcentra + Vit KMajor bleed: Kcentra weight-based + Vit K 10 mg IV
Acetaminophen (Tylenol) Overdose
N-Acetylcysteine Protocol · KCHC · July 2022
Rebecca Chu, PharmD, BCPS · July 2022
!Check acetaminophen level at 4 hours post ingestion. Plot on Rumack-Matthew nomogram. Best outcomes when NAC given within 8 hours of ingestion.
🧮 NAC Dose Calculator
N-Acetylcysteine (Acetadote / Mucomyst)
Toxicity Stages
StageTime from IngestionSigns & Symptoms
I0.5–24 hoursNausea, vomiting, diaphoresis, malaise. Labs may be normal.
II24–72 hoursRUQ pain, hepatic tenderness. Rise in AST/ALT, PT/INR, bilirubin.
III72–96 hoursPeak hepatotoxicity. Jaundice, coagulopathy, encephalopathy, renal failure. Highest mortality.
IV4 days – 2 weeksRecovery phase. Hepatic regeneration if survived Stage III.
Rumack-Matthew Nomogram — Key Thresholds
  • 4-hour level ≥150 mcg/mL: treatment line — initiate NAC
  • 4-hour level ≥300 mcg/mL: probable toxicity — aggressive treatment
  • If ingestion time unknown or level taken <4 hours: treat empirically, recheck at 4 hours
  • Chronic ingestion (multiple doses over time): nomogram does NOT apply — treat based on symptoms and liver enzymes
Beta-Blocker & Calcium Channel Blocker Overdose
Rebecca Chu, PharmD, BCPS · KCHC Antidotes & Reversals · July 2022
KCHC · July 2022 · Rebecca Chu, PharmD, BCPS
!Bradycardia + hypotension + hypoglycemia in BB OD. Bradycardia + hypotension ± hyperglycemia in CCB OD. At overdose doses, receptor specificity is lost.
AgentInitial DoseIf No Response / MaintenanceMonitor
Glucagon
BB and CCB		3–5 mg IV over 1–2 minRepeat at 4–10 mg if no response in 5–10 min · Infusion: 2–5 mg/hrGlucose Q30 min until stable, then Q1h · K⁺ and Mg²⁺ Q1h until stable then Q2h · Short half-life — anticipate rebound
High-Dose Insulin + Dextrose
BB and CCB — first-line for CCB		Regular insulin 0.5–1 unit/kg IV bolus + D50 25g IV × 1Insulin infusion 0.5–1 unit/kg/hr + Dextrose 0.5 g/kg/hr (titrate to maintain BG 100–250)BG Q30 min until stable · K⁺ Q1h · Replace K⁺ if <2.5 mEq/L
Parenteral Calcium
CCB only		CaCl₂ 10%: 20 mg/kg over 5–10 min (max 1–2 g)
OR Ca gluconate 10%: 60 mg/kg over 5–10 min (max 3–6 g)		Repeat Q10–20 min for 3–4 doses · Continuous: CaCl₂ 20–40 mg/kg/hr or Ca gluconate 60–120 mg/kg/hrCaCl₂ has 3× elemental Ca · Extravasation risk with CaCl₂ — prefer central line · Ca gluconate safer peripherally
Activated Charcoal
If immediate-release within 1–2h		Standard decontamination doseAirway must be protected
Whole Bowel Irrigation
Sustained-release formulation		Start 1.5–2 L/hr PEGContinue until rectal effluent is clear
Opioid Overdose & Naloxone Dosing
Rebecca Chu, PharmD, BCPS · KCHC Antidotes & Reversals · July 2022
KCHC · July 2022 · Rebecca Chu, PharmD, BCPS
!Classic triad: miosis · respiratory depression · altered mental status. Duration of naloxone (30–90 min) is shorter than most opioids — patient must be monitored continuously.
🧮 Naloxone Dose Calculator
Naloxone (Narcan)
Clinical Pearls — Naloxone
  • Opioid-dependent patients: start at 0.04–0.1 mg IV to avoid precipitated withdrawal. Titrate to adequate respirations — NOT full reversal.
  • Fentanyl OD: may require multiple doses due to high potency. Higher doses (2–4 mg) may be needed.
  • Infusion rule of thumb: start infusion at 2/3 of the effective bolus dose per hour.
  • Discharge: observe minimum 4–6 hours after last naloxone dose. Long-acting opioid OD (methadone, extended-release formulations) requires ICU admission.
Benzodiazepine Overdose & Flumazenil
Rebecca Chu, PharmD, BCPS · KCHC Antidotes & Reversals · July 2022
KCHC · July 2022 · Rebecca Chu, PharmD, BCPS
!Flumazenil CONTRAINDICATED in: chronic benzo users, polypharmacy OD, TCA co-ingestion, seizure disorder, unknown ingestion. Risk of life-threatening seizures.
ParameterDetails
AgentFlumazenil (Romazicon)
IndicationSingle-agent benzo OD in a benzo-naïve patient ONLY. Must rule out polypharmacy.
Dose0.2 mg IV → additional 0.2–1 mg IV every 1 min until patient wakes up · Maximum cumulative: 3–5 mg
Duration1–2 hours only — re-sedation common. Continuous monitoring required.
ContraindicationsChronic benzo use · Polypharmacy OD · TCA ingestion · Known seizure disorder · Elevated ICP · Suspected physical dependence
Clinical Approach — Most Benzo ODs

For most benzodiazepine overdoses: supportive care only. Protect airway, support ventilation, position patient. Flumazenil is the exception, not the rule. Most clinicians do not routinely use flumazenil due to seizure risk.

Digoxin Toxicity & DigiFAB Dosing
Rebecca Chu, PharmD, BCPS · KCHC Antidotes & Reversals · July 2022
KCHC · July 2022 · Rebecca Chu, PharmD, BCPS
!Narrow therapeutic window: 0.8–2 ng/mL. Toxicity enhanced by hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, renal failure. Monitor K⁺ carefully — avoid calcium in hyperkalemia from digoxin toxicity.
🧮 DigiFAB Dose Calculator
Digoxin Immune Fab (DigiFAB)
Digoxin Toxicity Management
ConditionManagement
HyperkalemiaGive DigiFAB first. Consider dextrose + insulin or sodium bicarbonate. AVOID calcium — can precipitate fatal dysrhythmias ("stone heart").
Bradycardia / AV blockAtropine 0.5–1 mg IV · DigiFAB · Pacing if unresponsive
Ventricular dysrhythmiasDigiFAB · Lidocaine or phenytoin (avoid Class IA antiarrhythmics)
Post-FAB monitoringStandard digoxin assays unreliable for ~7 days after DigiFAB. Use free serum digoxin level if available. Monitor K⁺ — hypokalemia common post-treatment.
Anticoagulant Reversal
Warfarin · UFH · LMWH · KCHC · July 2022
2018 ASH Guidelines · 2012 ACCP Guidelines · Rebecca Chu, PharmD, BCPS · July 2022
🧮 Kcentra (4-Factor PCC) Calculator — Warfarin Reversal
Weight-based dosing by INR
🧮 Protamine Sulfate Calculator — Heparin / LMWH Reversal
UFH or LMWH reversal
Warfarin Reversal — Guideline Summary
Scenario2018 ASH2012 ACCPVitamin K Dose
Serious / life-threatening bleed4-Factor PCC + Vitamin K IV4-Factor PCC + Vitamin K IV10 mg IV slow (over 10–20 min)
Non-major bleedHold warfarin + Vitamin K POHold warfarin + Vitamin K PO2.5–5 mg PO
Supratherapeutic INR, no bleedHold 1–2 doses, monitorHold + low-dose Vitamin K if INR >101–2.5 mg PO if INR >10
Other Toxins — Protocols
Rebecca Chu, PharmD, BCPS · KCHC Antidotes & Reversals · July 2022
Serotonin Syndrome · Anticholinergic · Cyanide · Toxic Alcohols · KCHC July 2022
Serotonin Syndrome
Classic Features — Hunter Criteria
  • Triad: Neuromuscular abnormalities + autonomic instability + altered mental status
  • Key findings: Clonus (spontaneous or inducible), hyperreflexia, diaphoresis, tremor, agitation, hyperthermia
  • Cyproheptadine: 12 mg PO load → 2 mg Q2h · Maintenance 4–8 mg Q6h · Max 32 mg/day
  • Benzos for agitation and seizures. Remove all serotonergic agents.
Anticholinergic Toxidrome
Classic Features

"Blind as a bat · Dry as a bone · Red as a beet · Hot as a hare · Mad as a hatter"

  • Mydriasis, dry skin/mucous membranes, flushing, hyperthermia, tachycardia, urinary retention, altered mental status
  • Physostigmine: 0.5–2 mg IV Q10–30 min · Do not infuse faster than 1 mg/min
  • Supportive care: cooling, benzos for agitation
Cyanide Poisoning
AntidoteDoseNotes
Hydroxocobalamin
Cyanokit — preferred		5g IV × 1 (or 70 mg/kg IV) · May repeat × 1Preferred agent. Safe with CO co-poisoning. ADEs: hypertension, rash, red discoloration of skin/urine/mucous membranes.
Sodium nitrate + Sodium thiosulfateNa nitrate 300 mg IV (10 mL of 3%) at 2.5–5 mL/min · Na thiosulfate max 12.5g IV over 10–30 minDo NOT use if concurrent CO poisoning — methemoglobin worsens O₂ delivery.
Toxic Alcohols — Comparison
AlcoholSourcesToxicityTreatment
Ethylene GlycolAntifreezeAnion-gap metabolic acidosis, renal failure, hypocalcemiaFomepizole 15 mg/kg IV · HD · Thiamine 100 mg IV Q6h · B6 100 mg IV Q6h
MethanolWindshield fluid, paint remover, embalming fluidAnion-gap metabolic acidosis, optic nerve toxicity, blindnessFomepizole 15 mg/kg IV · HD · Folinic acid 1–2 mg/kg (up to 50 mg) IV Q4–6h · Folic acid 50 mg IV Q4–6h
IsopropanolRubbing alcoholInebriation, hemorrhagic gastritis · Osmol gap elevated, NO anion gap acidosisSupportive only · HD if blood level >150 mg/dL or hemodynamic compromise
Lithium Toxicity
Level (mEq/L)SymptomsAction
<1.5GI complaints, fine tremorHydration with 0.9% NS · Monitor levels
1.5–2.0Coarse tremor, confusion, somnolenceAggressive hydration · Consider HD if symptomatic
>2.0Seizures, coma, cardiac dysrhythmiasHemodialysis urgently (reduces half-life to 3.6–5.7h from 12–27h)
≥4.0Severe toxicityHemodialysis mandatory
Geriatric Agent Selector
Patient Profile → Risk Flags → Recommended Screening Tools
AGS Beers 2019 · STOPP/START v2 · ACB Scale · Deprescribing.org 2018
!Beers 2019 edition (2023 update exists). STOPP/START v2 ~2011 (v3 2023 exists). Use as clinical decision support only.
⚡ Patient ProfileCrCl auto-calculates from SCr + weight
Physiologic Changes in Aging That Drive Drug Risk
ChangeDrug Therapy ImpactHigh-Risk Drug Classes
↓ Renal clearance (GFR falls ~1 mL/min/yr after 40)Renally-cleared drugs accumulateDigoxin, DOACs, metformin, gabapentin, LMWH, TMP-SMX
↓ Hepatic first-pass metabolism↑ Bioavailability of oral drugsOpioids, benzodiazepines, propranolol, nitrates
↑ Body fat · ↓ lean mass · ↓ total body water↑ Vd for lipophilic drugs → prolonged effectBenzodiazepines (accumulate), digoxin (↓ Vd → toxicity)
↓ Albumin (frail/malnourished patients)↑ Free fraction of protein-bound drugsWarfarin, phenytoin, NSAIDs, valproic acid
↑ CNS sensitivity to sedativesGreater CNS depression at same serum levelsOpioids, benzos, Z-drugs, anticholinergics, antipsychotics
↓ Baroreceptor sensitivity↑ Orthostatic hypotension → fallsAlpha-1 blockers, diuretics, antidepressants, antipsychotics
Beers Criteria
Potentially Inappropriate Medications in Older Adults · AGS 2019
AGS Beers Criteria® 2019 · 2023 update available
🔍 Beers Drug CheckerType any drug name to check Beers status
Table 2 — Avoid in Most Older Adults
Drug Class / DrugRecommendationRationaleEvidence
1st-gen antihistamines
Brompheniramine, chlorpheniramine, clemastine, cyproheptadine, diphenhydramine (oral), doxylamine, hydroxyzine, meclizine, promethazine		AvoidHighly anticholinergic; tolerance develops; confusion, dry mouth, constipation, fallsModerate · Strong
Antiparkinsonian agents
Benztropine (oral), trihexyphenidyl		AvoidNot recommended for EPS prevention; more effective PD agents availableModerate · Strong
Antispasmodics
Atropine, belladonna alkaloids, dicyclomine, hyoscyamine, methscopolamine, propantheline, scopolamine		AvoidHighly anticholinergic; uncertain effectivenessModerate · Strong
NSAIDs (non-selective oral)
Ibuprofen, naproxen, diclofenac, meloxicam, ketorolac, indomethacin, others		Avoid unless no alternatives + add PPI↑ GI bleeding, PUD, AKI, fluid retention, worsening HFModerate · Strong
Indomethacin · Ketorolac (all routes)AvoidMost CNS ADEs of all NSAIDs; GI bleeding; AKIModerate · Strong
MeperidineAvoidIneffective oral analgesic at common doses; higher neurotoxicity, delirium riskModerate · Strong
Digoxin >0.125 mg/dayAvoid as first-line for AF/HF; avoid >0.125 mg/dayNo added benefit at higher doses; ↑ toxicity risk esp. with renal impairmentLow/Mod · Strong
Nifedipine (immediate-release)AvoidHypotension risk; precipitates myocardial ischemiaHigh · Strong
DisopyramideAvoidStrongly anticholinergic; may induce HFLow · Strong
DronedaroneAvoid in permanent AF or decompensated HFWorse outcomes in permanent AF or recently decompensated HFHigh · Strong
Peripheral alpha-1 blockers
Doxazosin, prazosin, terazosin		Avoid as antihypertensiveHigh risk of orthostatic hypotensionModerate · Strong
Central alpha agonists
Clonidine, guanabenz, guanfacine, methyldopa, reserpine >0.1 mg/day		AvoidHigh risk of CNS ADEs; bradycardia; orthostatic hypotensionLow · Strong
Benzodiazepines
All — short/intermediate (alprazolam, lorazepam, oxazepam, temazepam) and long-acting (diazepam, flurazepam, chlordiazepoxide)		Avoid↑ Sensitivity; cognitive impairment, delirium, falls, fractures, MVAs in elderlyModerate · Strong
Non-benzo hypnotics (Z-drugs)
Eszopiclone, zaleplon, zolpidem		AvoidSame ADEs as benzos in elderly; minimal sleep improvement; ↑ ER visitsModerate · Strong
BarbituratesAvoidHigh physical dependence; greater overdose risk at low dosesModerate · Strong
Skeletal muscle relaxants
Carisoprodol, cyclobenzaprine, methocarbamol, orphenadrine, metaxalone		AvoidAnticholinergic, sedating, ↑ fracture risk; effectiveness questionableModerate · Strong
Sliding-scale insulinAvoid↑ Hypoglycemia risk without improved glycemic controlModerate · Strong
Glyburide · ChlorpropamideAvoidProlonged hypoglycemia in elderlyHigh · Strong
DesmopressinAvoid for nocturiaHigh risk of hyponatremia in elderlyModerate · Strong
Systemic estrogensAvoid (oral/patch); vaginal low-dose acceptableCarcinogenic potential; no cardioprotective effect in older womenHigh · Strong
NitrofurantoinAvoid for long-term suppression or CrCl <30Pulmonary toxicity, hepatotoxicity, peripheral neuropathyLow · Strong
TCAs
Amitriptyline, nortriptyline, doxepin >6 mg/day, imipramine, desipramine		Avoid (except low-dose doxepin ≤6 mg)Highly anticholinergic; sedating; orthostatic hypotension; pro-arrhythmicModerate · Strong
Table 3 — Avoid Based on Condition
ConditionDrugs to AvoidConcern
Heart FailureNSAIDs · COX-2 inhibitors · Non-DHP CCBs (diltiazem, verapamil) in systolic HF · Thiazolidinediones · Dronedarone (decompensated)Fluid retention, worsening HF, ↑ mortality
SyncopeAChE inhibitors · Non-selective alpha-1 blockers · Antipsychotics (chlorpromazine, thioridazine, olanzapine)Orthostatic hypotension → syncope
DeliriumAnticholinergics · Benzos · Z-drugs · H2-blockers · Antipsychotics (high-risk patients)Worsen or precipitate delirium
DementiaAnticholinergics · Antipsychotics (unless non-pharm options failed) · Z-drugs · BenzosCognitive worsening; ↑ mortality with antipsychotics
Falls / Fracture HistoryOpioids · Benzos · Z-drugs · Antipsychotics · SSRIs/SNRIs/TCAs · AnticonvulsantsAll increase falls/fracture risk
Parkinson's DiseaseAll antipsychotics except quetiapine, clozapine, pimavanserin · Metoclopramide · ProchlorperazineDopamine antagonists worsen PD symptoms
Peptic Ulcer / GI Bleed HistoryNSAIDs · Aspirin >325 mg/day · Corticosteroids without gastroprotectionExacerbate or cause new GI ulcers/bleeding
Table 4 — Use With Caution
DrugPopulationConcern
Aspirin (primary prevention)≥70 years↑ Major bleeding risk; limited net benefit for primary prevention
Dabigatran · Rivaroxaban≥75 years (VTE/AF treatment)↑ GI bleeding vs warfarin; limited safety data in very elderly
SSRIs, SNRIs, TCAs, antipsychotics, diuretics, mirtazapine, tramadol, carbamazepine, oxcarbazepineAll elderly↑ Risk of SIADH/hyponatremia — monitor Na⁺ closely when starting or changing doses
TMP-SMX + ACEI or ARBCrCl <45↑ Hyperkalemia risk
Opioids + Benzos · Opioids + GabapentinoidsAll↑ Respiratory depression, overdose, death — avoid combination
3+ CNS-active drugs
(antidepressants, antiepileptics, antipsychotics, benzos, opioids)		All elderly↑ Falls and fracture risk — minimize number of CNS-active drugs
Table 6 — Renal Dose Adjustments
DrugCrCl ThresholdAction
Apixaban<25 mL/minAvoid
Dabigatran<30 mL/minAvoid
Rivaroxaban<50 (AF/VTE)Reduce dose (15–50); Avoid <15
Enoxaparin<30 mL/minReduce dose
Fondaparinux<30 mL/minAvoid
Spironolactone<30 mL/minAvoid
Nitrofurantoin<30 mL/minAvoid
Colchicine<30 mL/minReduce dose; monitor
Ciprofloxacin<30 mL/minReduce dose
TMP-SMX<30 mL/minReduce (15–29); Avoid <15
Ranitidine<50 mL/minReduce dose
🔍 Medication Review SimulatorEnter med list → instant Beers / STOPP / ACB flags
STOPP Criteria
Screening Tool for Older Persons Potentially inappropriate Prescriptions · v2 2011
Pharmacist's Letter · September 2011 · v3 2023 available
!Review each active medication against this list. If a drug matches a STOPP criterion, assess whether discontinuation is appropriate given the full clinical picture.
DrugSituation to STOPConcern
CARDIOVASCULAR
NSAIDsWith GI ulcer/bleed history without PPI · SBP ≥160 · Heart failure · GFR <50 · Long-term for gout · With warfarin · With aspirin without PPIGI bleed, worsening HTN/HF, renal impairment
Digoxin >0.125 mg/dayLong-term with GFR <50Toxicity with reduced renal clearance
Warfarin>6 months for 1st uncomplicated DVT · >12 months for 1st uncomplicated PE · With bleeding disorder · With NSAID/aspirin without PPINo added benefit; bleeding risk
Loop diureticDependent edema without HF · Monotherapy for HTNIneffective; safer alternatives available
ThiazideWith gout historyWorsening gout
Non-DHP CCBs
Diltiazem, verapamil		Chronic constipation · With TCA · NYHA III/IV HF · Verapamil + beta-blockerWorsening constipation, HF, heart block
Vasodilators
Hydralazine, alpha-blockers		With orthostatic hypotension (>20 mmHg SBP drop standing)↑ Falls
CNS / PSYCHOTROPICS
AnticholinergicsBladder antispasmodics with dementia, constipation, BPH, or glaucomaAnticholinergic ADEs, confusion, urinary retention, worsening glaucoma
1st-gen antihistaminesUse >1 week · With fall historySedation, anticholinergic effects
BenzodiazepinesLong-acting agent >1 month · ≥1 fall in past 3 monthsSedation, confusion, falls
AntipsychoticsAs hypnotic >1 month · With parkinsonism · With epilepsy (phenothiazines) · With fall historyEPS, worsening Parkinson's, seizures, falls
SSRIsNa⁺ <130 mEq/L within past 2 monthsHyponatremia
TCAsWith dementia · glaucoma · arrhythmias · constipation · opioids · BPH · urinary retention · CCBAnticholinergic effects, pro-arrhythmic, constipation, urinary retention
OpioidsWith anticonvulsants (non-palliative) · With fall historyImpaired psychomotor function, syncope, additional falls
ENDOCRINE / GI / RESPIRATORY
Chlorpropamide · GlyburideType 2 diabetesProlonged hypoglycemia in elderly
Systemic corticosteroidsCOPD maintenanceSystemic corticosteroid side effects; inhaled preferred
TheophyllineMonotherapy for COPDSafer, more effective alternatives available
PPI (full therapeutic dose)>8 weeks without ongoing indication for high-dose useNo added benefit; see Deprescribing tab
EstrogensWith history of breast cancer or VTE · Intact uterus without progestinCancer recurrence; endometrial cancer risk
Metoclopramide · ProchlorperazineWith parkinsonismWorsening PD symptoms
Alpha-blockers (urinary)Urinary incontinence ≥1×/day in men · With urinary catheter >2 months · With orthostatic hypotensionUrinary frequency/incontinence, falls
START Criteria
Medications Frequently OMITTED in Older Adults · v2 2011
Pharmacist's Letter · September 2011 · v3 2023 available
!Review against active diagnoses. If the condition applies and no contraindication exists, consider initiating these evidence-based medications.
ConditionConsider STARTing (if no contraindication)
CARDIOVASCULAR
Heart FailureACEI or ARB · Beta-blocker (carvedilol, bisoprolol, metoprolol succinate for systolic HF)
Post-MIACEI or ARB · Beta-blocker · Statin
Atrial Fibrillation (chronic)Warfarin or DOAC for stroke prevention
CVD / PVD in sinus rhythmAspirin or Clopidogrel · Statin
Chronic stable anginaBeta-blocker
SBP repeatedly >160 mmHgAntihypertensive therapy
Diabetes with ≥1 CV risk factorAspirin (primary prevention)
RESPIRATORY
Mild–moderate COPD or AsthmaInhaled anticholinergic · Inhaled beta-2 agonist
Moderate–severe COPD or AsthmaInhaled corticosteroid
CNS / MUSCULOSKELETAL
Depressive symptoms ≥3 monthsAntidepressant
Parkinson's with functional impairmentLevodopa
Chronic systemic glucocorticoid useBisphosphonate
Known osteoporosisCalcium + Vitamin D
ENDOCRINE / GI
Type 2 DiabetesMetformin (if renal function permits)
Diabetic nephropathyACEI or ARB
Chronic diverticular disease / constipationDietary fiber
Chronic severe GERD or peptic stricturePPI
Anticholinergic Burden (ACB) Calculator
Aging Brain Care Program · Indiana University · 2012 Update
ACB Scale © 2008, 2012 Regenstrief Institute Inc. · www.agingbraincare.org
!Each 1-point increase in ACB total score = 46% increased risk of cognitive impairment over 6 years and 0.33-point decline in MMSE over 2 years. Each definite anticholinergic may increase mortality risk by 26%.
🧮 ACB Score CalculatorSelect all current medications
Score 1 — Possible anticholinergic effect
Score 2 — Definite anticholinergic (mild)
Score 3 — Definite anticholinergic (strong)
Total ACB Score
0
Medications: None selected
ACB Score Interpretation
  • Score 1: In vitro evidence of muscarinic antagonist activity
  • Score 2: Literature or expert opinion evidence of clinical anticholinergic effect
  • Score 3: Confirmed anticholinergic-cognitive effect
  • Each 1-point increase: 46% ↑ cognitive impairment risk over 6 years · 0.33-point ↓ MMSE over 2 years
  • Score ≥3: May increase mortality risk by 26% per point · Deprescribing review strongly recommended
Deprescribing Algorithms
PPI · Antihyperglycemics · August 2018
Deprescribing.org · Canadian Deprescribing Network · August 2018 · Strong Recommendation (Systematic Review + GRADE)
🧮 PPI Deprescribing Decision Tool
Should this PPI be deprescribed?
🧮 Antihyperglycemic Deprescribing Tool
Elderly T2DM — Deprescribing DecisionAge ≥65
A1C Targets in Elderly — Individualized
Patient ProfileA1C TargetRationale
Healthy, >10 year life expectancy<7.0–7.5%Standard target appropriate
Multiple comorbidities, moderate functional impairment<8.0%Reduce hypoglycemia risk while maintaining glycemic benefit
Frail, dementia, limited life expectancy (<5 years)<8.5% or avoid tight controlHarms of hypoglycemia outweigh tight glycemic control benefit
PPI Step-Down Dose Reference
PPIStandard DoseLow / Maintenance Dose
Omeprazole20 mg+10 mg
Esomeprazole20–40 mg20 mg
Lansoprazole30 mg+15 mg
Pantoprazole40 mg20 mg
Rabeprazole20 mg10 mg
Medication Safety
LASA · High Alert · Allergy Protocol · Titration · Restricted · NIOSH
Joint Commission MM Standards 2024 · KCHC · Alla Luka PharmD · Approved: Zoraya Pod RPh MPA
Standards Covered in This Tab
JC StandardTopicPane
MM.01.01.03High Alert & Hazardous Medications→ High Alert Meds
MM.01.02.01LASA Medications — Revised 02/2024→ LASA Checker
MM.04.01.01Medication Orders — Titration Requirements→ Titration Checklist
MM.05.01.01Pharmacist Order Verification — Type I Allergy Protocol→ Allergy Protocol
