NIOSH 2016Hazardous Drug Groups 1, 2, 3 · EPIC MAR Class A/B/C→ NIOSH Hazardous
Required Patient Information (MM.01.01.01)
Always Required
  • Age
  • Sex
  • Diagnosis
  • Allergies / Sensitivities
  • Current medications
When Necessary
  • Height & Weight
  • Pregnancy & Lactation status
  • Lab results
LASA Checker
Look-Alike / Sound-Alike Medications · KCHC List Revised 02/2024
Joint Commission MM.01.02.01 · ISMP Recommendations · KCHC 2024
🔍 LASA Drug SearchType any drug name to check
KCHC LASA List 2024 — All 11 Pairs
!Pairs 10 and 11 are NEW as of 02/2024. All pairs use tall-man lettering in ADCs and CPOE. Products stored separately. LASA stickers placed at storage locations.
#Drug ADrug BKey Risk2024
1PrezCOBIX®
Darunavir + cobicistat		PrezISTA®
Darunavir alone		Both contain darunavir — cobicistat adds pharmacokinetic boosting
2Influenza Flucelvax®
Egg-free (ovalbumin-free)		Influenza Afluria® / Flulaval® / Fluad® / Fluarix®
Standard formulations		Critical for egg allergy patients — egg-free vs egg-containing
3Tdap
AdoTdap — Adolescent/Adult		DTaP
Pediatric only		Different antigen concentrations — never interchange adult and pediatric formulations
4LORazepam
Ativan®		ALPRAZolam
Xanax®		Both benzodiazepines — tall-man lettering: LOR vs ALPRA
5PrednisoLONE
Prelone®		PredniSONE
Deltasone®		PrednisoLONE is active; predniSONE requires hepatic activation — not equivalent
6GuanFACINEguaiFENesin
Robitussin®		Antihypertensive/ADHD vs expectorant — completely different indications
7ChlorTHALIDONEChlorproMAZINEThiazide-like diuretic vs first-generation antipsychotic
8Bupivacaine HCl
Preservative free 0.75%		Bupivacaine HCl / DEXTROSE
Bupivacaine Spinal® — preservative free 0.75%		Spinal formulation contains dextrose — not interchangeable
9BupreNORphine
Suboxone®		BusPIRone
Buspar®		Opioid partial agonist/MAT vs non-benzo anxiolytic
10HydrALAZINE
Apresoline®		HydrOXYzine
Atarax®		Antihypertensive vs antihistamine/anxiolytic — high mix-up potentialNEW
11MetroNIDAZOLE
Flagyl®		MetFORMIN
Glucophage®		Antibiotic vs oral antidiabetic — very different clinical use and toxicity profileNEW
KCHC LASA Risk Reduction Strategies (MM.01.02.01)
  • LASA stickers placed at all storage locations for listed pairs
  • LASA combinations stored separately — never adjacent
  • Tall-man lettering installed in all ADCs and CPOE (Epic)
  • LASA warning alerts built into ADC systems
  • List reviewed annually; updated as needed by P&T Committee
High Alert Medications
MM.01.01.03 · Risk-Reduction Strategies by Drug Class
Joint Commission MM.01.01.03 · ISMP · TJC · KCHC 2024
!High Alert IV medications require dual nurse verification. Pharmacy places High Alert stickers at all stock locations. ADC machines have High Alert pop-ups. Medication Safety Committee reviews all errors.
High Alert Drug ClassRisk-Reduction Strategies at KCHC
Chemotherapy Agents
  • Removed from nursing units — segregated areas only
  • Must be ordered via a treatment plan
  • Two independent pharmacist calculations required on all orders
  • Dispensed in chemo bags with chemotherapy labels
  • Dual nurse verification required for IV administration
Neuromuscular Blockers
Cisatracurium, Rocuronium, Succinylcholine
  • Restricted to: ED, ICUs, PACU, OR, Anesthesia only
  • Order sets available
  • Stored in lidded containers
  • Auxiliary labels required
Insulin
  • Stored in color-coded bins in refrigerator
  • Tall-man lettering in ADCs for LASA pairs
  • Standardized concentration: 100 units/mL for vials
  • Standardized concentration for insulin drip
  • Order sets available
  • Insulin drip via smart pump
  • Dual nurse verification for IV insulin administration
Concentrated Electrolytes
KCl, Phosphate, Acetate; MgSO4; Ca Gluconate/Chloride; NaCl 2%, 3%, 23.4%
  • Order sets for all electrolyte replacements
  • Potassium: concentrated vials removed from all patient care areas — premixed solutions only
  • Hypertonic NaCl (2%, 3%, 23.4%): Restricted to ICU and ED Critical Care areas only
  • Magnesium: premixed solutions only
  • Stored in pharmacy — patient-specific orders only
Anticoagulants
  • Order sets available for all anticoagulation
  • Standardized concentrations (premixed solutions)
  • IV Heparin via smart pump
  • Dual nurse verification for IV heparin
  • Warfarin: daily INR monitoring + new order placed daily
IV Adrenergic Agonists
Epinephrine, Phenylephrine, Norepinephrine
  • Restricted to: ICU, ED, OR only
  • Standardized concentrations for all agents
  • Infusion pump drug library required
  • Titration parameters built into EMR
Controlled Drugs — Injectable Narcotics/Opiates
  • Limited injectable opioids available in ADC
  • Order sets for IV narcotics
  • Standard concentrations for IV narcotic drips; limited PCA concentrations
  • Pharmacist screens for duplication of pain scale indications
  • IV narcotic drips via smart pump
Oxytocin
  • Order sets with titration parameters
  • Standardized concentrations (premixed IV solutions)
Total Parenteral Nutrition (TPN)
  • Dual pharmacist verification required
  • Dual nurse verification required for administration
Type I Allergy — 2nd RPh Verification Protocol
Contraindicated Medication Orders · KCHC Pharmacy Department
KCHC Pharmacy Competency · Signed: Esdras Obas PharmD · 6/4/2024
!This protocol applies when a medication order is pending and the patient has a HIGH severity allergy listed in EPIC. Pharmacists are expected to identify and intervene on all contraindicated orders.
How to Identify Type I Allergic Reactions
Signs & Symptoms (HIGH Severity in EPIC)
• Urticaria (hives)
• Pruritus (itching)
• Angioedema (face/lips/throat swelling)
• Wheezing / shortness of breath
• Anaphylaxis (severe, life-threatening)
4-Step Intervention Protocol
Step 1 — Contact Prescriber
Contact primary resident physician by telephone
If no response → escalate to the attending physician of record. Discuss the contraindicated medication order before any dispensing occurs.
Step 2 — Antimicrobial-Specific Review
For antimicrobial orders: review chart for ID provider documentation
Confirm either: (a) desensitization has occurred or is planned, OR (b) benefits outweigh risks with shared decision-making documented by ID provider.
Step 3 — Two-Step Pharmacist Verification
Implement dual pharmacist verification for all severe allergy contraindicated orders
Document an iVent in EPIC under "Allergy/Cross Sensitivity" category. Record the discussion and agreement (or alternative plan) in the EHR via iVent.
Step 4 — Dual RPh Signature Required
Both pharmacists must initial the iVent
If prescriber does not agree on an alternative → second pharmacist reviews as second verification point. Both pharmacists initial iVent as signature attesting the order is deemed safe and appropriate prior to dispensing.
Key Points
  • This applies to orders with HIGH severity allergy flags in EPIC only
  • For antimicrobials: ID documentation is required before proceeding
  • iVent category: Allergy/Cross Sensitivity
  • No dispensing until dual RPh verification is complete and documented
  • Protocol signed by Dr. Esdras Obas, PharmD — 6/4/2024
Titration Order Checklist
MM.04.01.01 — Required Elements for All Titration Orders
Joint Commission MM.04.01.01 · KCHC CPOE/Epic 2024
!Every titration order entered in EPIC must contain all 6 required elements per TJC standard MM.04.01.01. Orders missing any element must be clarified with the prescriber before verification.
🗒️ Titration Order Verification Checklist
6 Required Elements — All Must Be Present
Common Titration Orders at KCHC — Checklist Examples
DrugTypical GoalCommon MaxInterval
NorepinephrineMAP ≥65 mmHgPer ICU order setEvery 5–15 min
NicardipineSBP target per order15 mg/hrEvery 5–15 min
PropofolRASS target (e.g., -2)Per ICU order setEvery 5–10 min
DexmedetomidineRASS target1.5 mcg/kg/hrEvery 30 min
Regular Insulin dripBlood glucose per protocolPer insulin protocolPer protocol schedule
IV HeparinaPTT per anticoagulation protocolPer protocolPer aPTT level
Acceptable Order Types (MM.04.01.01)
  • PRN orders: Must include clear indication and specific frequency
  • Titration orders: Must include all 6 elements above
  • All orders entered via EPIC CPOE
  • Pharmacist verifies all elements before dispensing
Restricted Medications
Location-Restricted Drug Classes at KCHC
Joint Commission MM.01.01.03 · KCHC 2024
!These medications have specific location restrictions at KCHC. Orders from outside restricted areas must be clarified before dispensing. Contact the prescriber and verify the clinical setting.
Drug / ClassPermitted LocationsRestriction RationaleStorage/Dispensing Rule
Neuromuscular Blockers
Cisatracurium · Rocuronium · Succinylcholine		 ED · ICUs · PACU · OR · Anesthesia ONLY Risk of respiratory arrest if administered without ventilator support or without trained personnel Stored in lidded containers. Auxiliary labels required. Order sets available.
IV Adrenergic Agonists
Epinephrine IV · Phenylephrine IV · Norepinephrine IV		 ICU · ED · OR ONLY Require continuous hemodynamic monitoring and infusion pump with drug library titration parameters Standardized concentrations. Smart pump drug library required. Titration parameters in EMR.
Hypertonic Sodium Chloride
NaCl 2% · NaCl 3% · NaCl 23.4%		 ICU · ED Critical Care Areas ONLY Risk of osmotic demyelination syndrome, cerebral edema if given incorrectly. Requires monitoring. Stored in Pharmacy only. Prepared for patient-specific orders. Never stocked on general floors.
Potassium Chloride — Concentrated Pharmacy ONLY Direct IV administration of concentrated KCl is immediately fatal Concentrated vials removed from all patient care areas. Only commercially premixed solutions permitted on units.
Chemotherapy Agents Segregated pharmacy areas ONLY Hazardous drug handling requirements. Risk of extravasation injury. Removed from all nursing units. Treatment plan required. Dual pharmacist calculation. Chemo bags with chemo labels.
What to Do When You See a Restricted Med Ordered Outside Its Area
  1. Do not dispense until location is verified
  2. Contact the prescriber — confirm the patient is in or being transferred to the approved area
  3. For NMBs: verify airway management capability and ventilator availability
  4. Document the verification in EPIC via iVent if outside normal parameters
  5. Escalate to charge pharmacist or Director on Duty if prescriber cannot be reached
KCHC Formulary Antimicrobial Restriction Policy · Attachment B · Version 1.1 · Last Updated: August 30, 2017
⚠ Pending update — Remdesivir addition · Verify against current KCHC Shareplus policy before clinical use
!
All non-formulary antibiotics require ID approval. Vancomycin IV and Piperacillin/Tazobactam: first 7 days no ID approval needed — ID approval required after 7 days. ICU grace periods apply concurrently.
DrugRestrictionNotes
ANTIBIOTICS
AztreonamID Approval RequiredICU: approval required after 24hr
AmikacinID Approval RequiredICU: approval required after 24hr
CefotaximeID Approval Required
CeftazidimeID Approval Required
CefepimeID Approval RequiredICU: approval required after 24hr
CeftarolineID Approval Required
Piperacillin / Tazobactam (Zosyn)ID Approval After 7 DaysFirst 7 days: no approval needed · After 7 days: ID approval required
Vancomycin IVID Approval After 7 DaysFirst 7 days: no approval needed · After 7 days: ID approval required
Vancomycin oral solutionID Approval RequiredApproval required for doses >125 mg
Vancomycin retention enemaID Approval Required
LinezolidID Approval Required⚠ Serotonin syndrome risk with fentanyl — monitor if both ordered. FDA warning. See Pain tab → Fentanyl Patch for full interaction note.
Levofloxacin IVID Approval RequiredICU: approval required after 24hr
MeropenemID Approval RequiredICU: approval required after 24hr
Rifampin IVID Approval Required
PyrimethamineID Approval Required
SulfadiazineID Approval Required
Polymyxin B SulfateID Approval Required
Remdesivir (Veklury)ID Approval Required⚠ Pending formal policy update — verify with KCHC Shareplus
ANTIFUNGALS
FlucytosineID Approval Required
CaspofunginID Approval Required
Fluconazole IVID Approval RequiredICU: approval required after 24hr
Amphotericin BID Approval Required
ItraconazoleID Approval RequiredUnrestricted for Dermatology Attending
ANTIVIRALS
All AntiretroviralsID Approval RequiredException: when ordered for PEP
FoscarnetID Approval Required
NIOSH Hazardous Medications
3 Drug Groups · EPIC MAR Class A/B/C · Effective 10/25/2023
NIOSH 2016 · NYC H+H Hazardous Medications List · EPIC MAR Alert Update 10/25/2023
!As of 10/25/2023, EPIC labels and the Medication Administration Record (MAR) display Class A, B, or C hazardous drug designation with special precaution warnings. Full NIOSH list and handling documents available on NYC H+H Insider Page under Information Links.
EPIC MAR Hazardous Drug Classes (Effective 10/25/2023)
Class A
Antineoplastic agents (NIOSH Group 1). Highest hazard — full PPE, closed system transfer devices, dedicated waste.
Class B
Non-antineoplastic hazardous drugs (NIOSH Group 2). Meet NIOSH criteria — carcinogenicity, teratogenicity, or organ toxicity at low doses.
Class C
Reproductive risk drugs (NIOSH Group 3). Primarily risk to men/women actively trying to conceive and pregnant/breastfeeding women.
NIOSH GroupDrug TypeKey ExamplesMain Concern
Group 1
EPIC Class A		Antineoplastic drugs (AHFS 10:00)Cyclophosphamide, methotrexate, doxorubicin, cisplatin, vincristine, carboplatinCarcinogenic, mutagenic, teratogenic. Also pose reproductive risk.
Group 2
EPIC Class B		Non-antineoplastic hazardous drugsAzathioprine, chlorambucil, ganciclovir, ribavirin, warfarin (in pregnancy), finasteride, dutasteride, mycophenolateMeet ≥1 NIOSH criterion: carcinogenicity, developmental toxicity, reproductive toxicity, organ toxicity at low doses, genotoxicity
Group 3
EPIC Class C		Reproductive risk drugsTestosterone, estrogens, progesterone, misoprostol, leuprolide, thalidomidePrimarily reproductive risk to those actively trying to conceive or who are pregnant/breastfeeding. May be present in breast milk.
Safe Handling Principles (All NIOSH Groups)
  • Appropriate PPE per NIOSH Table 5 guidance for each activity and formulation type
  • Engineering controls as specified in NIOSH guidelines
  • EPIC MAR will display hazardous designation (A/B/C) and alert nursing to special precautions
  • Full NYC H+H Hazardous Medications List available on Insider Page under Information Links
  • Handling documents include: NIOSH List 2016 (Publication 2016-161, supersedes 2014-138)
  • Report exposure or spills per KCHC occupational safety protocol
Pharmacy Compounding — Hazardous Drugs (USP 797)
  • Category 1 CSPs: ISO 5 PEC in unclassified SCA. BUD: 12 hr RT / 24 hr refrigerated
  • Category 2 CSPs: ISO 7 clean room suite. BUD: 4 days RT / 10 days refrigerated / 45 days frozen
  • Note: KCHC does not currently prepare Category 2 CSPs
  • Annual written competency test + semi-annual media fill and glove fingertip tests required for all compounding personnel
Oral Syringe Compounding & Dispensing — 3-Signature Workflow
D Main Pharmacy · Compounded Liquid Medications
Source: KCHC Memo — Compounding & Dispensing of Doses in Oral Syringes in D Main Pharmacy · March 16, 2025 · Franklin Akinkunmi R.Ph. MBA, Assistant Director, Pharmacy Services · cc: Zoraya Pod, DOP · KCHC Pharmacy Staff Meeting 3/31/2026
ℹ ALL patient-specific drawn up oral syringe(s) of compounded liquid medications must have 3 initials/signatures before dispensing to the Units/Floors. A designated Final Approver is now scheduled per shift.
Step 1 — Compounding of Stock in D Main Pharmacy
  1. A Pharmacist does the entry in EPIC (Compounding and Repackaging), pull/scan (or enter) items needed, follow the recipe to make the compound needed per Patient-Specific order(s) and checks off the "Technician Final Check"
  2. A second Pharmacist does the "Pharmacist Final Review" in EPIC, after checking compounded stock, items used according to recipe, final concentration documented, BUD, storage condition (Room Temp, Refrigerate) and any special instructions (shake well)
Step 2 — Dispensing of Doses in Oral Syringes
  1. A Pharmacy Technician draws up the required dose from the compounded stock, as on the patient-specific label, into an oral syringe or syringes
  2. The Verification Pharmacist for the shift verifies the drawn up oral syringe(s) for accuracy of medication, dosage strength, volume per dose, etc.
  3. A designated Final Approver is scheduled per shift. The Final Approver MUST approve ALL drawn up oral syringe(s) of compounded medications BEFORE dispensing/sending/delivery to the Units for patient administration
Required 3 Initials/Signatures Before Dispensing
①
Pharmacy Technician
Who drew up the dose in the oral syringe
②
Verification Pharmacist
Shift verification pharmacist
③
Designated Final Approver
Scheduled per shift · Must approve BEFORE dispensing
KCHC Memo: Compounding & Dispensing of Doses in Oral Syringes in D Main Pharmacy · March 16, 2025 · Franklin Akinkunmi R.Ph. MBA, Assistant Director, Pharmacy Services · cc: Zoraya Pod, DOP
Pediatric Agent Selector
Patient Profile → Quick Dose → Route to Protocol
KCHC Pediatric Antimicrobial Dosing Guideline · June 2023 · P&T Approved April 2023
!
Ages 2 months – 18 years. All doses assume normal renal function. Verify max doses. Not applicable to neonates (<2 months) or NICU patients.

Full Reference Table — All 15 Drugs
!
All doses assume normal renal function · Ages 2 months–18 years · Verify max doses at each calculation
DrugIndicationRouteDoseMax/Dose
AmoxicillinGeneralPO22.5 mg/kg q12h875 mg
High-dose (CAP, AOM)PO45 mg/kg q12h2,000 mg
Strep pharyngitisPO50 mg/kg once daily1,000 mg
Amox/ClavulanateGeneralPO22.5 mg/kg q12h875 mg
High-dose (use 600 mg/5 mL susp)PO45 mg/kg q12h2,000 mg
AmpicillinGeneral / CAPIV50 mg/kg/dose q6h (200 mg/kg/day)2,000 mg
High-dose (meningitis, endocarditis)IV75 mg/kg/dose q6h (300 mg/kg/day)3,000 mg
UTIIV37.5 mg/kg/dose q6h2,000 mg
CefazolinGeneralIV33.3 mg/kg/dose q8h2,000 mg
Ceftriaxone
⚠ No Ca²⁺		CAPIV75 mg/kg q24h2,000 mg
Intra-abdominal (+ metro)IV50 mg/kg q24h2,000 mg
Meningitis (not <28 days)IV100 mg/kg/day q24h4,000 mg/day
Otitis mediaIV50 mg/kg q24h × 1–3 days1,000 mg
ClindamycinGeneralPO/IV10 mg/kg/dose q8h600 mg
Severe (osteomyelitis, MRSA)PO/IVPO: 13.3 mg/kg q8h · IV: 13.3 mg/kg q8hPO 600 · IV 900 mg
DoxycyclineGeneralPO/IV2 mg/kg/dose q12h100 mg
Chlamydia (≥8 yrs)PO100 mg BID × 7 days100 mg
FluconazoleOropharyngeal / esophageal candidiasisPO/IV6 mg/kg q24h400 mg
Systemic / invasive candidiasisPO/IV12 mg/kg q24h800 mg
Vulvovaginal candidiasis (adolescents)PO150 mg single dose150 mg
GentamicinGeneralIV2.5 mg/kg q8h—
Synergy (Gram-positive)IV2 mg/kg q8h—
MetronidazoleGeneral / AppendicitisPO/IV10 mg/kg q8h500 mg (append) / 1,333 mg (gen)
C. difficilePO/IV10 mg/kg q8h500 mg
Trichomoniasis <45 kgPO15 mg/kg q8h667 mg
Trichomoniasis ≥45 kgPO500 mg q12h × 7 days500 mg
OseltamivirInfants ≤8 monthsPO3 mg/kg q12h—
Infants ≥9 monthsPO3.5 mg/kg q12h—
≤15 kgPO30 mg q12h30 mg
>15–23 kg / >23–40 kgPO45 mg q12h / 60 mg q12h60 mg
>40 kgPO75 mg q12h75 mg
Pip/TazoGeneral ≤40 kgIV100 mg/kg q8h (300 mg/kg/day)3,000 mg
General >40 kgIV3,000 mg q6h3,000 mg
TMP/SMX
*TMP component		SSTIPO/IV6 mg/kg q12h320 mg
Severe (osteomyelitis, meningitis, PCP)PO/IV5 mg/kg q6–8h—
⚠ Ceftriaxone — Calcium Compatibility
Incompatible with all calcium-containing solutions (LR, PN). Never co-infuse via Y-site in any patient. Sequential administration acceptable in non-neonates if line is thoroughly flushed.
Gentamicin TDM
For patients on gentamicin >48 hours with normal renal function: draw trough before 4th dose. Target: <2 mcg/mL (general) · <1 mcg/mL (synergy).
Pediatric Conditions
CAP · SSTI · UTI — Empiric Treatment Protocols
🫁 Community-Acquired Pneumonia (CAP)
Pediatric CAP
Community-Acquired Pneumonia · Ages 3 Months – 18 Years
KCHC Pediatric Antimicrobial Dosing Guideline · June 2023 · P&T Approved April 2023
!
Exclusions: Sickle Cell Disease · Immunodeficiency · Complicated pneumonia. Adjust to narrowest spectrum once culture data available.
🧮 CAP Dose Calculator
CAP Treatment Reference
SettingAgentDoseMax
Inpatient — No AllergyAmpicillin IV50 mg/kg/dose q6h (200 mg/kg/day)2,000 mg/dose
Inpatient — Not Fully ImmunizedAmpicillin IV or Ceftriaxone IVAmp 50 mg/kg q6h · Ceftriaxone 75 mg/kg q24h2,000 mg
Inpatient — PCN AllergyClindamycin IV13.3 mg/kg/dose q8h (40 mg/kg/day)900 mg/dose
Outpatient — No AllergyAmoxicillin PO (High-dose)45 mg/kg/dose q12h (90 mg/kg/day)2,000 mg/dose
Outpatient — PCN AllergyClindamycin PO13.3 mg/kg/dose q8h (40 mg/kg/day)600 mg/dose
Outpatient — AtypicalAzithromycin PO10 mg/kg Day 1 → 5 mg/kg Days 2–5500 / 250 mg
Severe — S. aureus concernAdd Vancomycin IV15 mg/kg q6h—
Admission Criteria (Any of the Following)
  • SpO₂ <90% sustained · Cyanosis
  • Retractions, grunting (impending respiratory failure)
  • Nasal flaring, head bobbing
  • Dehydration, vomiting, inability to take oral medications
  • Co-morbid conditions · Infants 2–6 months with suspected bacterial CAP
Discharge Criteria
  • No increased work of breathing or tachycardia
  • Decreased fever for 12–24 hours
  • SpO₂ >90% on room air for 12–24 hours
  • Tolerating PO · Follow up with PMD in 2–3 days · Complete 7–10 day antibiotic course
🧫 Skin & Soft Tissue Infection (SSTI)
Pediatric SSTI
Skin & Soft Tissue Infection · >60 Days Old
KCHC Pediatric Antimicrobial Dosing Guideline · June 2023 · P&T Approved April 2023
!
Exclusions: Immunocompromised · Necrotizing fasciitis · Periorbital/orbital · Perianal/perineal/groin · Bone/joint · Animal bite.
🧮 SSTI Dose Calculator
SSTI Algorithm
TypeRouteAgentDose
Non-purulent — Mild (Outpatient)POCephalexin25 mg/kg q12h × 5 days
Non-purulent — Moderate (Inpatient)IVCefazolin33.3 mg/kg/dose q8h (max 2,000 mg)
Not improving on CefazolinIVVancomycin15 mg/kg q6h · Trough 10–15 mcg/mL
Purulent/MRSA — MildPOTMP/SMX6 mg/kg q12h × 5 days min (max 320 mg/dose TMP)
Purulent/MRSA — SevereIVVancomycin15 mg/kg q6h · Trough 10–15 mcg/mL
PCN/Ceph Allergy — GeneralPO/IVClindamycin10 mg/kg/dose q8h (30 mg/kg/day, max 600 mg)
PCN/Ceph Allergy — Severe/MRSA cellulitisPO/IVClindamycinPO: 13.3 mg/kg q8h (max 600 mg) · IV: 13.3 mg/kg q8h (max 900 mg)
🚿 Urinary Tract Infection (UTI)
Pediatric UTI
>60 Days Old · Cystitis + Pyelonephritis
KCHC Pediatric Antimicrobial Dosing Guideline · June 2023 · P&T Approved April 2023
!
Culture before antibiotics. Adjust to narrowest spectrum once susceptibilities available. Exclusions: Immunocompromised · Toxic appearing · Multiple infections.
🧮 UTI Dose Calculator
UTI Treatment Reference
ConditionRouteAgentDoseDuration
Cystitis — No AllergyPOCephalexin50–100 mg/kg/day ÷ BID or TID (max 500 mg/dose)7 days
Cystitis — IVIVCefazolin33.3 mg/kg/dose q8h (100 mg/kg/day, max 2,000 mg/dose)Per culture
Cystitis — PCN AllergyPOCiprofloxacin10–20 mg/kg/dose BID (max 750 mg)7 days
Pyelonephritis <2 yearsPO or IVCephalexin or CefazolinSame as cystitis dosing above10 days
Pyelonephritis >2 yearsPO or IVCephalexin or CefazolinSame as cystitis dosing above7 days
Pediatric Status Epilepticus
Convulsive SE Pathway · All Pediatric Areas Except NICU/Nursery
Kings County Pediatric Convulsive Status Epilepticus Pathway · June 2022
!Inclusion: Continuous seizure >5 min OR intermittent without return to baseline. Exclusion: <2 months · Non-epileptic events. Rapid Response #3144 Code 66 · Peds Neurology 917-219-1267
🧮 SE Weight-Based CalculatorEnter weight → all tier doses calculated
Levetiracetam — Pyxis Weight-Band Table (60 mg/kg)
Weight RangePyxis DoseWeight RangePyxis Dose
4.5 – <8.5 kg250 mg25 – <35 kg1,500 mg
8.5 – <12.5 kg500 mg35 – <50 kg2,000 mg
12.5 – <18 kg750 mg50 – <75 kg3,000 mg
18 – <25 kg1,000 mg>75 kg4,500 mg
INH Ingestion — Add Pyridoxine STAT
  • Known quantity: 1g Pyridoxine per 1g INH ingested — up to 70 mg/kg (max 5,000 mg) over 10 min
  • Unknown quantity: 70 mg/kg Pyridoxine (max 5,000 mg) over 10 min
  • If seizure stops: run remaining Pyridoxine over 4 hours
CT Head — Emergent Indications (Once Stabilized)
  • Suspected neurosurgical emergency
  • Known intracranial lesion OR new focal neurological deficit prior to seizure
  • Recent trauma preceding status epilepticus
  • Concern for brain abscess or need for LP
Levalbuterol Continuous Inhalation
Acute Hyperactive Airway · Approved: PICU · NICU · ICUs · ED
⚠ DRAFT PROTOCOL — NOT P&T OR MEDICAL BOARD APPROVED · Verify with supervising pharmacist before dispensing
KCHC Pharmacy · DRAFT · No Effective Date · No Approval Date
💊 Dose SelectorLevalbuterol = 50% of desired albuterol dose
Preparation Reference
DoseDurationRateTotal VolLevalbuterolNS 0.9%
2.5 mg/hr4 hr12.5 mL/hr50 mL10 mg (4 mL)46 mL
8 hr6.25 mL/hr50 mL20 mg (8 mL)42 mL
5 mg/hr4 hr12.5 mL/hr50 mL20 mg (8 mL)42 mL
8 hr6.25 mL/hr50 mL40 mg (16 mL)34 mL
7.5 mg/hr4 hr12.5 mL/hr50 mL30 mg (12 mL)38 mL
8 hr6.25 mL/hr50 mL60 mg (24 mL)26 mL
10 mg/hr4 hr12.5 mL/hr50 mL40 mg (16 mL)34 mL
8 hr6.25 mL/hr50 mL80 mg (32 mL)18 mL
Adverse Effects · Monitoring
  • Tachycardia (~3%) · Nervousness · Tremors · Dizziness · Nausea
  • ↓ Serum potassium — monitor electrolytes
  • Pharmacist prepares one dose at a time · Label "For Inhalation Use Only" · 12-hour expiration
  • Physician reassesses every 4 or 8 hours
HIV PEP & Expedited Partner Therapy
Post-Exposure Prophylaxis · Non-Occupational · SART Policy
KCHC Pharmacy Policy · Revised: Jun 2025 · EPT Policy: Dec 2020
!
Start PEP within 72 hours of exposure. Duration 28 days. Does NOT require ID approval. Orders through EPIC. Available from Omnicell ED or Inpatient Pharmacy.
PopulationDolutegravirPartner Agent
≥12 yrs · >35 kg · CrCl >50Tivicay® 50 mg QDTruvada® (FTC 200/TDF 300) 1 tab daily
≥12 yrs · >35 kg · CrCl 15–50Tivicay® 50 mg QDVemlidy® (TAF) 25 mg QD + Lamivudine 150 mg QD
≥12 yrs · >35 kg · On HDTivicay® 50 mg QDVemlidy® 25 mg QD (after HD) + Lamivudine 150 mg QD
17–22 kg (Peds SART)Tivicay PD® 25 mg QDTruvada® FTC 100/TDF 150 daily
22–28 kg (Peds SART)Tivicay PD® 30 mg QDTruvada® FTC 133/TDF 200 daily
28–35 kg (Peds SART)Tivicay PD® 30 mg QDTruvada® FTC 167/TDF 250 daily
4 wks–<2 yrs (unable to swallow)Tivicay PD® per weight bandZidovudine syrup + Lamivudine oral 4 mg/kg/dose BID
Expedited Partner Therapy (EPT)
IndicationAgentDoseRoute
Chlamydia EPTAzithromycin1g oral sachet — single dosePO
Gonorrhea (co-treatment)Ceftriaxone500 mg IM × 1 (or 1g if >150 kg)IM
Furosemide Continuous Infusion
Pediatric / Neonatal · Weight-Based Dosing
Source: KCHC Pharmacy Staff Meeting · March 31, 2026 · Franklin Akinkunmi R.Ph. MBA, Assistant Director Pharmacy Services
⚠ Medication Error Alert: A provider intended to order furosemide 1 mg/kg IV push (one-time) but instead ordered a continuous infusion at 1 mg/kg/hr × 24 hrs — 10× the maximum CI dose. Always verify route and frequency. Usual CI max is 0.4 mg/kg/hr.
💧 Furosemide CI Dose CalculatorPediatric/Neonatal · Weight-based
Dosing Reference
ParameterValue
Starting dose0.1 to 0.2 mg/kg/hour
TitrationIncrease by 0.1 mg/kg/hour increments every 12 to 24 hours based on urine output
Maximum dose0.4 mg/kg/hour
Product (EPIC)Furosemide Infusion 1 mg/mL (Neonate/Pediatric) · Sodium chloride 0.9% 22.5 mL
MonitoringUrine output · Electrolytes (K⁺, Mg²⁺, Na⁺) · BUN/Cr · Blood pressure · Daily weight
IV Push (one-time) — do NOT confuse with CI1 mg/kg one-time — this was the clinically appropriate intended dose in the error case. The error occurred when this was entered as 1 mg/kg/hr continuous × 24 hrs, making it a 10-fold overdose vs. the CI maximum of 0.4 mg/kg/hr.
KCHC Pharmacy Staff Meeting · March 31, 2026 · Peds Clinical Pharmacist presentation
💡 Key distinction: IV Push = one-time dose in mg/kg. Continuous Infusion = ongoing rate in mg/kg/hr. These are easily confused in EPIC — always verify the frequency field says "Continuous" and the dose has "/hr" units before verifying.
KCHC Pharmacy Staff Meeting · March 31, 2026 · Franklin Akinkunmi R.Ph. MBA, Assistant Director Pharmacy Services · cc: Zoraya Pod, DOP
Oncology Agent Selector
Select Cancer Type → Regimen + Pharmacy Flags · OR select drug below for lookup
🏥 Clinical Content — KCHC Life Infusions
Sarah Kamal, PharmD · KCHC Life Infusions Center · Internal Clinical Notes
Verified against: ASCO 2020 Antiemetic Guidelines · ASCO HER2+ Breast Cancer Guideline · NCCN 2024–2025 (via ASCO Post) · FDA Prescribing Information · Oncology.docx · Chemo Thought Process.docx
⚠ No KCHC P&T approval date on source documents. Verify regimen details with oncology pharmacist before clinical use.
🎯 Step 1 — Patient Profile + Cancer TypeApp adjusts regimen based on patient factors
🔍 Step 2 — Drug Lookup (Optional)Select agent for flags · filters · cardiac · protocol routing
AgentECHOFilterLight SensitiveKey Flag
Doxorubicin✓ Baseline✓Cumulative dose limit 450–550 mg/m² · Vesicant
Trastuzumab✓ Q3 monthsHold if LVEF ≥16% decrease or below normal + ≥10% decrease
Pertuzumab✓ Q3 months✓ RequiredHER2+ confirmation required
Pembrolizumab✓ 0.22µ filterTSH + free T4 baseline · PD-1 inhibitor
Vincristine✓ Required2 mg single dose cap · FATAL if intrathecal
5-FU✓DPYD testing recommended before use (NCCN 2024)
CarboplatinCalvert equation (AUC-based) · CrCl cap 125 · Min SCr 0.7
Rituximab✓ RequiredHepB surface Ag + core Ab before first dose
PegfilgrastimGive 24h after last chemo · NOT within 14 days before next cycle
Order Verification Tool
Interactive Pre-Dispensing Checklist · Regimen-Specific · Go / Hold / Flag
Sarah Kamal, PharmD · KCHC Life Infusions · Internal Clinical Notes · Verified: ASCO 2020 · NCCN 2024–2025 · FDA PI · No KCHC P&T date — verify with oncology pharmacist
🧮 CrCl Quick Calc (Cockcroft-Gault) ▼
CrCl = [(140-Age) × Weight / (72 × SCr)] × 0.85 if female · Source: Cockcroft-Gault
!
Complete every section before verifying. Two independent pharmacist calculations required for all chemo at KCHC (JC MM Standard). If any item is flagged, escalate before proceeding.
Select Regimen Being VerifiedChecklist adapts to selected regimen
📅 1. Timing & Scheduling
🧪 2. Labs — Must Be Current (within 7 days)
💊 3. Dose Verification
💉 4. Supportive Medications
⚠️ 5. Drug-Specific Safety
Carboplatin Dosing
Calvert Equation — AUC-Based Dosing
Sarah Kamal, PharmD · KCHC Life Infusions · Internal Clinical Notes · Verified: ASCO 2020 · NCCN 2024–2025 · FDA PI · No KCHC P&T date — verify with oncology pharmacist
🧮 Carboplatin Calvert CalculatorDose (mg) = AUC × (CrCl + 25)
Age
Weight (kg)
SCr (mg/dL)
Sex
Will be capped at 125 mL/min if higher (NCI 2010)
Calvert Formula Reference
ParameterValue / RuleSource
FormulaTotal Dose (mg) = AUC × (GFR + 25)Calvert et al, JCO 1989
GFR estimateCockcroft-Gault equation (most common US practice)NCI / Clinical practice
CrCl capDo not exceed 125 mL/min (prevents erroneous overdose due to CG overestimation)NCI action letter, Oct 2010 ✅ Verified
Minimum SCr0.7 mg/dL (clinical practice — prevents GFR overestimation in low muscle mass)Common clinical practice ⚠️ Not in FDA label
AUC 2 (weekly)Used in weekly carboplatin regimensRegimen-specific ⚠️ Unverified source
AUC 5–7 (monthly)Standard for untreated patients per ASCO/NCCNPublished literature ✅ Verified
Obese patientsUse adjusted body weight in Cockcroft-Gault — actual weight overestimates GFRPublished literature ✅ Verified
UnitsDose is in mg — NOT mg/m²FDA prescribing information ✅ Verified
⚠️ Carboplatin Safety Notes
  • Do not use aluminum needles or IV sets — aluminum reacts with carboplatin causing precipitate and loss of potency
  • Light sensitive — protect from light during administration
  • Infuse over 15 minutes or longer
  • Two independent pharmacist calculations required at KCHC for all chemo orders
Critical Dose Limits
Caps · Pump Rates · Special Protocols
Sarah Kamal, PharmD · KCHC Life Infusions · Internal Clinical Notes · Verified: ASCO 2020 · NCCN 2024–2025 · FDA PI · No KCHC P&T date — verify with oncology pharmacist
🧮 CrCl Quick Calc (Cockcroft-Gault) ▼
CrCl = [(140-Age) × Weight / (72 × SCr)] × 0.85 if female · Source: Cockcroft-Gault
Cisplatin Max 100 mg/m² per cycle
✅ NCCN
Reason: Nephrotoxicity — cisplatin accumulates in renal tubules; exceeding 100 mg/m² per cycle significantly increases irreversible renal damage risk.
Verification: ✅ Published clinical practice / NCCN — standard across regimens (cisplatin-based lung, head & neck, cervical, testicular).
Also check: CrCl <30 — avoid cisplatin; use carboplatin instead.
Vincristine — Dose Cap Single dose capped at 2 mg
✅ ASCO
Reason: Peripheral neuropathy — dose-limiting neurotoxicity. Cap applies in CHOP, CVP, DA-EPOCH-R regimens.
Verification: ✅ ASCO guideline confirmed — regimen-specific, not universal. Some regimens (e.g., ALL) may use uncapped doses — verify with oncologist.
Key flag: Confirm dose ≤2 mg before dispensing in any lymphoma regimen.
Vincristine — Administration ⛔ MUST be in 50 mL minibag — NEVER syringe
✅ FDA 2020
Reason: Fatal if given intrathecally — dispensing in a minibag prevents IT administration error (syringe could be confused with intrathecal syringe).
Verification: ✅ NCCN “Just Bag It” campaign · FDA labeling update 2020 · ISMP high-alert medication.
Label must read: “FATAL IF GIVEN INTRATHECALLY — FOR INTRAVENOUS USE ONLY”
Mitomycin Max dose 20 mg
⚠️ Verify
Reason: Cumulative toxicity — myelosuppression and hemolytic uremic syndrome at higher doses.
Verification: ⚠️ Unverified in KCHC source documents — consistent with published ranges. Confirm with oncology pharmacist before applying.
Note: Light sensitive — protect from light during preparation and administration.
Potassium Chloride (IV) 10 mEq/100 mL peripheral
✅ KCHC
Reason: Cardiac toxicity — rapid IV potassium infusion can cause fatal arrhythmia.
Limits:
• Peripheral line: max 10 mEq per 100 mL
• Central line: max 20 mEq IV
• Oral: max 40 mEq PO
• If >10 mEq needed IV — use 2 separate orders of 10 mEq, or give in 1 liter, or switch to PO.
Verification: ✅ Confirmed — KCHC Med Safety policy.
!
Do NOT change the rate — change the volume and hours. 5-FU pumps run at fixed rates only. Source: Sarah Kamal PharmD / KCHC Life Infusions.
Select duration
5-FU Pump Parameters
⛔ Rate—
Volume—
Duration—
Notes—
RuleDo NOT change the rate — adjust volume and hours only
Which infusion?
Bevacizumab Infusion Rate
⏱ Infuse Over—
Condition—
Bevacizumab Monitoring
  • Monitor blood pressure — bevacizumab can increase BP
  • Check urine protein — hold if >2g in 24-hour urine
  • Mvasi (biosimilar): recommend baseline ECHO to MD
Cardiac Monitoring / ECHO
LVEF Assessment · Hold Criteria · QT Prolongation
Sarah Kamal, PharmD · KCHC Life Infusions · Internal Clinical Notes · Verified: ASCO 2020 · NCCN 2024–2025 · FDA PI · No KCHC P&T date — verify with oncology pharmacist
!Source conflict: Sarah Kamal’s notes state hold if EF <40% or <45% with >10% drop. FDA PI states hold for ≥16% absolute decrease OR below institutional normal + ≥10% decrease. Use FDA label language — confirm with oncology team.
Select agent to see ECHO requirements
—
Baseline ECHO—
Monitoring Schedule—
Hold Criteria—
Source—
⚡ Electrolyte Check Required Before Administration

Ensure K, Mg, Ca are within normal limits before giving any of these agents. Consider holding therapy if QTc >500 ms. Source: Sarah Kamal PharmD / KCHC Life Infusions / Oncology.docx

Fluorouracil (5-FU) ⚠️ Moderate — check K/Mg/Ca
Doxorubicin ⚠️ Moderate
HER2 Inhibitors (trastuzumab, pertuzumab) ✓ Low — monitor in combination
Action threshold: QTc >500 ms — consider holding therapy and notifying prescriber. Ensure K+ and Mg2+ are repleted before each cycle.
PD-1 / PD-L1 Inhibitor Monitoring
Thyroid · Baseline Labs · Special Requirements
Sarah Kamal, PharmD · KCHC Life Infusions · Internal Clinical Notes · Verified: ASCO 2020 · NCCN 2024–2025 · FDA PI · No KCHC P&T date — verify with oncology pharmacist
Select agent
—
Class—
Baseline Labs Required—
Thyroid Monitoring—
Special Requirements—
🧪 Thyroid Management Protocol — All PD-1/PD-L1 Inhibitors
✓ Obtain baseline TSH + Free T4 before starting any PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitor
If TSH >4.5 mIU/L → obtain serum Free T4
Low Free T4 = clinical hypothyroidism → treat with levothyroxine to normalize TSH
Thyroid replacement indicated if TSH >10 or low T4
MD usually does not hold chemo for thyroid levels — prescriber must be notified
✓ Consistent with FDA labeling for all four PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors
Rituximab & Biologic Safety
HepB Screening · Prophylaxis · Infliximab · Soliris / Ultomiris REMS
Sarah Kamal, PharmD · KCHC Life Infusions · Internal Clinical Notes · Verified: ASCO 2020 · NCCN 2024–2025 · FDA PI · No KCHC P&T date — verify with oncology pharmacist
!HBV reactivation can be severe and fatal. Screening required before first dose. ✓ Confirmed per FDA label and ASCO/IDSA guidelines.
HepB Surface Antigen Tap to expand
Negative = no active infection · Positive = active HBV infection
If positive: consult ID/hepatology before proceeding with rituximab.
HepB Core Antibody ⚠️ Tap to expand
Reactive = recovered from past infection (immune) · Core + Surface positive = ask MD for ppx
If core antibody reactive: prescribe Entecavir or Tenofovir daily until 6–12 months post-rituximab treatment.
Core negative + Surface negative = no prior infection — consider vaccination if time allows.
Rituximab — Inpatient Administration
  • Order set AND non-formulary sheet required for inpatient
  • Premedicate: Tylenol + Benadryl — enter as separate inpatient orders
  • Filter required during administration
  • R-CHOP: Rituximab (Truxima), Cyclophosphamide, Doxorubicin, Vincristine, Prednisone, Neulasta Onpro
  • Prednisone 100 mg × 5 days — ensure entered for inpatient
Infliximab (Remicade) — Pre-Treatment Requirements
HepB and HepC screening required before first dose
TB Quantiferon test required
Filter required during administration
If >1,000 mg: prepare in 500 mL NS
No order set needed for inpatient — add separate orders for Tylenol and Benadryl
Soliris & Ultomiris — REMS Requirements
REMS enrollment required before dispensing — verify at ultsolrems.com
Screenshot confirming patient received meningitis vaccine — add to iVent
Bexsero 2-dose series: 0 and 6 months · If 2nd dose <6 months after 1st, a 3rd dose required at least 4 months after 2nd
3-dose option: 0, 1–2, and 6 months (for high-risk patients ≥10 years)
ACIP: 2-dose series (0 and 6 months) for healthy adolescents 16–23 years via shared clinical decision-making
Bone Modifying Agents
Zometa · Xgeva · Prolia · Reclast · Calcium Monitoring
Sarah Kamal, PharmD · KCHC Life Infusions · Internal Clinical Notes · Verified: ASCO 2020 · NCCN 2024–2025 · FDA PI · No KCHC P&T date — verify with oncology pharmacist
🧮 CrCl Quick Calc (Cockcroft-Gault) ▼
CrCl = [(140-Age) × Weight / (72 × SCr)] × 0.85 if female · Source: Cockcroft-Gault
Select agent
—
Indication—
Dose—
Frequency—
Key Safety—
⚠️ Do NOT Confuse With—
⚠️ Calcium Monitoring — All Bone Modifying Agents
Both Zometa and Xgeva decrease calcium — check Ca before each dose
8.6 mg/dL is borderline low — consult MD before proceeding
Be concerned if calcium >12–14 mg/dL (hypercalcemia of malignancy)
Dental clearance required before initiating Zometa, Xgeva, or Prolia — ONJ risk
G-CSF — Neulasta / Zarxio Reference
  • Pegfilgrastim (Neulasta): 6 mg SC once per cycle · Give 24 hours after last chemo dose · Do NOT give within 14 days before next cycle ✓ FDA confirmed
  • Neulasta Onpro: Apply day of chemo — pump activates 27 hours later · Received day pump is removed for FOLFOX
  • Filgrastim (Zarxio): 5 mcg/kg/day IV/SC · Give when ANC <2 · Can give day of pump removal · Round to nearest 300 mcg or 480 mcg vial
  • Neulasta Onpro eligibility: Do NOT recommend if: age ≤39 · HIV · bone marrow disease · significant comorbidities · history of neutropenic complications · Source: Sarah Kamal PharmD / KCHC Life Infusions
Light Sensitive & Filtered Medications
Administration Requirements
Sarah Kamal, PharmD · KCHC Life Infusions · Internal Clinical Notes · Verified: ASCO 2020 · NCCN 2024–2025 · FDA PI · No KCHC P&T date — verify with oncology pharmacist
!
Protect from light during storage and administration. Use amber bags/tubing or foil wrapping. Do not expose to direct sunlight or fluorescent light for extended periods.
DOXOrubicin HIGH
Protect entire bag and tubing · Also in EPOCH regimen · Light-sensitive bag required for every dose.
EPOCH (regimen) HIGH
Contains doxorubicin — protect entire multi-drug bag from light.
Dacarbazine HIGH
Degrades rapidly in light — wrap bag and tubing immediately. Also check: if solution turns pink it is degraded — do NOT use.
Irinotecan MODERATE
Protect from light during storage and infusion.
Fluorouracil (5-FU) MODERATE
Protect pump bag during administration.
Enhertu MODERATE
Protect from light per prescribing information.
Trodelvy MODERATE
Store protected from light.
Methotrexate MODERATE
Always YELLOW — inspect color before dispensing. If not yellow, do not use.
Intrathecal: stability 4–6 hours at room temperature · No heparin with intrathecal methotrexate.
Mitomycin (IV / Gel Instillation) MODERATE
Protect from light · Max dose 20 mg.
Carmustine MODERATE
Contains alcohol diluent · Protect from light.
!
These medications require an in-line filter during administration. Verify filter pore size against prescribing information for each agent.
Ado-Trastuzumab (Kadcyla) ✓ FILTER
In-line filter required · Do NOT confuse with trastuzumab (Herceptin) or Enhertu — three different agents.
Abatacept (Orencia) ✓ FILTER
In-line filter required · Silicone-free syringe required for reconstitution.
Cabazitaxel (Jevtana) ✓ FILTER
In-line filter required · Two-step dilution required · Non-PVC containers.
Cetuximab (Erbitux) ✓ FILTER
In-line filter required · Do not shake · Premedicate · EGFR testing required · Infuse over 120 min (2 hours).
Durvalumab (Imfinzi) ✓ FILTER
In-line filter required · Also light sensitive · PD-L1 inhibitor — TSH + Free T4 baseline required.
Etoposide (Vepesid) ✓ FILTER
In-line filter required · Low concentration required to prevent precipitation · Non-PVC tubing.
Infliximab (Remicade) ✓ FILTER
In-line filter required · HepB/C + TB Quantiferon check first · If >1,000 mg: prepare in 500 mL NS.
Nivolumab (Opdivo) ✓ FILTER
In-line filter required · PD-1 inhibitor · TSH + Free T4 baseline required before treatment.
Ocrelizumab (Ocrevus) ✓ FILTER
In-line filter required · Premedicate · Infusion reaction risk · MS indication.
Paclitaxel (Taxol) ✓ 0.22µm
0.22-micron filter required · Non-PVC tubing and containers · Premedicate: Dexamethasone + Diphenhydramine + H2 blocker.
Pembrolizumab (Keytruda) ✓ FILTER
In-line filter required · PD-1 inhibitor · TSH + Free T4 baseline required before treatment.
Diabetes Agent Selector
Patient Profile → Recommendations → Protocol Routing
Shireen Farzadeh PharmD BCPS BCGP · NYC H+H/Kings County · August 2023 (KCHC source) · Updated references: ADA 2025 · AACE 2022 · AHA/ACC 2025 · Beers Criteria 2019 · NYCH+H Order Sets

What's in This Tab
PaneContent
🎯 Glycemic TargetsSetting selector → target range (standard, ICU, EN/PN, perioperative, elderly, terminally ill)

Glycemic Targets
Setting-Specific BG Goals — Inpatient & Outpatient
Shireen Farzadeh PharmD BCPS BCGP · NYC H+H/Kings County · August 2023 (KCHC source) · Updated references: ADA 2025 · AACE 2022 · AHA/ACC 2025 · Beers Criteria 2019 · NYCH+H Order Sets
🎯 Select Patient SettingDisplays recommended target range
All Glycemic Targets Reference (AACE 2022)
Setting / PopulationTarget BG / A1cNotes
Standard Inpatient (non-critically ill)140–180 mg/dLOnce insulin started. Level A evidence.
Selected patients — if achievable without hypoglycemia110–140 mg/dLLevel C evidence. Not universal.
Severe comorbidities180–250 mg/dLAcceptable when tighter control not feasible
Terminally ill / short life expectancy>250 mg/dL acceptableComfort-focused. Avoid hypoglycemia.
Enteral / Parenteral Nutrition<140 mg/dLCaution — increases hypoglycemia risk substantially
Perioperative80–180 mg/dLTight targets not advised — associated with more hypoglycemia
Critically Ill (ICU)140–180 mg/dLInsulin infusion preferred. Transition to SQ when clinically stable.
Older Adults (non-frail)A1c <7.5%ADA recommendation
Older Adults (frail / multiple comorbidities)A1c 8–8.5%Avoid hypoglycemia. Deprescribing may be appropriate.
Hypoglycemia Classifications (ADA)
LevelBG ThresholdAction
Level 1<70 mg/dLModify regimen — reassess insulin/agent doses
Level 2<54 mg/dLClinically significant — requires immediate treatment
Level 3Any BG with altered mental status or seizureSevere — requires third party assistance
Inpatient Insulin Dosing
TDD Calculator · Basal / Prandial / Correctional · IV→SQ Transition
Shireen Farzadeh PharmD BCPS BCGP · NYC H+H/Kings County · August 2023 (KCHC source) · Updated references: ADA 2025 · AACE 2022 · AHA/ACC 2025 · Beers Criteria 2019 · NYCH+H Order Sets
!
Before verifying insulin orders: check home dose, previously received doses, and glucose tab in EPIC. Avoid sliding scale alone for >24 hours.
🧮 Insulin Dose CalculatorEstimates based on weight and A1c
Affects TDD rate: <10% → 0.4 u/kg · ≥10% → 0.5 u/kg · ≥12% → 0.6 u/kg
Inpatient Insulin Protocol Reference
SituationRegimenNotes
Good oral intakeBasal + Prandial + CorrectionalStandard inpatient regimen
Poor oral intake / NPOBasal + Correctional onlyHold prandial. Add correctional: regular q6h OR rapid-acting q4h
Sliding scale onlyStrongly discouraged alone >24hAACE Level A — not adequate as sole therapy
Initiation thresholdBG ≥180 mg/dL on 2 occasionsStart insulin when persistent hyperglycemia confirmed
BG <100 mg/dLReassess regimenMay need dose reduction
BG <70 mg/dLModify regimen immediatelyTreat hypoglycemia per protocol
IV Insulin → SQ Transition (Critically Ill)
  • Give SQ basal insulin 2 hours before stopping IV insulin infusion
  • Base SQ dose on insulin infusion rate during the last 6 hours when stable glycemic goals were achieved
  • Continue BG monitoring after transition — adjust as needed
  • Transition when patient is clinically stable and tolerating oral intake
Enteral / Parenteral Nutrition — Correctional Insulin
  • Administer correctional insulin SQ every 6h using human regular insulin OR every 4h using rapid-acting insulin
  • If enteral nutrition is interrupted: start 10% dextrose infusion immediately to prevent hypoglycemia while adjusting insulin
  • Target BG: <140 mg/dL (caution — increases hypoglycemia risk)
Oral Antihyperglycemic Agents — Inpatient
Keep or Hold · Renal Dosing · Surgery Rules
Shireen Farzadeh PharmD BCPS BCGP · NYC H+H/Kings County · August 2023 (KCHC source) · Updated references: ADA 2025 · AACE 2022 · AHA/ACC 2025 · Beers Criteria 2019 · NYCH+H Order Sets
!
No universal KCHC policy on holding oral medications. Use clinical judgment. Most should be held inpatient with exceptions below. Endocrinology consult may recommend continued use.
Agent / ClassInpatient StatusCondition / Notes
Sitagliptin (DPP-4i)✓ May continueRequires renal dose adjustment. Safe inpatient option.
Empagliflozin (SGLT-2i)✓ May continueHold if eGFR <20. Hold 3–4 days before surgery.
Metformin✓ May continueHold if acute kidney injury, contrast planned, or surgery day. Hold if eGFR <30 (relative).
Sulfonylureas (glipizide, glimepiride)⚠ Generally holdHypoglycemia risk inpatient. Avoid glyburide especially in elderly (Beers Criteria).
Glyburide✗ Avoid — especially elderlyProlonged hypoglycemia risk. Beers Criteria: avoid in older adults.
TZDs (pioglitazone)✗ Generally holdFluid retention risk. Not suitable acutely.
GLP-1 Agonists (semaglutide, liraglutide)⚠ Generally holdGI effects complicate inpatient management. Preferred over insulin outpatient.
All SGLT-2i✗ Hold 3–4 days pre-surgeryDKA risk perioperatively. Also hold if pending procedure.
Key Take-Home Points (Shireen Farzadeh PharmD)
Recommended Inpatient Practice
  • Hold most antihyperglycemics except: Sitagliptin (renal adjust), Empagliflozin (if eGFR ≥20), Metformin (if no AKI) — or if endocrinology recommends continuation
  • Encourage providers to use diabetes order sets in EPIC
  • Prior to verifying insulin orders: check home dose, previously received doses, glucose tab in EPIC
  • Medication errors secondary to oral agents and insulin are common but preventable
  • Consider resuming oral agents 1–2 days prior to discharge
Sulfonylurea Preference — Elderly (Beers Criteria 2019)
  • Avoid glyburide in older adults — prolonged hypoglycemia risk (long duration of action)
  • Prefer glipizide or glimepiride — shorter duration of action if sulfonylurea is necessary
  • Source: AGS Beers Criteria 2019 (cited in Farzadeh PharmD presentation)
Perioperative Insulin Management
Insulin Adjustment by Surgical Timing
Shireen Farzadeh PharmD BCPS BCGP · NYC H+H/Kings County · August 2023 (KCHC source) · Updated references: ADA 2025 · AACE 2022 · AHA/ACC 2025 · Beers Criteria 2019 · NYCH+H Order Sets
🔪 Perioperative Insulin CalculatorSelect timing + insulin type → adjustment
Perioperative Protocol Reference
TimingAction
Evening before surgeryDecrease insulin by 25% · If NPO: decrease by 50%
Morning of surgery — NPHGive 50% of NPH dose
Morning of surgery — Long-actingGive 75–80% of long-acting basal dose
Morning of surgery — Oral agentsHold ALL oral antihyperglycemics · Hold prandial insulin
MetforminHold on day of surgery
SGLT-2 inhibitorsHold 3–4 days before surgery (DKA risk — not just morning of)
NPODecrease long-acting insulin by 50% · Hold all prandial · Continue correctional
Target BG perioperative80–180 mg/dL · Tight targets associated with more hypoglycemia — not advised
T2DM Pharmacologic Approach
ADA 2022 Algorithm · Comorbidity-Based Selection
Shireen Farzadeh PharmD BCPS BCGP · NYC H+H/Kings County · August 2023 (KCHC source) · Updated references: ADA 2025 · AACE 2022 · AHA/ACC 2025 · Beers Criteria 2019 · NYCH+H Order Sets
Comorbidity / SituationPreferred Agent(s)Notes
ASCVD / CVD / High CVD RiskGLP-1 agonist AND/OR SGLT-2iRegardless of glycemic need. With or without metformin.
Heart Failure (HF)SGLT-2i preferredProven CV benefit. GLP-1 agonist also acceptable.
CKDSGLT-2i AND/OR GLP-1 agonistRenoprotective effects. Check eGFR thresholds for each agent.
MASLD / MASH
(Fatty liver / metabolic steatohepatitis)		GLP-1 agonist or dual GIP/GLP-1 (tirzepatide)New ADA 2025 — recommended with lifestyle changes to promote weight loss and treat MASLD/MASH. For advanced fibrosis: consider resmetirom (thyroid hormone receptor-β agonist) + GI/hepatology referral.
No compelling comorbidityMetformin + lifestyleFirst-line. Add agents based on patient-centered factors.
A1c >1.5% above targetDual combination therapyConsider two agents to reach target A1c.
A1c >10% or BG ≥300 mg/dLConsider early insulinGLP-1 agonist preferred over insulin when possible.
Elderly patientsAvoid glyburideUse glipizide or glimepiride if SU needed. Beers Criteria 2019.
Drug Class Quick Reference (ADA 2022)
ClassExamplesKey BenefitKey Caution
MetforminMetforminFirst-line, weight neutral, low costHold if AKI, eGFR <30, contrast, surgery
GLP-1 AgonistsSemaglutide, Liraglutide, DulaglutideCV benefit · Weight loss · A1c reductionGI side effects · Not inpatient
SGLT-2 InhibitorsEmpagliflozin, Dapagliflozin, CanagliflozinCV + renal benefit · Weight lossHold pre-op 3–4 days · DKA risk · eGFR threshold
DPP-4 InhibitorsSitagliptin, Saxagliptin, AlogliptinSafe inpatient · Renal adjustHF risk (saxagliptin) · Modest A1c lowering
SulfonylureasGlipizide, Glimepiride, GlyburideLow cost · EffectiveHypoglycemia · Avoid glyburide in elderly
InsulinGlargine, Detemir, Lispro, AspartMost effective · Any A1cHypoglycemia · Weight gain · Requires monitoring
❤️ 2025 AHA/ACC Hypertension Guideline — Key Points
2025 AHA/ACC/AANP/AAPA/ABC/ACCP/ACPM/AGS/AMA/ASPC/NMA/PCNA/SGIM · Published August 2025 · Replaces 2017 guideline
BP CategoryRangeAction
Normal<120/80 mmHgLifestyle counseling
Elevated120–129/<80 mmHgLifestyle modification
Stage 1 HTN130–139/80–89 mmHgLifestyle ± meds (based on CVD risk via PREVENT calculator)
Stage 2 HTN≥140/90 mmHgLifestyle + medication (regardless of risk)
  • BP goal (most patients): <130/80 mmHg · Aspirational target: <120/80 mmHg (Class IIa — supported by SPRINT, ESPRIT, BPROAD trials)
  • Diabetes + HTN: Initiate at SBP ≥130 mmHg · Goal <130/80 mmHg · Encourage <120 mmHg if tolerated (BPROAD trial — 13,000 T2DM patients)
  • Risk calculator UPDATED: PREVENT equations replace Pooled Cohort Equations. 10-yr CVD risk ≥7.5% = increased risk threshold for treatment decisions
  • First-line agents (unchanged): ACEi or ARB · Thiazide-like diuretic (chlorthalidone or indapamide preferred) · Dihydropyridine CCB · Often need all 3 classes to reach goal
  • GLP-1 RAs: Recognized as BP-lowering adjuncts in patients with obesity or metabolic syndrome
  • Resistant HTN: Uncontrolled on ≥3 agents (including a diuretic) at optimal doses
  • Cuffless BP devices: Class III — NOT recommended for HTN diagnosis or management
  • Dementia: Lowering BP reduces dementia risk by ~15% (new evidence cited)
Cardiovascular Risk Management
  • Hypertension (ADA 2025 + 2025 AHA/ACC): Initiate pharmacotherapy at SBP ≥130 mmHg · Goal: <130/80 mmHg · Aspirational target: <120 mmHg if tolerated (Class IIa — BPROAD trial 2025). ACEi or ARB for patients with CAD or urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio ≥300 mg/g. Thiazide-like diuretics (chlorthalidone, indapamide) preferred for CV outcomes. Dihydropyridine CCB as add-on.
  • Statins — Secondary prevention (ASCVD): High-intensity statin preferred (ACS, MI, stroke, PAD, revascularization)
  • Statins — Primary prevention (2025 AHA/ACC): Use PREVENT risk calculator (replaces Pooled Cohort Equations): professional.heart.org → PREVENT Calculator — 10-yr CVD risk ≥7.5% = increased short-term risk · Ages 30–79 · No race variable · Includes eGFR, BMI, HbA1c, HF risk
  • Aspirin for primary prevention: USPSTF April 2022 — routine aspirin for primary CVD prevention no longer recommended for most adults
  • GLP-1 RAs + BP: GLP-1 receptor agonists recognized as BP-lowering adjuncts in patients with obesity or metabolic syndrome (2025 AHA/ACC)
📋 ADA 2025 Key Updates — What Changed
Source: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes 2025 · January 2025 · Diabetes Care 48(Suppl 1)
Update2022 (App Source)ADA 2025 (New)
CGM UseMainly insulin usersRecommended for ALL T2DM on any glucose-lowering agent — not just insulin
Treatment approachStepwise — add one agent at a timeEarlier combination therapy — parallel use, not sequential
GLP-1 RA indicationsASCVD, HF, CKD+ MASLD/MASH (fatty liver) now added as formal indication
Tirzepatide (dual GIP/GLP-1)Not prominently featuredAdded for perioperative care + MASLD/MASH in appropriate patients
Cannabis warningNot mentionedNEW — Avoid cannabis in patients at DKA risk (hyperemesis syndrome risk)
Presymptomatic T1DMAutoantibody screening mentionedAntibody-based screening for family members / high-risk individuals strengthened. Teplizumab (Tzield) can delay T1DM onset in Stage 2.
SleepNot highlightedSleep elevated as equal pillar to diet and exercise in diabetes management
Medication shortagesNot addressedFirst-ever formal guidance for managing diabetes during medication shortages
BP target in diabetesNot stated in KCHC sourceGoal <130/80 mmHg · Aspirational: <120 mmHg if tolerated (ADA 2025 + 2025 AHA/ACC)
CV risk calculatorPooled Cohort Equations (PCE)PREVENT equations replace PCE (2025 AHA/ACC guideline)
⚠ Core inpatient protocols (insulin dosing, glycemic targets, oral agent hold rules, perioperative adjustments) remain consistent between ADA 2022 and ADA 2025 — no changes required to those sections.
Inpatient Monitoring & EPIC Tips
A1C · Fingerstick Schedule · EPIC Glucose Workflow
Shireen Farzadeh PharmD BCPS BCGP · NYC H+H/Kings County · August 2023 (KCHC source) · Updated references: ADA 2025 · AACE 2022 · AHA/ACC 2025 · Beers Criteria 2019 · NYCH+H Order Sets
📋 Inpatient Diabetes Monitoring Checklist
EPIC Glucose Workflow Tips
View Glucose Trends + Insulin Administrations in One Screen
  • Path 1: Summary → Index → Pharmacy Monitoring → Glucose
  • Path 2: Summary → Glucose
Add Insulin Column to Patient List
  • My List → Edit List → Properties → Insulin → Accept
Other Useful EPIC Navigation
  • View order sets: Encourage prescribers to utilize diabetes order sets
  • View previously administered medications: Medications → Endocrinology
  • View diet: Orders → Active → Diet
  • View fingerstick monitoring: Orders → Active → Lab
Glucocorticoid-Induced Hyperglycemia — Notes
  • Steroids predominantly raise postprandial glucose — prandial coverage often needed
  • NPH insulin dosed with AM steroids is a common strategy for once-daily steroid regimens
  • Monitor BG closely when initiating or tapering corticosteroids
  • Consult endocrinology for complex cases
OB/GYN Agent Selector
Clinical Situation → Protocol Routing
KCHC Hospital Manual · Induction of Labor · Date Effective: May 2017 · Reviewed: Dec 2020
KCHC Hospital Manual · Obstetric Hemorrhage · Date Effective: Sept 2019 · Reviewed: Dec 2020
KCHC OB/GYN & Women's Health · IV Iron L&D · Date Effective: July 2019 · Reviewed: Dec 2020
⚡ Select Clinical SituationRoutes to correct protocol
IV Iron — Iron Deficiency Anemia in Pregnancy
1.Indications: severe anemia 2nd/3rd trimester, intolerant of oral iron, after 30 weeks
2.Choose agent: Venofer (iron sucrose) OR Injectafer (ferric carboxymaltose)
3.Contraindicated in 1st trimester
4.FHR check before infusion. Observe minimum 30 min after. NST after Injectafer.
5.No test dose required for either agent
→ Tap IV Iron L&D tab for full protocol

Bishop Score Calculator
Cervical Favorability Assessment — Modified Bishop Score
KCHC Hospital Manual · Induction of Labor · Date Effective: May 2017 · Reviewed: Dec 2020
📏 Bishop Score — Enter Cervical ExamScore ≥6 = favorable · <6 = ripening needed
Modified Bishop Score Reference Table
ParameterScore 0Score 1Score 2Score 3
Dilation (cm)Closed1–23–4≥5–6
Effacement (%)0–3040–5060–70≥80
Station-3-2-1, 0+1, +2
ConsistencyFirmMediumSoft—
PositionPosteriorMidpositionAnterior—
Score Interpretation
ScoreInterpretationAction
≥8Highly FavorableInduction success rate similar to spontaneous labor. Oxytocin appropriate.
6–7FavorableOxytocin induction appropriate. No cervical ripening required.
4–5UnfavorableCervical ripening recommended. Cook Balloon or Misoprostol.
0–3UnfavorableCervical ripening required before oxytocin.
Induction of Labor Protocol
Indications · Contraindications · Cook Balloon · Misoprostol
KCHC Hospital Manual · Induction of Labor · Date Effective: May 2017 · Reviewed: Dec 2020
!
KCHC does not perform elective inductions. All inductions must have medically-indicated reasons where benefits of delivery outweigh risks of continuing pregnancy.
Indications for Induction of Labor
  • Chorioamnionitis
  • Intrauterine fetal demise
  • Hypertensive disorders of pregnancy (gestational HTN, preeclampsia, superimposed)
  • Postterm pregnancy
  • Premature rupture of membranes
  • Fetal growth restriction
  • Oligohydramnios
  • Other medically indicated conditions as determined by OB
Contraindications to Induction
  • Vasa previa or placenta previa (placental edge <2 cm from internal os)
  • Unfavorable fetal position undeliverable without conversion (breech, transverse)
  • Previous classical cesarean section
  • Active genital herpes infection
  • Previous myomectomy entering the endometrial cavity
  • Umbilical cord prolapse
  • Category III fetal heart rate tracing
  • Hypersensitivity to oxytocin or prostaglandins
  • Hypertonic uterine activity
  • More than 1 prior low segment transverse cesarean section
  • EFW >4,500 g in non-diabetic or >4,000 g in diabetic woman
Mechanical Ripening — Cook Cervical Balloon
Cook Cervical Ripening Balloon Protocol
Uterine Balloon (Red valve — "U")
  • Inflate with 40 mL sterile water in 20 mL increments
  • Pull inflated balloon back against internal os
  • Maximum: 80 mL per balloon
Vaginal Balloon (Green valve — "V")
  • Inflate with 40 mL sterile saline in 20 mL increments
  • Maximum: 80 mL per balloon
Device duration: Maximum 12 hours — remove after 12h regardless of outcome
Remove by deflating both balloons through red and green Check-Flo devices
Medical Ripening — Misoprostol (Prostaglandin)
!Prostaglandins are limited to unfavorable cervix (Bishop <6). Contraindicated in women with prior cesarean delivery or uterine surgery.
RouteInitial DoseSubsequent DosesMaximum
Vaginal25 mcgq3–6hPer clinical judgment
Oral / Sublingual50 mcgq3–6h300 mcg total
Misoprostol Management Rules
  • Continuous FHR and uterine activity monitoring required throughout
  • 2 hours after insertion: bathroom privileges permitted if Category I tracing
  • If no active labor after 6 doses and Bishop <6 → use other cervical ripening method
  • If oxytocin follows misoprostol: minimum 4-hour interval required
  • Tachysystole: >5 contractions in 10-minute period averaged over 30-minute window → stop misoprostol, position patient, notify OB, consider tocolysis
Obstetric Hemorrhage Protocol
Stage 1 / 2 / 3 · Medication Doses · Stat Labs · MTP
KCHC Hospital Manual · Obstetric Hemorrhage · Date Effective: Sept 2019 · Reviewed: Dec 2020
!Hemorrhage defined as: Blood loss >500 mL post vaginal birth OR >1,000 mL post cesarean birth. Activate Acute Blood Loss Protocol immediately.
🩸 Hemorrhage Response Checklist
Select stage to load actions and stat labs. Check items off as completed. Reference tables remain below.
✅ Post-Hemorrhage Management
📋 Stat Labs — Mark as Ordered / Resulted
0 of 11 items completed (0%)
Hemorrhage Medications — Quick Reference
DrugDose / RouteKey CautionTiming
Oxytocin10 units IMGive to all patients post-birthImmediately post-delivery
Tranexamic Acid (TXA)1,000 mg in 100 mL NS IV over 10 minAvoid with thromboembolic disease, cardiac rhythm disease, SAH, renal insufficiencyStage 1 — give if not already given
Methylergonovine (Methergine)0.2 mg IMAvoid with hypertensionSecond-line uterotonic
Carboprost (Hemabate)250 mcg IM q15 min × max 8 dosesAvoid with asthma · Use caution in HTNSecond-line uterotonic
Misoprostol (Cytotec)800–1,000 mcg PR
600 mcg PO
800 mcg SL		Alternative route if other agents unavailableSecond-line uterotonic
Hemorrhage Stages
⚠ Stage 1 Hemorrhage
Vital Signs (Low)Vital Signs (Elevated)
HR 50–60 bpm · SBP 80–90 · DBP 40–49HR 101–110 bpm · SBP 141–150 · DBP 91–100
  • Call Hemorrhage Code → activate Hemorrhage Code Team
  • Activate Massive Transfusion Protocol → first round of blood within 5–10 min
  • Give TXA 1g in 100 mL NS over 10 min (if not already given)
  • Ensure second large-bore IV (16g minimum). Strongly consider 14g or Cordis.
  • Consider moving patient to available OR
  • Start Hemorrhage Checklist with time documentation
  • Draw stat labs: CBC, Fibrinogen, PT/PTT, CMP, Blood gas
🔴 Stage 2 Hemorrhage — Vaginal Deliveries ONLY · EBL <1,500 mL
Vital Signs (Low)Vital Signs (Elevated)
HR <50 bpm · SBP <80 · DBP 40–49HR 151–199 bpm · SBP 141–150 · DBP 101–109
  • Place arterial line
  • Start second round of blood via Belmont rapid transfusion (1:1 or 1:1.5 ratio)
  • Continue quantifying blood loss at 10-minute intervals
  • Consider Bakri balloon if appropriate
  • Give antibiotic prophylaxis
  • Protect airway — consider intubation
  • Surgical management: uterine curettage, placental bed suture, uterine artery ligation, B-Lynch suture, hysterectomy
🚨 Stage 3 Hemorrhage — EBL >1,500 mL
Vital Signs (Low)Vital Signs (Elevated)
HR <50 bpm · DBP <39HR >130 bpm · SBP >200 · DBP >110
  • Start second round of blood via Belmont (1:1 or 1:1.5 ratio)
  • Continue quantifying blood loss at 10-minute intervals
  • Give antibiotic prophylaxis · Protect airway · Consider intubation
  • Surgical management as per Stage 2
  • Continue subsequent MTP rounds — call Blood Bank to stop MTP when hemorrhage stops
  • Obtain Critical Care bed · Notify family · Continue 10-minute team huddles
Cesarean >1,500 mL blood loss: Proceed directly to Stage 3 management — skip Stage 2
Post-Hemorrhage Management
  • Transfer to monitored setting: PACU or ICU
  • Debrief with entire team using H+H Standard Debriefing tool
  • Attending-to-Attending SBAR (OB, Anesthesia, Critical Care) upon ICU transfer
  • Complete all documentation · Submit occurrence report
  • Stat labs: CBC, Fibrinogen, PT/PTT, CMP, blood gas
IV Iron — L&D Protocol
Venofer · Injectafer · Iron Deficiency Anemia in Pregnancy
KCHC OB/GYN & Women's Health · IV Iron L&D · Date Effective: July 2019 · Reviewed: Dec 2020
💉 Select IV Iron AgentDisplays dose, preparation, and schedule
Injectafer (Ferric Carboxymaltose) — Preparation & Administration
Dose
750 mg per dose
Preparation
750 mg in max 100 mL NS over ≥15 min
Schedule
2 doses, at least 7 days apart
Post-infusion
Observe 30 min. Perform NST after administration.
No test dose required · No labs needed day of infusion · FHR check before · Monitor for anaphylaxis · Have Benadryl + CPR equipment available
Source: KCHC OB/GYN - Womens Health · July 2019 · Reviewed Dec 2020
Comparison — Venofer vs Injectafer
ParameterVenofer (Iron Sucrose)Injectafer (Ferric Carboxymaltose)
Dose per infusion200 mg750 mg
PreparationUndiluted slow IV push over 2–5 min OR 200 mg in max 100 mL NS over ≥15 min750 mg in max 100 mL NS over ≥15 min
Number of doses5 doses over 14 days2 doses, ≥7 days apart
NST after?Not requiredRequired after administration
Test dose?Not requiredNot required
Observation30 min post-infusion30 min post-infusion
LocationTriage or L&D roomTriage
Indications — IV Iron in Pregnancy
  • Severe anemia in 2nd or 3rd trimester with symptomatic anemia
  • Insufficient time to replete iron orally (after 30 weeks gestational age)
  • Anatomic abnormalities affecting absorption: history of bariatric surgery, IBD
  • Failure to respond to oral iron therapy
Contraindications
  • Contraindicated in 1st trimester — both agents
  • Known hypersensitivity to Venofer or Injectafer
Administration Checklist — Both Agents
